This query is applicable to Windows. It monitors the registry to check if some options related to the configuration of Windows Defender have been disabled, which could indicate malicious activity. 
- DisableBehaviorMonitoring disables behavior monitoring. 
- DisableOnAccessProtection disables the monitoring of files and program activity. 
- DisableScanOnRealtimeEnable disables the setting that allows the user to configure process scanning when real-time protection is on.
- DisableIOAVProtection enables the scanning of downloaded files and attachments.
- DisableRealtimeMonitoring, as its name suggests, disables real-time protection.
- DisableScriptScanning enables the scanning of scripts by Windows Defender.

osQuery.label: windows_defender_real_time_registry_configuration
osQuery.name: Windows Defender Registry Configuration Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Real-Time Protection"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Real-Time Protection")
AND name IN ("DisableBehaviorMonitoring", "DisableOnAccessProtection", "DisableScanOnRealtimeEnable", "DisableRealtimeMonitoring", "DisableIOAVProtection", "DisableScriptScanning")
AND data = "1";

queries.title: Windows Defender Registry Configuration Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) is used to manage Windows systems both locally and remotely. It is often used by malicious actors for establishing persistence on the host. This query retrieves data on WMI consumers using PowerShell or cmd.

osQuery.label: wmi_suspicious_scripts_monitoring
osQuery.name: WMI Events Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, command_line_template, executable_path, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_cli_event_consumers
WHERE command_line_template LIKE 
regex_match(lower(command_line_template), ".*(powershell|cmd).*", 0);

queries.title: Windows Management Instrumentation Suspicious Scripts Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. PowerShell cmdlet "Get-Process" gets the processes that are running on the local computer. It is often used by adversaries for process discovery or process dumping through PowerShell scripts. Hosts where this cmdlet is detected used in conjunction with process name like "lsass.exe" could indicate potential compromise.

osQuery.label: windows_powershell_events_get_process
osQuery.name: PowerShell Events
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
time, datetime, script_text, script_name, script_path
FROM orbital_powershell_events
WHERE
script_text LIKE "%Get-Process%" AND
script_text LIKE "%lsass%";

queries.title: Windows PowerShell Events Get-Process Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable for Windows, MacOS and Linux. It retrieves information about a host such as:
  * hostname - network hostname including domain
  * local_hostname - local hostname
  * cpu_type - CPU type
  * cpu_subtype - CPU subtype
  * cpu_brand - CPU brand string contains vendor and model
  * physical_memory - total physical memory in bytes

osQuery.label: inventory_system_information
osQuery.name: Inventory System Information

queries.title: Inventory System Information

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieves data on process's open network sockets on the system:
* name - process name
* fd - socket file descriptor number
* socket - socket handle or inode number
* family - network protocol (IPv4, IPv6)
* protocol - transport protocol (TCP/UDP)
* local_address - socket local address
* remote_address - socket remote address
* local_port - socket local port
* remote_port - socket remote port

osQuery.label: process_socket_search
osQuery.name: Process Sockets
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.name, pos.fd, pos.socket, pos.family, 
pos.protocol, pos.local_address, pos.remote_address, 
pos.local_port, pos.remote_port
FROM process_open_sockets pos
LEFT JOIN processes p ON p.pid=pos.pid
WHERE 
p.name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="process_name") 
AND pos.protocol LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="net_protocol")
AND pos.local_address LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="src_ip")
AND pos.local_port LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="src_port")
AND pos.remote_address LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="dest_ip")
AND pos.remote_port LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="dest_port")
AND pos.remote_address NOT IN ("", "0.0.0.0", "127.0.0.1", "::", "::1","0");

queries.title: Process Socket Search
warnings: This query does not include connections on loopback interfaces.
This query does not include listening sockets.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves all Windows Event Logs from the specified event log channel:
* TimeGenerated - time event occurred on the host
* Message - data associated with the event
* EventCode - identifier of the event
* LogFile - event channel: application, security, system, setup, etc.
* SourceName - provider name of the event: Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-General, Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing, etc.
Search suggestions:
* To retrieve all events for the last 24 hours set the parameters "datetime" to "YYYY-MM-DD-.*" using the current date values
* To retrieve specific Windows Event Logs use the parameter "eventid_pattern". For example, setting "eventid_pattern" to "(4624|4625)" will retrieve all successful and failed logon events from the host.

osQuery.label: windows_events_param
osQuery.name: Windows Events
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS TimeGenerated,
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventCode,
channel AS Logfile,
provider_name AS SourceName,
"N/A" AS Type,
"N/A" AS EventIdentifier,
"N/A" AS TimeWritten
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "event_source") AND
eventid LIKE regex_match(eventid, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="eventid_pattern"), 0) AND
provider_name LIKE regex_match(provider_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="provider_name_pattern"), 0) AND
datetime LIKE regex_match(datetime, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Events Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retreives managed policies configuration data from the host:
* domain - system or manager-chosen domain key
* uuid - optional UUID assigned to policy set
* name - policy key name
* value - policy value
* username - policy applies only to this user
* manual - if policy was loaded manually or not
Search suggestion:
* To find all policies that were loaded manually set parameter "loaded_manually" to "True"

osQuery.label: macos_managed_policies_monitoring
osQuery.name: Managed Policies Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
domain, uuid, name, value, username,
CASE manual WHEN "1" THEN "True" ELSE "False" END "Loaded manually"
FROM managed_policies
WHERE
domain LIKE regex_match(domain, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "domain_pattern"), 0) AND
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
value LIKE regex_match(value, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "value_pattern"), 0) AND
manual LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "loaded_manually");

queries.title: Managed Configuration Policies Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. A pass the hash attack involves an attacker using stolen hashed user credentials to authenticate on a target system. This query checks for Windows Event Logs that indicate a potential pass the hash attempt on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_events_pass_the_hash_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
data AS Message,
datetime AS "Time Generated",
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4624 AND ((
data LIKE '%"LogonType":"3"%' AND
data LIKE '%"LogonProcessName":"NtLmSsp%' AND
data LIKE '%"SubjectUserSid":"S-1-0-0%' AND
data LIKE '%"KeyLength":"0"%') OR (
data LIKE '%"LogonType":"9"%' AND
data LIKE '%"LogonProcessName":"seclogo%'));

queries.title: Pass the Hash Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Credentials Guards is a security feature which only allows trusted, privileged applications and processes to access user secrets, or credentials. This query retrieves systems where Credential Guard is active, as well as data on Credential Guard processes.

osQuery.label: windows_cred_guard_status_monitoring
osQuery.name: Credential Guard Process Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT pid, name, path, cmdline, state, cwd, uid, gid, on_disk, threads
FROM processes
WHERE name LIKE "lsaiso.exe"

queries.title: Credential Guard Status Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Shortcuts or symbolic links always reference other files or programs that will be executed when the shortcut is clicked. Malware often uses shortcut files to launch malicious executables on the host. This query retrieves data on shortcut files present on the system.

osQuery.label: windows_shortcut_files_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shortcut Files Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
Win32.Caption, Win32.FileName, Win32.Path, Win32.Target, 
Win32.CreationDate, h.sha256, Win32.Status, Win32.Extension, Win32.Archive, 
Win32.Compressed, Win32.Encrypted, 
WIn32.EncryptionMethod, Win32.Hidden
FROM Win32_ShortcutFile Win32
JOIN hash h ON h.path=Win32.Target
WHERE Win32.Target LIKE regex_match(Win32.Target, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="target_file"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Shortcut Files Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on all services running on the host.

osQuery.label: running_services_search
osQuery.name: Running Services Data

queries.title: Running Services Search by Path
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux and MacOS. It retrieves the disk encryption status and information from the host:
* name - disk name
* uuid - disk universally unique identifier
* encrypted - "Encrypted" or "Not encrypted"
* type - description of cipher type and mode if available 
* username - username of currently authenticated user 
* user_uuid - universally unique identifier of authenticated user
* encryption_status - encrypted, not encrypted, or undefined
Search suggestion:
* This query could be used to list all hosts where file-vault encryption has not been enable on the root file system. Set parameter "encryption_status_pattern" to "Not encrypted". The root file system name generally will be set to "/dev/disk1s1". You can also use mounts table to verify the name, the root file system in the mounts table will have path set to "\\".

osQuery.label: macos_linux_disk_encryption_monitoring
osQuery.name: Disk Encryption Status and Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
de.name, de.uuid, de.type, 
u.username, de.user_uuid, de.encryption_status 
FROM disk_encryption de
LEFT JOIN users u ON de.uid=u.uid
WHERE
encryption_status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "encryption_status_pattern") AND
u.username LIKE regex_match(u.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Disk Encryption Status Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. A PowerShell profile is often used to customize user environments. Malware may modify the existing profiles to include arbitrary commands, functions, modules in order to gain persistence on the host or to obtain privilege escalation. This query retrieves data for existing PowerShell profiles on the system. Any changes to these profiles should be investigated.

osQuery.label: powershell_profiles_monitoring
osQuery.name: PowerShell Profiles File Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.path, f.filename, h.sha256, f.uid, f.gid, f.mode, f.size,
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time,
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified,
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time,
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS creation_time,
f.type
FROM file f LEFT JOIN hash h ON f.path=h.path
WHERE (
f.path LIKE "C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\%%" OR
f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\Documents\%%") AND
f.filename LIKE "%profile.ps1" AND
f.path NOT LIKE "C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\Examples\profile.ps1";

queries.title: PowerShell Profiles Files Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. User Account Control (UAC) is a security feature of Windows which helps prevent unauthorized changes to the operating system. This query retrieves UAC settings from registry.
"ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin" values:
* 0 - allows the Consent Admin to perform an operation that requires elevation without consent or credentials
* 1 - prompts the Consent Admin to enter his or her user name and password (or another valid admin) when an operation requires elevation of privilege. This operation occurs on the secure desktop.
* 2 - prompts the administrator in Admin Approval Mode to select either "Permit" or "Deny" an operation that requires elevation of privilege. If the Consent Admin selects Permit, the operation will continue with the highest available privilege. This operation occurs on the secure desktop.
* 3 (Recommended) - prompts the Consent Admin to enter his or her user name and password (or that of another valid admin) when an operation requires elevation of privilege.
* 4 - prompts the administrator in Admin Approval Mode to select either "Permit" or "Deny" an operation that requires elevation of privilege. If the Consent Admin selects Permit, the operation will continue with the highest available privilege.
* 5 - this is the default. It is used to prompt the administrator in Admin Approval Mode to select either "Permit" or "Deny" for an operation that requires elevation of privilege for any non-Windows binaries. 
"PromptOnSecureDesktop" values:
* 0 - disables secure desktop prompting. All credential or consent prompting will occur on the interactive user's desktop.
* 1 (Recommended) - forces all UAC prompts to happen on the user's secure desktop.  
"EnableLUA" values:
* 0 - disables the "administrator in Admin Approval Mode" user type
* 1 (Recommended) - enables the "administrator in Admin Approval Mode" user type while also enabling all other User Account Control (UAC) policies.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_uac_secure_desktop_admin_password
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
path IN (
"HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin",
"HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\PromptOnSecureDesktop",
"HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\EnableLUA");

queries.title: User Access Control Settings Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The DPAPI (Data Protection Application Programming Interface) Key is used to encrypt user details. Malicious actors may attempt to attack the DPAPI on the Domain Controller to gain further access on the system. This query retrieves Windows Event Logs with Event ID "4662" to search for evidence of such activity on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_events_extracting_backup_key_monitoring
osQuery.name: Extracting Backup Key Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
data AS Message,
datetime AS "Time Generated",
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4662 AND 
data LIKE '%"ObjectType":"SecretObject"%' AND
data LIKE '%"AccessMask":"0x2"%' AND
data LIKE '%"ObjectName":"BCKUPKEY%';

queries.title: Extracting Backup Key Windows Events Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. PsExec is a free Microsoft tool that can be used to execute a program on another computer. It is often used by both IT administrators and attackers. This query retrieves Windows Event Logs related to PsExec activity on the system. Retrieved data should be reviewed for any suspicious entries.

osQuery.label: psexec_windows_event_logs_monitoring
osQuery.name: PsExec Windows Event Logs Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
(channel = "Security" AND data LIKE "%psexesvc%" AND provider_name NOT LIKE "CiscoOrbital") OR
(channel = "Application" AND data LIKE "%psexesvc%" AND provider_name NOT LIKE "CiscoOrbital") OR
(channel = "System" AND data LIKE "%psexesvc%" AND provider_name NOT LIKE "CiscoOrbital");

queries.title: PsExec Windows Event Logs Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Security Events with ID "4648" indicate a logon with explicit credentials. This query retrieves such logons for non system/service accounts where name of the account that requested the new logon session does match the name of the account whose credentials were used. Such events should be investigated for suspicious activity on the host if found:
* datetime - time event was generated
* subject_user_sid - SID of account that requested the new logon session with explicit credentials
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that requested the new logon session
* subject_domain_name - subject’s domain or computer name
* target_user_name - the name of the account whose credentials were used
* target_domain_name - subject’s domain or computer name
* target_server_name - the name of the server on which the new process was run
* process_name - full path and the name of the executable for the process
* ip_address - IP address of machine from which logon attempt was performed
* ip_port - source port which was used for logon attempt from remote machine (0 for interactive logons)
* target_info - additional information indicating protocol used

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_logon_explicit_credentials
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
WITH b1 AS (
SELECT                                                  
datetime,                                               
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_sid,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetServerName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_server_name,
regex_match(data, 'ProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS process_name,
regex_match(data, 'IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip_address,
regex_match(data, 'IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip_port,
regex_match(data, 'TargetInfo":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_info,      
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4648) 
SELECT * FROM b1 
WHERE 
subject_user_sid LIKE regex_match(subject_user_sid, 'S-1-5-[0-9][0-9]-[0-9].*', 0) AND
target_info LIKE "%/%" AND 
subject_user_name!=target_user_name;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Logons with Explicit Credentials

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It retrieves data on kernel modules both loaded and within the load search path: 
* name - module name
* size - size of module content
* used_by - module reverse dependencies
* status - kernel module status 
* address - kernel module address
A Linux Kernel module is designed to extend the functionality of the kernel without the need to reboot the system. Adversaries can use loadable kernel modules to covertly persist on a system and evade defenses.

osQuery.label: linux_kernel_modules_monitoring
osQuery.name: Kernel Modules
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, size, used_by, status, address 
FROM kernel_modules
WHERE
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
used_by LIKE regex_match(used_by, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "used_by_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Kernel Modules Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Linux and MacOS. It retrieves the environment variables for each process running on the host.

osQuery.label: macos_linux_process_envs_monitoring
osQuery.name: Process Environment Variables Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.name AS "Process Name", p.pid, 
pe.key AS "Environment Variable Name", 
pe.value AS "Environment Variable Value"
FROM process_envs pe 
JOIN processes p ON p.pid=pe.pid 
WHERE 
p.name LIKE regex_match(p.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "process_name_pattern"), 0) AND
pe.key LIKE regex_match(pe.key, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "envar_name_pattern"), 0)  AND 
pe.value LIKE regex_match(pe.value, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "envar_value_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Process Environment Variables Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. HTML Help (hh.exe) is a legitimate Microsoft program designed to run compiled HTML (.chm) files. Compiled HTML files are compressed compilations of various content such as HTML documents, images, VBA, JScript, Java and ActiveX scripts. Malware often abuses .chm files by embedding malicious code in scripts that run when chm file is opened by hh.exe. This technique can be used to bypass Windows Device guard and execute arbitrary code. This query retrieves data on hh.exe process running on the system and what hh.exe process executed.

osQuery.label: windows_hh_process_monitoring
osQuery.name: HTML Help Process
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, path, cmdline, state, uid, gid, is_elevated_token
FROM processes
WHERE 
name LIKE "hh.exe";

queries.title: Windows HTML Help Process Monitoring

starting490.label: windows_hh_process_monitoring
starting490.name: HTML Help Process
starting490.sql:
SELECT
name, path, cmdline, state, uid, gid, elevated_token AS is_elevated_token
FROM processes
WHERE
name LIKE "hh.exe";

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Adversaries may search for private key certificate files on compromised systems for insecurely stored credentials. Private cryptographic keys and certificates could be used for authentication, encryption/decryption, and digital signatures. This query retrieves Windows events related to private key access which should be reviewed for suspicious activity.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_private_key_access
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Microsoft-Windows-CAPI2/Operational" AND
eventid = 70;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: Private Key Access Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Windows Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) allows applications and services to be integrated with antimalware software. AMSI is integrated into several Windows 10 components including User Account Control (UAC), PowerShell, Windows Script Host, JavaScript, VBScript, and Office VBA Macros. AMSI can be disabled for scripts on a system by setting the "Windows Script\Settings\AmsiEnable" registry key to 0 and several tools exist to do this before executing malicious scripts.

osQuery.label: windows_amsi_disabled
osQuery.name: Antimalware Scanning Interface (AMSI) Enable Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows Script\Settings"
AND name LIKE "AmsiEnable"
AND data="0";

queries.title: Registry Antimalware Scanning Interface (AMSI) Disabled

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. System Integrity Protection (SIP) is a security technology that is designed to prevent potentially malicious software from modifying protected files and folders. This query retrieves SIP status on the host:
* config_flag - the SIP configuration flag
* enabled - if this configuration enabled
* enabled_nvram - if this configuration enabled in nvram

osQuery.label: macos_sip_config_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Integrity Protection (SIP) Status
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
config_flag, 
CASE enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled " ELSE "Disabled" END "enabled_status",
CASE enabled_nvram WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled " ELSE "Disabled" END "enabled_nvram_status"
FROM sip_config
WHERE
config_flag LIKE regex_match(config_flag, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "config_flag_pattern"), 0) AND
enabled_status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "enabled_pattern") AND
enabled_nvram_status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "enabled_nvram_pattern");

queries.title: System Integrity Protection (SIP )Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. User Access Control (UAC) is a feature in Windows where every application ran under an administrator user account only runs in the context of a standard user. The registry key "FilterAdministratorToken" set to "1" forces the built-in administrator account to use UAC. When an operation requires elevated privileges, the user will be prompted with "Yes" or "No" actions. Malware can disable UAC for local administrator in order to gain persistence on the host or execute malicious code.

osQuery.label: registry_uac_for_admin_account_monitoring
osQuery.name: UAC for Administrator Account Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
CASE data
WHEN 0 THEN "Disabled"
ELSE "Enabled" END "UAC for Built-in Administrator Account",
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS "Last Modified"
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System"
and name LIKE "FilterAdministratorToken";

queries.title: User Access Control For Administrators Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. NetShell (Netsh) is a Windows utility used to interact with the network configuration of a system. It is possible to extend its functionality by adding helper digital link libraries (DLLs) to it. Malware could attempt to add a malicious helper DLL in order to gain persistence on the host.

osQuery.label: netsh_helper_dll_monitoring
osQuery.name: Netsh Registry Key

queries.title: Netsh Helper DLL Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. "CVE-2020-1472" is a privilege elevation vulnerability in the Netlogon Remote Protocol (MS-NRPC). This query checks for hosts that are vulnerable to "CVE-2020-1472" by checking if correct patches were applied on the system. Please note that as some KB's could be superseded in the future - the Microsoft documentation should be checked for additional KB values mitigating the CVE.
The query also retrieves the registry key "FullSecureChannelProtection" value data that could be set to:
* "1" indicating that DCs will deny vulnerable Netlogon secure channel connections unless the account is allowed by the Create Vulnerable Connection list in the "Domain controller: Allow vulnerable Netlogon secure channel connections" group policy.
* "0" indicating that DCs will allow vulnerable Netlogon secure channel connections from non-Windows devices. 
Please note that this option will be deprecated in the enforcement phase release meaning that the key set to "0" does not indicate that the Enforcement Mode is "Disabled." The Enforcement mode will be "Enabled" independently of what value the registry key is set to. Therefore the CVE status is based solely on the correct patches applied and the registry data is provided only as an additional information.

osQuery.label: windows_cve_2020_1472_patches_v2
osQuery.name: CVE-2020-1472 Patches Data
osQuery.sql:
WITH b1 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, name FROM os_version),
b2 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, GROUP_CONCAT(hotfix_id) AS kb_installed FROM patches),
b3 AS (
SELECT 1 AS one, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS enforcement_mode_status_last_modified,
CASE data
WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled"
ELSE "Disabled"
END enforcement_mode_status
FROM registry
WHERE path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Netlogon\Parameters\FullSecureChannelProtection"
)
SELECT b1.name, b2.kb_installed, b3.enforcement_mode_status, b3.enforcement_mode_status_last_modified,
CASE
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%2019%" AND kb_installed LIKE "%KB4565349%" THEN "Patched"
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%2016%" AND kb_installed LIKE "%KB4571694%" THEN "Patched"
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%2012 R2%" AND (kb_installed LIKE "%KB4571703%" OR kb_installed LIKE "%KB4571723%") THEN "Patched"
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%2012%" AND b1.name NOT LIKE "%R2%" AND (kb_installed LIKE "%KB4571736%" OR kb_installed LIKE "%KB4571702%") THEN "Patched"
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%2008%" AND (kb_installed LIKE "%KB4571719%" OR kb_installed LIKE "%KB4571729%") THEN "Patched"
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%1903%" AND kb_installed LIKE "%KB4565351%" THEN "Patched"
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%1909%" AND kb_installed LIKE "%KB4565351%" THEN "Patched"
WHEN b1.name LIKE "%2004%" AND kb_installed LIKE "%KB4566782%" THEN "Patched" 
ELSE "Unpatched"
END "CVE_2020_1472_Status"
FROM b1 JOIN b2 USING(one) JOIN b3 USING(one);

queries.title: CVE-2020-1472 Monitoring 2.0.0

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The "TypedURL" registry key stores 25 recent URLs that are typed in the IE or Windows Explorer address bar. This key shows only links that are fully typed, automatically completed while typing, or links that are selected from the list of stored URLs in IE address bar. Websites that are accessed via IE Favourites are not recorded. This information could be useful during for forensic investigation when data on recently visited sites is required.

osQuery.label: ie_typed_url_search
osQuery.name: Internet Explorer TypedURL Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\TypedURLs"
AND data LIKE regex_match(data, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="typed_url"), 0);

queries.title: Internet Explorer TypedURL Registry Key Search

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It determines whether the default behavior of running .exe file extensions has changed. A change from the default value may be related to malware attempting to execute whenever a .exe file is ran.

osQuery.label: registry_exe_persistenthandler_monitoring
osQuery.name: Executable Files PersistenceHandler Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\.exe\PersistentHandler" OR key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Classes\.exe\PersistentHandler")
AND (data <> "{098f2470-bae0-11cd-b579-08002b30bfeb}");

queries.title: Executable Files PersistentHandler Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. By default, a guest user can view the event log on the system. This may give away important information such as usernames, internal IP addresses, running applications on the system etc. Therefore, anonymous/guest access should be restricted from viewing the system event log. This can be done by setting the registry key "RestrictGuestAccess" to 1. This query retrieves hosts where this registry key has not been set representing a security risk in the environment.

osQuery.label: registry_event_log_restrictions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs Restrictions Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Services\EventLog\Application"
AND name LIKE "RestrictGuestAccess"
AND data!="1";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Restrictions Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It is designed to retrieve data for users accounts on the host:
* uid - user ID
* gid - group ID
* username - user name
* description - optional user description
* directory - user home directory
* shell - users configured default shell
* uuid - user UUID (Darwin) or SID (Windows)
* type - Whether the account is roaming (domain), local or system profile

osQuery.label: user_accounts
osQuery.name: User Accounts
osQuery.sql:
SELECT uid, gid, username, description, directory, shell, uuid, type 
FROM users;

queries.title: User Accounts Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves settings for the Application Layer Firewall (ALF) service on the host:
* Allow signed mode status 
* Firewall unloading status 
* Global mode status
* Logging node status
* Firewall logging option
* Stealth mode status
* ALF version

osQuery.label: macos_alf_monitoring
osQuery.name: Application Layer Firewall Service Details
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
CASE allow_signed_enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" END "allow_signed_status", 
CASE firewall_unload WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" END "firewall_unloading_status", 
CASE global_state WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled with exceptions" WHEN "2" THEN "Configured to block all incoming connections" WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" END "global_state_status", 
CASE logging_enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" END "logging_mode_status", 
logging_option, 
CASE stealth_enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" END "stealth_mode_status", 
version
FROM alf
WHERE
"allow_signed_status" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "allow_signed_mode") AND
"firewall_unloading_status" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "firewall_unloading_mode") AND
"global_state_status" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "global_state_mode") AND
"logging_mode_status" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "logging_mode") AND
logging_option LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "logging") AND
"stealth_mode_status" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "stealth_mode") AND
version LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "alf_version");

queries.title: Application Layer Firewall (ALF) Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs with ID "4662" which indicate that an operation was performed on an Active Directory object:
* datetime - system time at which the event occurred
* subject_user_name - account that performed operation on the object
* subject_domain_name - domain of the account that performed operation on the object
* object_server 
* object_type - type or class of the object that was accessed
* object_name - distinguished name of the object that was accessed
* operation_type - the type of operation which was performed on an object
* data - event data

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_ad_object_access_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectServer":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_server,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectType":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_type,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_name,
regex_match(data, 'OperationType":"([^"]+)', 1) AS operation_type,
data
FROM
windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel="Security" AND
eventid = 4662;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Operation on Active Directory Object was Performed

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Server Message Block (SMB) is a network communicating protocol providing shared access to files, printers and serial ports. Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) provides the user with a graphical interface to connect to another host on the network. Malicious actors often use RDP/SMB connections for lateral movement on the network, code execution or to establish persistence on the host. Therefore, all SMB/RDP sessions should be audited on a regular basis. This query retrieves data on existing SMB/RDP connections in the environment.

osQuery.label: rdp_smb_connections_monitoring
osQuery.name: RDP / SMB Connections Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT pos.pid, p.name, u.username, pos.local_address, pos.local_port, pos.remote_address, pos.remote_port
FROM process_open_sockets pos
JOIN processes p ON p.pid = pos.pid
LEFT JOIN users u ON p.uid = u.uid
WHERE pos.local_port IN (445, 3389)
AND pos.remote_port!="";

techniques.T1048.002: Exfiltration Over Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol

queries.title: RDP and SMB Connections Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Screensavers are programs that are execute after a certain time of user inactivity. Malware can set its malicious executable as a screensaver in order to gain persistence on the host. The GroupPolicy keys override normal user selection through the interface. This query retrieves the following data about screensavers:
* Hosts with screensaver option was enabled
* Image file paths to executables that are specified to run as screensavers
* Image file paths to executables that are specified to run as screensavers by group policy
* Hosts where screensaver option is not secured and password is not required for unlocking
* User inactivity timeout before the screensaver is executed on the host

osQuery.label: screensaver_enabled_monitoring
osQuery.name: ScreenSaver Enabled Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Control Panel\Desktop"
AND name LIKE "ScreenSaveActive"
AND data="1";

osQuery.label: screensaver_executable_monitoring
osQuery.name: ScreenSaver Executable Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT r.key AS reg_key, r.path, r.name, r.data, h.sha256, DATETIME(r.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry r
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=r.data
WHERE r.key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Control Panel\Desktop"
AND r.name LIKE "SCRNSAVE.EXE";

osQuery.label: screensaver_executable_gp_monitoring
osQuery.name: ScreenSaver Executable GroupPolicy Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT r.key AS reg_key, r.path, r.name, r.data, h.sha256, DATETIME(r.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry r
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=r.data
WHERE r.key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Control Panel\Desktop"
AND r.name LIKE "SCRNSAVE.EXE";

osQuery.label: screensaver_unsecure_unlocking_monitoring
osQuery.name: ScreenSaver Not Secured Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Control Panel\Desktop"
AND name LIKE "ScreenSaverIsSecure"
AND data="0";

osQuery.label: screensaver_timeout_monitoring
osQuery.name: ScreenSaver Timeout Before Execution
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Control Panel\Desktop"
AND name LIKE "ScreenSaveTimeout";

queries.title: Screensaver Settings Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on the recent Outlook email attachments. When you open an email attachment, Outlook saves its copy to a temporary folder on the hard drive. This way, the email and its content are accessible offline.

osQuery.label: outlook_email_attachments_monitoring
osQuery.name: Outlook Email Attachment Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.path, f.filename, h.sha256, f.uid, f.gid, f.mode, f.size,
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time,
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified,
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time,
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS creation_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type
FROM file f LEFT JOIN hash h ON f.path=h.path
WHERE f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Outlook\%";

queries.title: Outlook Email Attachments Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Wininit.exe is a background application launcher for Windows OS which is responsible for running the Windows Initialization process. It launches critical system services on the system such as the Local Security Authority (lsass), the Service Control Manager (SCM), and the Local Session Manager (LSM) at startup. Malware could use a legitimate name for Windows services when attempting to evade the system defenses. One way to distinguish between legitimate and fake services is to check for a parent process that started the suspicious executable. For example, in case of lsass.exe and services.exe the parent should always be wininit.exe. This query identifies hosts where this is not the case.

osQuery.label: parent_process_not_wininit_tmp
osQuery.name: Process Parent Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE parent_child AS 
SELECT 
child_processes.pid AS child_pid,
child_processes.name AS child_name,
parent_processes.pid AS parent_pid,
parent_processes.name AS parent_name 
FROM processes child_processes 
INNER JOIN processes parent_processes ON child_processes.parent=parent_processes.pid;

osQuery.label: parent_process_not_wininit
osQuery.name: Process Parent
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
child_pid, child_name, parent_pid, parent_name 
FROM parent_child 
WHERE 
child_name IN ("lsass.exe", "services.exe") AND 
parent_name NOT LIKE "wininit.exe";

queries.title: Parent Process Not Wininit

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It monitors the registry to check if the windows firewall has been disabled. Disabled firewall may indicate malicious activity.

osQuery.label: windows_firewall_disabled
osQuery.name: Windows Firewall Disabled
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch")
FROM registry
WHERE 
(key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Services\sharedaccess\parameters\firewallpolicy\%profile"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet002\Services\sharedaccess\parameters\firewallpolicy\%profile"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\sharedaccess\parameters\firewallpolicy\%profile"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\%profile") 
AND name LIKE "EnableFirewall"
AND data NOT LIKE "1";

queries.title: Windows Firewall Disabled

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It returns data on Internet Explorer extensions installed on the host for a particular user. This information can be used in attempt to detect an extension performing malicious activity since it is a common practice for malware to disguise itself as a legitimate and well-known browser extension.

osQuery.label: windows_ie_browser_extensions
osQuery.name: Windows IE Browser Extensions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, registry_path, version, path 
FROM ie_extensions;

queries.title: Windows IE Browser Extensions Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Remote Management (WinRM) service allows remote administration of a Windows host. It is often used by malicious groups, such as Cobalt Strike, for remote execution on a target host. By default, WinRM is enabled only on Windows Servers and disabled on all client operating systems. This query retrieves registry keys that configure WinRM on the host:
* Key "AllowUnencryptedTraffic": 1 will configure WinRM service to send and receive unencrypted messages over the network, this value should be set to 0
* Key "AllowRemoteShellAccess": when set to 1 new remote shell connections will be accepted by the server

osQuery.label: winrm_registry_configuration
osQuery.name: WinRM Registry Keys

queries.title: Windows Remote Management Allows Unencrypted Traffic

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The presence of multiple Service Host processes (svchost.exe) on the system is a normal behaviour for Windows hosts. However, all svchost.exe processes normally execute with the "-k" argument. This query retrieves data on svchost.exe that executed without this argument which is an indicator of suspicious activity on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_svchost_command_line_monitoring
osQuery.name: Svchost Process Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT s.name, s.service_type, s.display_name, s.status, s.pid, s.start_type, s.path, s.module_path, s.description, s.user_account
FROM services s 
WHERE s.path LIKE "%\svchost.exe%" AND 
s.path NOT LIKE "% -k%";

queries.title: Svchost Command Line Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves event log file configuration settings on the system. The following data is retrieved:
* File name
* Archive - if True, a file that contains Windows events should be archived
* Compressed - if True, a file that contains Windows events is compressed
* Encrypted - if True, file that contains Windows events is encrypted
* Hidden - if True, a file that contains Windows events is hidden
* File size in bytes
* Maximum file size in bytes permitted for the file. A value of zero indicates no size limit. 
* CreationDate - date that the file that contains Windows events was created
* LastAccessed - date and time that the file that contains Windows events was last accessed
* LastModified - date and time that the file that contains Windows events was last modified
* NumberOfRecords - number of records in the file that contains Windows events
* OverwriteOutDated - number of days after which an event can be overwritten
* OverWritePolicy - current overwrite policy the Event Log service employs for this log file
* Sources - list of applications that are registered to log into this log file
* Status - current status of the object: OK, Error, Degraded, Unknown, Predicting Failure, Starting, Stopping, Service, Stressed, NonRecover, No Contact, Lost Comm
** 1 means True, 0 means False

osQuery.label: event_log_files_configuration_monitoring
osQuery.name: Event Log Files Configuration
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
FileName,
Archive,
Compressed,
Encrypted,
Hidden,
FileSize,
MaxFileSize,
DATETIME(CreationDate, "unixepoch") AS "Creation_Date",
DATETIME(LastAccessed, "unixepoch") AS "Last_Accessed",
DATETIME(LastModified, "unixepoch") AS "Last_Modified",
NumberOfRecords,
OverwriteOutDated,
OverWritePolicy,
Sources,
Status
FROM Win32_NTEventlogFile;

queries.title: Event Log Files Configuration Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It identifies attempts to reset account password remotely. Malicious actors may manipulate account password to maintain persistence on the host or to evade password duration policies. All hosts where this query returned results should be investigated for suspicious activity:
* remote logon datetime
* remote ip
* remote port
* remote logon user name
* full remote logon event data
* full password reset event data

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_remote_password_reset
osQuery.name: Event Logs Data
osQuery.sql:
WITH b1 AS (
SELECT 
datetime, 
regex_match(data, '"TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, '"IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) AS remote_ip, 
regex_match(data, '"IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) AS remote_port, 
regex_match(data, '"TargetLogonId":"([^"]+)', 1) AS logon_id, 
data 
FROM windows_eventlog 
WHERE 
channel = "Security" AND 
eventid = 4624 AND 
remote_ip NOT IN ("-", "127.0.0.1", "::1")),
b2 AS (
SELECT 
datetime, 
regex_match(data, '"SubjectLogonId":"([^"]+)', 1) as logon_id, 
data 
FROM windows_eventlog 
WHERE 
channel = "Security" AND 
eventid = 4724)
SELECT
b1.datetime AS remote_logon_datetime,
b1.remote_ip,
b1.remote_port,
b1.target_user_name,
b1.data as remote_logon_data,
b2.data as password_reset_data
FROM b1 
JOIN b2 ON b1.logon_id = b2.logon_id;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: Remote Password Reset

queries.description: This query is applicable to MacOS. MacOS LaunchAgent "Apple Media Services Tool Daemon" (amstoold) that defines XPC service is missing from the default OS installations. But since it is specified as the launchd file that starts at the user logon, an attacker could attempt to put their custom binary in the location pointed to by the "com.apple.amstoold.plist" to achieve persistence on the host. All systems where launchd plist for amstoold and the binary itself exist should be review for potential compromise. All systems where this binary does not exist but laucnhd entry is present should be reviewed in order to remove/update the offending plist.

osQuery.label: macos_amstoold_binary_monitoring
osQuery.name: Launchd Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
l.path, l.name, l.label, l.run_at_load, l.program_arguments, l.process_type, h.sha256 
FROM launchd l 
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=l.program_arguments 
WHERE 
l.path LIKE "%amstoold%";

queries.title: LaunchAgent Amstoold Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It determines if the option to change the Windows password has been disabled on the system by setting the registry key "DisablePasswordChange". A registry key change to "1" value may be related to malware establishing the persistence on the host by preventing the account password change.

osQuery.label: windows_password_change_disabled
osQuery.name: Windows Password Change Disabled
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\%ControlSet%\Services\Netlogon\Parameters")
AND name LIKE "DisablePasswordChange" AND data <> 0;

queries.title: Registry Key DisablePasswordChange Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It is designed to retrieve details for logical drives on the system:
* device_id - the drive id
* free_space - the amount of free space in bytes
* size - the total amount of space in bytes
* file_system - the file system of the drive
* boot_partition - true if Windows booted from this partition

osQuery.label: mapped_drives
osQuery.name: Mapped Drives

queries.title: Mapped Drives Monitoring
warnings: This query does not show smb shares connected without mapping to a drive

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It can detect presence of China Chopper webshell in specific server directories:
* search for webshell in server host directories

osQuery.label: yara_china_chopper_webshell_tiny_php
osQuery.name: china_chopper_webshell_tiny_php
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, matches, count, strings
FROM yara
WHERE
path LIKE "/var/www/%%" AND
sigrule='rule china_chopper_webshell { meta: author = "FireEye" strings: $s0 = /[<][?]php\s\@eval[(]\$_POST\[.+\][)];[?][>]/ nocase ascii  condition: any of them }' AND
matches!=""

queries.title: Linux China Chopper Webshell Monitoring
warnings: This query can take some time to load the results due to YARA scans.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the following information from installed programs on the host:
* user product name
* version
* installation location
* installation source
* language
* publisher
* path and filename of the uninstaller
* intall_date

osQuery.label: installed_programs_on_windows_host
osQuery.name: Installed Programs On Windows Host

queries.title: Installed Programs On Windows Host

queries.description:
This queries is applicable to MacOS. It is designed to capture volatile and non-volatile data from the endpoint that can help with forensic investigations.
New queries:
Removed queries:
Updated queries:

osQuery.label: hosts_file
osQuery.name: Hosts File Data

osQuery.label: sha256_hash_of_running_processes
osQuery.name: SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.pid, p.name, p.path, p.cmdline, p.state,  h.sha256 
FROM processes p 
INNER JOIN hash h 
ON p.path=h.path;

osQuery.label: interface_names_and_associated_ips
osQuery.name: Interface Names And Associated IPs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT interface, address, mask, type, friendly_name 
FROM interface_addresses 
WHERE interface NOT LIKE "lo%" 
AND interface!=1;

osQuery.label: process_running_without_a_binary_on_disk
osQuery.name: Process Running Without A Binary On Disk

osQuery.label: logged_in_users
osQuery.name: Logged In Users

osQuery.label: disk_encryption_monitoring
osQuery.name: Disk Encryption Status and Details

osQuery.label: operating_system_attributes
osQuery.name: Operating System Attributes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version, major, minor, patch, build, platform, codename, install_date
FROM os_version;

osQuery.label: listening_ports
osQuery.name: Listening Ports
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.path, lp.address, lp.protocol, lp.port 
FROM listening_ports lp LEFT JOIN processes p ON lp.pid = p.pid 
WHERE lp.port != 0 
AND lp.address!="127.0.0.1" 
AND lp.address!="::1" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\system32\%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/libexec/%";

osQuery.label: startup_items
osQuery.name: Startup Items
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, args, source, type, status, username 
FROM startup_items;

osQuery.label: user_accounts
osQuery.name: User Accounts
osQuery.sql:
SELECT uid, gid, username, description, directory, shell, uuid, type 
FROM users;

osQuery.label: usb_devices
osQuery.name: USB Devices
osQuery.sql:
SELECT usb_address, usb_port, vendor, version, model, serial, class, subclass, protocol, removable 
FROM usb_devices;

osQuery.label: processes_with_network_connections
osQuery.name: Processes With Network Connections

osQuery.label: user_groups_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Groups
osQuery.sql:
SELECT g.groupname, u.username, g.comment
FROM groups g
LEFT JOIN user_groups ug ON g.gid=ug.gid
LEFT JOIN users u ON ug.uid=u.uid;

osQuery.label: mounted_devices_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Mounted Devices and Filesystems
osQuery.sql:
SELECT device, device_alias, path, type, blocks_size, blocks, blocks_free, blocks_available, inodes, inodes_free, flags
FROM mounts;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT module_id, sha256, module_path 
FROM loaded_module_hashes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_processes
osQuery.name: Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT pid, name AS process_name, path AS process_path 
FROM processes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_vs_processes
osQuery.name: Loaded Module ID To Process ID
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT lh.module_id, pmm.pid AS pid 
FROM process_memory_map pmm 
JOIN loaded_module_hashes lh ON lh.module_path=pmm.path;

osQuery.label: wifi_networks_monitoring
osQuery.name: Wi-Fi Networks Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT ssid, network_name, security_type, datetime(last_connected,"unixepoch"), passpoint, possibly_hidden, roaming, roaming_profile, captive_portal, auto_login, temporarily_disabled, disabled
FROM wifi_networks;

osQuery.label: macos_account_policy_data_monitoring
osQuery.name: Account Policy Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
users.username, uid, DATETIME(creation_time, "unixepoch") as "Account Created", failed_login_count,
CASE failed_login_timestamp WHEN "0.0" THEN "0.0" ELSE DATETIME(failed_login_timestamp, "unixepoch") END "Last Failed Login Attempt",
DATETIME(password_last_set_time,  "unixepoch") AS "Password Last Changed"
FROM account_policy_data
JOIN users USING(uid);

osQuery.label: macos_alf_exceptions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Application Layer Firewall Service Exceptions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT path, state 
FROM alf_exceptions;

osQuery.label: macos_alf_explicit_auths_monitoring
osQuery.name: ALF Services Allowed Networking
osQuery.sql:
SELECT process
FROM alf_explicit_auths;

osQuery.label: macos_apps_monitoring
osQuery.name: Installed Applications Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, bundle_executable, bundle_identifier, bundle_name, bundle_version, bundle_package_type,
environment, element, development_region, display_name, info_string, category, applescript_enabled, copyright,
datetime(last_opened_time, "unixepoch") AS last_time_used 
FROM apps;

osQuery.label: arp_cache_inspection
osQuery.name: ARPP Cache
osQuery.sql:
SELECT address, mac
FROM arp_cache;

osQuery.label: macos_asl_monitoring
osQuery.name: ASL System Events Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") as time, host, sender, facility, pid, gid, uid,
CASE level
WHEN "0" THEN "Emergency Level"
WHEN "1" THEN "Alert Level"
WHEN "2" THEN "Critical Level"
WHEN "3" THEN "Error Level"
WHEN "4" THEN "Warning Level"
WHEN "5" THEN "Notice Level"
WHEN "6" THEN "Info Level"
WHEN "7" THEN "Debug Level"
ELSE "Unknown" END "Logging Level",
message, extra
FROM asl;

osQuery.label: macos_authorizations_monitoring
osQuery.name: Authorization Rights Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
label, modified,
allow_root, timeout, version, tries, authenticate_user, shared, comment,
created, class, session_owner
FROM authorizations;

osQuery.label: certificates_search
osQuery.name: Certificates
osQuery.sql:
SELECT common_name, subject, issuer, ca, self_signed, not_valid_before, not_valid_after, sha1, path, serial
FROM certificates;

osQuery.label: chrome_extensions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Chrome Extensions

osQuery.label: macos_linux_crontab_monitoring
osQuery.name: System and User Crontab Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT event, minute, hour, day_of_month, month, day_of_week, command, path 
FROM crontab;

osQuery.label: macos_linux_dns_resolvers_monitoring
osQuery.name: DNS Resolvers

osQuery.label: macos_event_taps_monitoring
osQuery.name: Installed Event Taps
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
p1.name AS "Process Being Tapped",
p2.name AS "Tapping Process",
CASE et.enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled " ELSE "Disabled" END "Event Tap Status",
et.event_tap_id,
et.event_tapped
FROM event_taps et
JOIN processes p1 ON p1.pid = et.process_being_tapped
JOIN processes p2 ON p2.pid = et.tapping_process;

osQuery.label: macos_kernel_extensions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Kernel Extensions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT idx, refs, size, name, version, linked_against, path
FROM kernel_extensions;

osQuery.label: macos_keychain_items_monitoring
osQuery.name: Keychain Items Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
label, description, comment, created, modified, type, path
FROM
keychain_items;

osQuery.label: macos_linux_known_hosts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Known Hosts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
users.username, uid, key, key_file
FROM known_hosts
JOIN users USING(uid);

osQuery.label: macos_linux_last_logins_logouts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Logins/Logouts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
l.username, l.tty, l.pid, 
p.name AS "process name", 
l.type, 
DATETIME(l.time, "unixepoch") AS "entry timestamp", 
l.host as hostname
FROM last l
LEFT JOIN processes p ON p.pid = l.pid;

osQuery.label: macos_launchd_monitoring
osQuery.name: LaunchAgents and LaunchDaemons Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
path, name, label, program,
run_at_load, keep_alive, disabled,
username, groupname, stdout_path, stderr_path,
start_interval, program_arguments, watch_paths,
queue_directories, inetd_compatibility,
start_on_mount, root_directory,
working_directory, process_type
FROM launchd;

osQuery.label: macos_managed_policies_monitoring
osQuery.name: Managed Policies Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
domain, uuid, name, value, username,
CASE manual WHEN "1" THEN "True" ELSE "False" END "Loaded manually"
FROM managed_policies;

osQuery.label: macos_package_install_history_monitoring
osQuery.name: Package Install History Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
package_id, DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") AS "install_time", name, version, source, content_type
FROM
package_install_history;

osQuery.label: platform_information
osQuery.name: Platform Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT vendor, version, date, revision, address, size, volume_size, extra
FROM platform_info;

osQuery.label: macos_linux_process_envs_monitoring
osQuery.name: Process Environment Variables Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.name AS "Process Name", p.pid, 
pe.key AS "Environment Variable Name", 
pe.value AS "Environment Variable Value"
FROM process_envs pe 
JOIN processes p ON p.pid=pe.pid;

osQuery.label: macos_shared_folders_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shared Folders

osQuery.label: shell_history
osQuery.name: Shell History

osQuery.label: macos_sip_config_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Integrity Protection (SIP) Status
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
config_flag, 
CASE enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled " ELSE "Disabled" END "enabled_status",
CASE enabled_nvram WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled " ELSE "Disabled" END "enabled_nvram_status"
FROM sip_config;

osQuery.label: ssh_configs_monitoring
osQuery.name: SSH Configuration Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT u.username, sc.block, sc.option, sc.ssh_config_file, h.sha256
FROM users u
JOIN ssh_configs sc USING(uid)
JOIN hash h ON h.path = sc.ssh_config_file;

osQuery.label: ssh_user_keys_monitoring
osQuery.name: User SSH Keys Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT u.username, usk.path, usk.encrypted, sha256
FROM users u
JOIN user_ssh_keys usk
USING (uid)
JOIN hash h ON h.path=usk.path;

osQuery.label: macos_linux_sudoers_monitoring
osQuery.name: Sudoers Data

osQuery.label: macos_linux_writable_suid_bin_monitoring
osQuery.name: Writable Suid Binaries Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.path, sb.username, sb.groupname, f.mode, sb.permissions
FROM suid_bin sb
LEFT JOIN file f ON sb.path = f.path
WHERE
sb.permissions LIKE regex_match(sb.permissions, "(S|G)", 0) AND
f.mode LIKE regex_match(f.mode, ".*(2|3|6|7)$", 0);

osQuery.label: macos_linux_system_controls
osQuery.name: System Controls Settings
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, oid, subsystem, current_value, config_value, type
FROM system_controls;

osQuery.label: inventory_system_information
osQuery.name: Inventory System Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT hostname, local_hostname, cpu_type, cpu_subtype, cpu_brand, physical_memory 
FROM system_info;

osQuery.label: time_machine
osQuery.name: Time Machine Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
datetime(b.backup_date,"unixepoch") as "Backup Date", 
destination_id, alias, root_volume_uuid,
bytes_available, bytes_used, encryption 
FROM time_machine_backups AS b
JOIN time_machine_destinations
USING (destination_id);

osQuery.label: macos_xprotect_entries_monitoring
osQuery.name: XProtect Signatures

osQuery.label: macos_xprotect_reports_monitoring
osQuery.name: XProtect Reports Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, user_action, DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") AS "Quarantine Alert" 
FROM xprotect_reports;

queries.title: Forensic Snapshot MacOS 0.0.8
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Background Activity Monitor (BAM) is a Windows service that controls activity of background applications. The "BAM" registry key provides path of the executable file that was run on the system and last execution date/time. This information could be used for forensic investigation of a compromised hosts. This query collects BAM registry entries from the host:
* user security identifier (SID)
* username
* path - application file path
* last_execution_time - most recent time application was executed

osQuery.label: windows_bam_entries_monitoring
osQuery.name: Background Activity Monitor Entries
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
sid,
u.username,
path,
DATETIME(last_execution_time, "unixepoch") AS last_execution_time
FROM background_activities_moderator
JOIN users u ON u.uuid=sid
WHERE
path NOT IN ("Version", "SequenceNumber") AND
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0) AND
u.username LIKE regex_match(u.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0)
ORDER BY last_execution_time DESC;

queries.title: Background Activity Monitor (BAM) Entries

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Malware can modify service settings using the registry to disable security tools currently running on the system. This query returns registry data on services that have a "DeleteFlag" set.

osQuery.label: registry_deleteflags_for_services
osQuery.name: Registry DeleteFlags For Services
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\system\controlset001\services\%"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\system\controlset002\services\%"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\system\currentcontrolset\services\%")
AND name LIKE "DeleteFlag"
AND data NOT LIKE "0";

queries.title: Registry DeleteFlags For Services Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Volume Shadow Copies Service (VSS) is a Windows service that provides functionality for backing up critical system files to assist with the system recovery. Volume Shadow Copy (VSC) is a system snapshot at a given point in time. The "FilesNotToSnapshot" registry key contains the list of files that should be deleted from the newly created VSC. By default, the hibernation file is not included in this list. Malware could attempt to add the "hiberfil.sys" to this list in attempt to hide its behavior from the analyst.

osQuery.label: hiberfil_removed_from_vsc
osQuery.name: BackupRestore Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\system\currentcontrolset\control\backuprestore\filesnottosnapshot"
AND data LIKE "%hiberfil%";

queries.title: Hiberfil Removed From Volume Shadow Copy

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. TCP timestamps are an extension of the original TCP Stack. Enabled TCP Timestamp could be used by malicious actors to collect information about the target such as host uptime, their operation systems (OS), and the network layout. This query retrieves data related to the TCP Timestamp registry settings.

osQuery.label: tcp_timestamp_registry
osQuery.name: TCP Timestamp Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch")
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Services\Tcpip\Parameters"
AND name="Tcp1323Opts"
AND data IN ("2", "3");

queries.title: TCP Timestamp Registry Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Windows Portable Devices (WPD) class subkey contains information such as the manufacturer, model/description, and the volume name/label of the connected devices such as phones, external hard drives, cameras, etc. This query retrieves data on this registry key.

osQuery.label: windows_portable_devices_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Portable Devices Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, name, type, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Portable Devices\Devices\%"
AND data LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="device_name");

queries.title: Windows Portable Devices Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It determines whether "Developer Mode" state is enabled on the host. Enabling developer mode lowers the security level of a system significantly. It allows users to run uncertified or unsigned applications. Windows also warns of such risks when the mode is initially enabled. This setting is not recommended outside of development environments.

osQuery.label: developer_mode
osQuery.name: Developer Mode Registry Key

queries.title: Developer Mode Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on Windows Defender features from the host. Data displayed:
* AMEngineVersion - The AM Engine version (major, minor, build, revision)
* AMProductVersion - Product version (major, minor, build, revision)
* AMServiceEnabled - If the AM Engine is enabled
* AMServiceVersion - Service version (major, minor, build, revision)
* AntispywareEnabled - Specifies whether Antispyware protection is enabled
* AntispywareSignatureAge - Antispyware Signature age in days
* AntispywareSignatureLastUpdated - Antispyware Last updated  
* AntispywareSignatureVersion - The Antispyware Signature version (major, minor, build, revision)
* AntivirusEnabled - Specifies whether Antivirus protection is enabled
* AntivirusSignatureAge - Antivirus Signature age in days
* AntivirusSignatureLastUpdated - Antivirus Last updated
* AntivirusSignatureVersion - The Antivirus Signature version (major, minor, build, revision)
* BehaviorMonitorEnabled - Specifies whether behavior monitoring is enabled
* FullScanAge - Last full scan age in days
* FullScanEndTime - Time of last Full Scan end 
* FullScanStartTime - Time of last Full Scan start 
* IoavProtectionEnabled - Scan all downloaded files and attachments
* LastFullScanSource - Last scan source
* LastQuickScanSource - Last Quick scan source
* NISEnabled - If the NRI Engine is enabled
* NISEngineVersion - NRI Engine version (major, minor, build, revision)
* NISSignatureAge - NRI Signature age in days
* NISSignatureLastUpdated - NRI Last updated
* NISSignatureVersion - The NRI Signature version (major, minor, build, revision)
* OnAccessProtectionEnabled - Specifies whether the computer is monitoring file and program activity on your computer
* QuickScanAge - Last quick scan age in days
* QuickScanEndTime - Time of last Quick Scan end
* QuickScanStartTime - Time of last Quick Scan start
* RealTimeProtectionEnabled - Specifies whether real-time protection is enabled
* RealTimeScanDirection - Real-time scan direction enumeration

osQuery.label: windows_msft_mpcomputerstatus_windows_defender_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Defender Settings
osQuery.sql:
SELECT AMEngineVersion, AMProductVersion,
CASE AMServiceEnabled WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS AMService_Status,
AMServiceVersion,
CASE AntispywareEnabled WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS Antispyware_Status,
AntispywareSignatureAge, DATETIME(AntispywareSignatureLastUpdated, "unixepoch") as "AntispywareSignatureLastUpdated",
AntispywareSignatureVersion, 
CASE AntivirusEnabled WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS Antivirus_Status,
AntivirusSignatureAge, DATETIME(AntivirusSignatureLastUpdated, "unixepoch") AS "AntivirusSignatureLastUpdated",
AntivirusSignatureVersion, 
CASE BehaviorMonitorEnabled WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS "BehaviorMonitor_Status",
FullScanAge, FullScanEndTime, FullScanStartTime, 
CASE IoavProtectionEnabled WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS "IoavProtection_Status",
CASE LastFullScanSource
WHEN "0" THEN "Unknown"
WHEN "1" THEN "User"
WHEN "2" THEN "System"
WHEN "3" THEN "Real-Time"
WHEN "4" THEN "IOAV"
END AS "LastFullScan_Source", 
CASE LastQuickScanSource
WHEN "0" THEN "Unknown"
WHEN "1" THEN "User"
WHEN "2" THEN "System"
WHEN "3" THEN "Real-Time"
WHEN "4" THEN "IOAV"
END AS "LastQuickScan_Source", 
CASE "NISEnabled" WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS "NIS_Status",
NISEngineVersion, NISSignatureAge, NISSignatureLastUpdated, NISSignatureVersion,
CASE OnAccessProtectionEnabled WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS "OnAccessProtection_Status",
QuickScanAge, DATETIME(QuickScanEndTime, "unixepoch") AS "QuickScanEndTime",
DATETIME(QuickScanStartTime, "unixepoch") AS "QuickScanStartTime",
CASE RealTimeProtectionEnabled WHEN "0" THEN "Disabled" WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" END AS "RealTimeProtection_Status",
CASE RealTimeScanDirection
WHEN "0" THEN "Both"
WHEN "1" THEN "Incoming"
WHEN "2" THEN "Outgoing"
END AS "RealTimeScan_Direction"
FROM MSFT_MpComputerStatus
WHERE
AMService_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="AMService Status") AND 
Antispyware_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="AntiSpyware Status") AND
Antivirus_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="Antivirus Status") AND
BehaviorMonitor_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="BehaviorMonitor Status") AND
IoavProtection_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="IoavProtection Status") AND
NIS_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="NIS Status") AND 
OnAccessProtection_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="OnAccessProtection Status") AND
RealTimeProtection_Status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="RealTimeProtection Status");

queries.title: Windows Defender Settings Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on the registry keys required for Orbital configuration.

osQuery.label: orbital_registry_configuration
osQuery.name: Orbital Registry Configuration

queries.title: Orbital Registry Configuration Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. WMI class "Win32_DeviceGuard" contains information about Windows Defender Device Guard hardware-based security features. Data displayed are: 
* AvailableSecurityProperties - enumerates and reports state on the relevant security properties for Windows Defender Device Guard
* CodeIntegrityPolicyEnforcementStatus - indicates the status of the Device Guard Code Integrity policy
* RequiredSecurityProperties - lists the required security properties to enable virtualization-based security
* SecurityServicesConfigured - indicates whether the Windows Defender Credential Guard (HVCI) service has been configured
* SecurityServicesRunning - indicated whether the Windows Defender Credential Guard service is running
* UsermodeCodeIntegrityPolicyEnforcementStatus - indicates the status of the Device Guard user mode Code Integrity policy 
* VirtualizationBasedSecurityStatus - indicates whether VBS is enabled and running

osQuery.label: device_guard_monitoring
osQuery.name: Device Guard Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
AvailableSecurityProperties,
CodeIntegrityPolicyEnforcementStatus,
RequiredSecurityProperties,
SecurityServicesConfigured,
SecurityServicesRunning,
UsermodeCodeIntegrityPolicyEnforcementStatus,
VirtualizationBasedSecurityStatus
FROM Win32_DeviceGuard;

queries.title: Device Guard Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves MS Office documents from the host that were "trusted" by the used and therefore have macros enabled.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_msoffice_trusted_documents
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
SPLIT(key, '\', 5) AS office_version,
path, name, data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
path LIKE (
SELECT
path || "\%\Word\Security\Trusted Documents\TrustRecords\%" AS temp
FROM registry
WHERE
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Office") AND
data LIKE "%FFFFFF7F";

queries.title: MS Office Trusted Documents Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs for Print Spooler service with EventID "808" that indicate this service failing to load a certain plug-in module. Such events could help to identify cases when Print Spooler service is loading malicious drivers like in cases of CVE-2021-1675 or CVE-2021-34527. All Digital Link Libraries (DLLs) that Print Spooler service attempted to load should be investigated:
* datetime - system time at which the event occurred 
* plugin_dll_name - PluginDllName field value
* dll_path -  a path to DLL that failed to load
* event_code - error code indicating the reason for DLL load failure 
* SHA256 for the DLL that failed to load if it is present on the host
* data - full data associated with the event

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_print_spooler_plugin_load
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'PluginDllName":"(.*)","ErrorCode', 1) as plugin_dll_name,
replace(regex_match(lower(data), '(c:\\\\windows\\\\[^\s]+\.dll)', 1),"\\", "\") as dll_path,
regex_match(data, 'ErrorCode":"([^"]+)', 1) as error_code,
h.sha256,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path = dll_path
WHERE
channel = "Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Admin" AND
eventid = 808;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs for Print Spooler Failed Plug-in Loads

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the list of commands that were executed using the start-menu's run command window. This is the same list which is used to populate the command history in the run window.

osQuery.label: windows_start_run_commands_monitoring
osQuery.name: Start/Run History Registry Key

queries.title: Windows Start/Run History Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It retrieves information on memory usage on the host:
* memory_total - total amount of physical RAM, in bytes
* memory_free - the amount of physical RAM, in bytes, left unused by the system
* buffers - the amount of physical RAM, in bytes, used for file buffers
* cached - he amount of physical RAM, in bytes, used as cache memory
* swap_cached - the amount of swap, in bytes, used as cache memory
* active - the total amount of buffer or page cache memory, in bytes, that is in active use
* inactive - the total amount of buffer or page cache memory, in bytes, that are free and available
* swap_total - the total amount of swap available, in bytes
* swap_free - the total amount of swap free, in bytes

osQuery.label: linux_memory_info
osQuery.name: Memory Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
memory_total, memory_free, buffers, cached, swap_cached, active, inactive, swap_total, swap_free
FROM
memory_info;

queries.title: Memory Information Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieves data on the parent process for the child process specified by the user.

osQuery.label: process_parent_tmp
osQuery.name: Process Parent Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE parent_child AS 
SELECT 
child_processes.pid AS child_pid, 
child_processes.name AS child_name, 
parent_processes.pid AS parent_pid, 
parent_processes.name AS parent_name
FROM processes child_processes 
INNER JOIN processes parent_processes 
ON child_processes.parent=parent_processes.pid;

osQuery.label: process_parent
osQuery.name: Process Parent
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
pid, name, path, cmdline, cwd, uid, gid, on_disk, total_size 
FROM processes 
WHERE 
pid IN (
SELECT 
parent_pid 
FROM parent_child 
WHERE 
child_name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="process_child"));

queries.title: Process Parent Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Named and anonymous pipes are used for Inter-process communication (IPC) on Windows. They may be used by various malware components, injected into different processes, for communicating with each other. Moreover, named pipe impersonation is a well known privilege escalation technique used in post exploitation frameworks such as Meterpreter. This query retrieves data for named and anonymous pipes on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_pipe
osQuery.name: Windows Pipe

queries.title: Windows Pipe Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves data about the applications installed on the system:
* name - Name of the Name.app folder
* path - Absolute and full Name.app path
* bundle_executable - Info properties CFBundleExecutable label
