* bundle_identifier - Info properties CFBundleIdentifier label
* bundle_name - Info properties CFBundleName label
* bundle_version - Info properties CFBundleVersion label
* bundle_package_type - Info properties CFBundlePackageType label
* environment - Application-set environment variables
* element - Does the app identify as a background agent
* development_region - Info properties CFBundleDevelopmentRegion label
* display_name - Info properties CFBundleDisplayName label
* info_string - Info properties CFBundleGetInfoString label
* category - The UTI that categorizes the app for the App Store
* applescript_enabled - Info properties NSAppleScriptEnabled label
* copyright - Info properties NSHumanReadableCopyright label
* last_time_used - The time that the app was last used  
Search suggestion:
* To find all applications running as background agents that did not come from the Apple store set element_patten to "1" and category_pattern to NOT "app-category"

osQuery.label: macos_apps_monitoring
osQuery.name: Installed Applications Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name,
path,
bundle_executable,
bundle_identifier,
bundle_name,
bundle_version,
bundle_package_type,
environment,
element,
development_region,
display_name,
info_string,
category,
applescript_enabled,
copyright,
datetime(last_opened_time, "unixepoch") AS last_time_used 
FROM apps 
WHERE
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0) AND
bundle_identifier LIKE regex_match(bundle_identifier, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "bundle_identifier_pattern"), 0) AND
bundle_name LIKE regex_match(bundle_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "bundle_name_pattern"), 0) AND
bundle_version LIKE regex_match(bundle_version, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "bundle_version_pattern"), 0) AND
element LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "element_pattern") AND
category LIKE regex_match(category, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "category_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Installed Applications Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) provides management of local account passwords of domain joined computers. LAPS settings are stored in the registry under the "AdmPwd" registry key and should be examined for proper configuration.
Setting that should be monitored are listed below:
* AdmPwdEnabled - should be set to 1 for enabled
* PasswordComplexity
* PasswordLength
* PasswordAgeDays
* AdminAccountName - is the name of administrator account to manage
* PwdExpirationProtectionEnabled - should be set to not allow password expiration time longer than required by policy
* PwdEncryptionEnabled, EncryptionKey, PublicKey - password encryption settings 
* PwdHistoryEnabled - should be set to maintain password history
* ManualPasswordChangeProtectionEnabled - set to disable manual changes of password
* MaxPasswordAge

osQuery.label: password_settings_monitoring
osQuery.name: Password Settings Registry Key

queries.title: Password Settings Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. The Mozilla Fifefox browsers version prior Mozilla Firefox 72.0.1 and Firefox ESR 68.4.1 are vulnerable to CVE-2019-17026 vulnerability exploitation. CVE-2019-17026 is a type confusion vulnerability in Just-In-Time(JIT) compiler for Mozilla's JavaScript engine allowing for arbitrary code execution. This query retrieves hosts where the vulnerable browser version is installed.

osQuery.label: cve_2019_17026_monitoring
osQuery.name: Vulnerable FireFox Versions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version 
FROM programs
WHERE 
((name LIKE "%Mozilla%" OR name LIKE "%Firefox%") AND name NOT LIKE "%maintenance%" AND name NOT LIKE "%ESR%" AND CAST(SPLIT(version,".",0) AS INTEGER) < 72) OR
(name LIKE "%Mozilla%ESR%" AND CAST(SPLIT(version,".",0) || SPLIT(version,".",1) || SPLIT(version,".", 2) AS INTEGER) < 6841);

queries.title: Firefox CVE-2019-17026 Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It retrieves information on installed RPM package files from the host:
* package - RPM package name
* path - file path within the package
* username - file default username from info DB
* groupname - file default groupname from info DB
* mode - file permissions mode from info DB
* size - expected file size in bytes from RPM info DB
* sha256 - SHA256 file digest from RPM info DB

osQuery.label: linux_rpm_package_files
osQuery.name: RPM Package Files Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
package, path, username, groupname, mode, size, sha256
FROM
rpm_package_files
WHERE
package LIKE regex_match(package, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "package_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: RPM Package Files Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. A Browser Helper Object (BHO) is an add-on designed for Internet Explorer to enhance its functionality. BHOs are often used by malware for malicious purposes such as establishing persistence on the host or for browser hijacking.

osQuery.label: browser_helper_objects
osQuery.name: Browser Helper Objects

queries.title: Browser Helper Objects Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It returns attributes of files whose path matches the provided pattern. Retrieved information includes:
* directory - directory of file(s)
* filename - name portion of file path
* uid - owning user ID
* gid - owning group ID
* mode - permission bits
* device - device ID (optional)
* size - size of file in bytes
* atime - last access time
* mtime - last modification time
* ctime - last status change time
* btime - birth or create time 
* hard_links - number of hard links 
* symlinks - 1 if the path is a symlink, otherwise 0
* file_id - file ID
* sha256 of the file

osQuery.label: file_search
osQuery.name: File Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.directory, f.filename, f.uid, f.gid, 
f.mode, f.device, f.size, 
f.atime, 
f.mtime, 
f.ctime, 
f.btime, 
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.file_id, h.sha256
FROM file f 
LEFT JOIN hash h on f.path=h.path 
WHERE 
f.path LIKE (SELECT v from __vars WHERE n="file_path") AND 
f.path NOT LIKE (SELECT v from __vars WHERE n="not_file_path");

queries.title: File Search
warnings: This query can produce excessive amounts of data and results can take time depending on the number and size of files being queried. Careful selection of parameters is advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Malware may attempt to modify the "FirewallPolicy" registry key enabling exceptions in Windows Firewall. This could result in unsolicited incoming messages as well as ensuring that configured exception rules are active. This query returns information on registry key if "Do not allow exceptions" policy setting for Windows Firewall was enabled.

osQuery.label: registry_firewall_exception_enabled
osQuery.name: Registry Firewall Exceptions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy\%Profile"
AND name LIKE "DoNotAllowExceptions" 
AND data NOT LIKE "0";

queries.title: Registry Firewall Exception Enabled

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Event Logs with ID 1 and Provider "Microsoft-Windows-Audit-CVE" is a new edition by Microsoft to monitor vulnerabilities exploitation on the host. For example, CVE-2020-0601 is a vulnerability in the way Windows validates Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) certificates. If unpatched it allows attackers to spoof a code-signing certificate that could be used to sign a malicious executable. On the system where the update to generate the new Windows Event Logs has been applied, the Windows Event Log with Event ID 1 will be generated indicating an attempt to exploit CVE-2020-0601. This query is designed to retrieve such Windows Event Logs.

osQuery.label: cert_validation_exploit_monitoring
osQuery.name: Event Logs for Attempt to Exploit a Known Vulnerability
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Application" AND
eventid = 1 AND 
provider_name LIKE "%Audit-CVE";

queries.title: Monitoring Event Logs for Attempts to Exploit a Known Vulnerability

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Client/Server Run-time Subsystem (CSRSS) is responsible for Win32 console handling, GUI shutdown, and running the Console Window Host process (conhost). Malware could name itself "conhost.exe" attempting to evade the system defences. One way to distinguish between legitimate and fake conhost services is to check for a parent process that started the suspicious executable. In case of conhost.exe the parent should always be csrss.exe.

osQuery.label: unusual_conhost_parent_process_tmp
osQuery.name: Conhost Parent Process Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE parent_child AS 
SELECT 
child_processes.pid AS child_pid,
child_processes.name AS child_name,
parent_processes.pid AS parent_pid,
parent_processes.name AS parent_name 
FROM processes child_processes 
INNER JOIN processes parent_processes ON child_processes.parent=parent_processes.pid;

osQuery.label: unusual_conhost_parent_process
osQuery.name: Conhost Parent Process
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
child_pid, child_name, parent_pid, parent_name 
FROM parent_child 
WHERE 
LOWER(child_name) IN ("conhost.exe") AND 
parent_name != "csrss.exe";

queries.title: Unusual Conhost Parent Process

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs) that are specified in "AppInit_DLLs" registry key are loaded by user32.dll into every process that loads user32.dll. Malware with Admin permissions can modify this registry key to establish persistence on the host. This query retrieves data on what DLL are listed under "AppInit_Dlls"  key value.

osQuery.label: registry_appinit_dll_key
osQuery.name: Registry AppInit_Dll Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE ( key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows" 
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432NODE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows" )
AND name LIKE "AppInit_DLLs";

queries.title: Registry AppInit_Dll Key Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. This query will show the registry path of HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\.NETFramework if it's subkey ETWEnabled has been set to 0, which means .NET event tracing has been disabled.

osQuery.label: disabling_dotnet_event_tracing_via_registry_monitoring
osQuery.name: .NET Event Tracing Disabled Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT datetime(mtime, "unixepoch", "UTC") AS last_modified, path as reg_path, name, data 
FROM registry 
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\.NETFramework" 
AND name ="ETWEnabled" AND data="0";

queries.title: Disable .NET Event Tracing via Registry Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves information about running binaries that are unsigned. This information includes: |
  * Process_ID
  * Process_Path
Note that it does not take Catalog signed files into account, and as such requires additional filtering.

osQuery.label: processes_launched_from_unsigned_disk_files
osQuery.name: Processes Launched From Unsigned Disk Files

queries.title: Processes Launched From Unsigned Disk Files

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. The MacOS authorization database controls access to elevated rights in the MacOS User Interface. It is initialized from the property list file "/System/Library/Security/authorization.plist". This query retrieves settings from the authorization plist file:
* label - item name, usually in reverse domain format
* modified - last modified timestamp
* allow_root - whether a right should be allowed automatically if the requesting process is running with uid of "0"
* timeout - the maximum age of a (cached/shared) credential accepted for this right
* version - right version
* tries - number of attempts application will try to obtain the rights
* authenticate_user - if user must be authenticated for this right
* shared - whether a credential generated on success is shared with other apps
* comment - optional right comment
* created - created timestamp
* class - right class: user, rule, evaluate-mechanisms, allow, deny
* session_owner: true/false

osQuery.label: macos_authorizations_monitoring
osQuery.name: Authorization Rights Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
label, modified,
allow_root, timeout, version, tries, authenticate_user, shared, comment,
created, class, session_owner
FROM authorizations
WHERE
label LIKE regex_match(label, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "label_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Authorization Rights Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieved the following data for system user groups:
* group name
* username
* group description

osQuery.label: user_groups_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Groups
osQuery.sql:
SELECT g.groupname, u.username, g.comment
FROM groups g
LEFT JOIN user_groups ug ON g.gid=ug.gid
LEFT JOIN users u ON ug.uid=u.uid;

queries.title: User Groups Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS and Linux. It retrieves data on rules for running commands as other users via sudo:
* source - source file containing the given rule
* header - symbol for given rule
* rule_details - rule definition

osQuery.label: macos_linux_sudoers_monitoring
osQuery.name: Sudoers Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
source, header, rule_details
FROM sudoers
WHERE
source LIKE regex_match(source, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "source_pattern"), 0) AND
header LIKE regex_match(header, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "header_pattern"), 0) AND
rule_details LIKE regex_match(rule_details, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "rule_details_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Sudoers Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. When a Plug and Play driver install is attempted, Windows will log the event with ID of 20001 including the status of the event inside the log. This could be useful when investigating malicious driver or USB on the system. The event log contains such details as device instance (driver, usb), device description, device name, installation status, driver provider, and event time. This event is triggered by any PnP-capable devices, including but not limited to USB, FireWire, and PCMCIA devices.

osQuery.label: windows_pnp_events_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows PnP Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
regex_match(data, '"DriverName":"([^"]+)', 1) as Driver_Name,
regex_match(data, '"DriverProvider":"([^"]+)', 1) as Driver_Provider,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "System" AND
eventid = 20001;

queries.title: Windows Plug And Play Event Logs Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. Table "xprotect_meta" lists browser plugins or extensions. This could be used to verify if a suspicious plugin or extension has been installed or not. The following data is retrieved: 
* identifier - browser plugin or extension identifier
* type - either plugin or extension
* developer_id - developer identity (SHA1) of extension
* min_version - the minimum allowed plugin version.

osQuery.label: macos_xprotect_meta_monitoring
osQuery.name: XProtect Browser Related Signatures
osQuery.sql:
SELECT identifier, type, developer_id, min_version
FROM xprotect_meta
WHERE
identifier LIKE regex_match(identifier, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "identifier_pattern"), 0) AND
type LIKE regex_match(type, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "type_value"), 0) AND
developer_id LIKE regex_match(developer_id, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "developer_id_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: XProtect Meta Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It can detect unsigned binaries having extensions '.exe', '.dll' or '.sys' in Windows system directory (C:\Windows\System32). This query will retrieve following:
* path - path of the unsigned binary

osQuery.label: windows_unsigned_binaries_in_system_directories
osQuery.name: windows_unsigned_binaries_in_system_directories
osQuery.sql:
SELECT path
FROM authenticode 
WHERE  (path LIKE 'C:\Windows\System32\%.dll' OR path LIKE 'C:\Windows\System32\%.exe' OR path LIKE 'C:\Windows\System32\%.sys')
AND result = 'missing';

queries.title: Unsigned Binaries in Windows System Directories

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs for new processes created on the host:
* datetime - system time at which the event occurred
* new process name
* parent process name
* data associated with the event

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_process_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
replace(regex_match(data, 'NewProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "\\", "\") AS new_process_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'ParentProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "\\", "\") AS parent_process_name, 
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4688 AND
new_process_name LIKE regex_match(new_process_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "new_process_name_pattern"), 0) AND
parent_process_name LIKE regex_match(parent_process_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "parent_process_name_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Process Windows Event Logs Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on Chocolatey packages installed on the host filtered by the specified package name, author or version.

osQuery.label: chocolatey_packages
osQuery.name: Chocolatey Packages
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version, summary, author, license, path
FROM chocolatey_packages
WHERE name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="package_name")
AND author LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="package_author")
AND version LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="package_version");

queries.title: Chocolatey Packages Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves program data based on the following parameters:
* program name
* program version
* program install location
* program install source
* program language
* program publisher
* program uninstall string
* program identifying number
* program installation date

osQuery.label: installed_programs_search
osQuery.name: Installed Programs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version, install_location, install_source, language, publisher, uninstall_string, identifying_number,
(substr(install_date,1,4) || '-' || substr(install_date,5,2) || '-' || substr(install_date,7,2)) as installation_date
FROM programs 
WHERE name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="program_name")
AND install_location LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="program_install_location")
AND publisher LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="program_publisher")
AND uninstall_string LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="program_uninstall_string")
AND installation_date BETWEEN (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="installed_from") AND (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="installed_to");

queries.title: Installed Programs Search
warnings: This query only returns data on programs where an installed data is available. Wildcard '%' does not match NULL column values. Please edit or remove appropriate conditions to include null values in results.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on the current registry size. The registry is created at system startup from a number of files on the hard drive. If these files become full of rubbish, the server startup time could be slower affecting the system performance. Data displayed are:
* CurrentSize - current physical size of the Windows registry
* MaximumSize - maximum size of the Windows registry
* ProposedSize - proposed size of the Windows registry
* Status - indicates the current status of the object
* Percentage of MaximumSize used

osQuery.label: registry_file_size_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry File Size
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
CurrentSize,
MaximumSize,
ProposedSize,
Status,
PRINTF("%.2f", ((CAST(CurrentSize AS REAL)/MaximumSize)*100)) AS PercentageUsed
FROM Win32_Registry;

queries.title: Registry File Size Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It scans binaries of running processes for strings indicating that a binary contain references to SysInternals suite:
* path - the path scanned
* matches - list of YARA matches
* count - number of YARA matches
* strings - matching strings
* tags - matching tags
* sha256 of the scanned binary

osQuery.label: windows_yara_pe_sysinternals
osQuery.name: Yara Scan Results
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
y.path, y.matches, y.count,
y.strings, y.sigrule, y.tags,
h.sha256
FROM yara y
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=y.path
WHERE
y.path IN (SELECT path FROM processes WHERE path!="" AND path LIKE "C:\Users\%") AND ( 
y.sigrule = 'rule pe_sysinternals_string : antianalysis { strings: $si0 = "sysinternals" ascii wide nocase $si1 = "tcpview.exe" ascii wide nocase  $si2 = "autoruns.exe" ascii wide nocase $si3 = "filmon.exe" ascii wide nocase $si4 = "regmon.exe" ascii wide nocase $si5 = "procexp.exe" ascii wide nocase $st1 = "tcpview" ascii wide nocase  $st2 = "autoruns" ascii wide nocase $st3 = "filmon" ascii wide nocase $st4 = "regmon" ascii wide nocase $st5 = "procexp" ascii wide nocase condition: any of ($si*) or 2 of ($st*) }' OR
y.sigrule = 'rule pe_sysinternals_autoruns { strings: $si0 = "SOFTWARE\\Sysinternals\\Autoruns" ascii wide nocase $sp1 = "AutoRuns Data (*.txt)" ascii wide nocase $sp2 = "\\autoruns.chm" ascii wide nocase condition: $si0 and 1 of ($sp*) }') AND
y.count != 0;

queries.title: YARA Scan: Binary Contains References to SysInternals

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows EventID "4624" indicates that the user successfully logged on the system. This log contains field "LogonType" which indicates the type of logon. The "LogonType" 8 or "NetworkCleartext" indicates that the user-credentials were passed as clear-text over a network. The built-in authentications agents all hash credentials before sending them across the network. This is an unusual event that normally does not happen inside the secure environment and should be investigated when observed.

osQuery.label: cleartext_rdp_authentication_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs For LogonType 8
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4624 AND
data LIKE '%"LogonType":"8"%';

queries.title: ClearText RDP Connection Monitoring

queries.description: This table is applicable to Windows. It scans a specified directory with a specified YARA rule.

osQuery.label: windows_yara_search
osQuery.name: YARA Scan Results
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
y.path,
y.matches,
y.count,
y.sigfile,
y.sigrule,
y.strings,
h.sha256
FROM yara y
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=y.path
WHERE
y.path LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern") AND
y.sigrule = (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "yara_sigrule");

default: rule pe_wmi_cmd : anomaly { strings: $wmi = /select (\*|.{1,20}) from win32_/ ascii wide nocase condition: $wmi }
parameters.name: yara_sigrule

queries.title: YARA Scan: Windows System Scanning

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on Internet Explorer (IE) extensions based on the pattern specified. Data retrieved are:
* name - extension display name 
* registry path - extension identifier
* version - version of the executable
* path - path to the executable

osQuery.label: internet_explorer_extensions_search
osQuery.name: Internet Explorer Extensions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT ie.name, ie.registry_path, ie.version, ie.path
FROM ie_extensions ie
WHERE ie.name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="ie_extension_name")
AND ie.path LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="ie_extension_path");

queries.title: Internet Explorer Extensions Search

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Malware could modify the computer name in registry to log its presence on the host or to break the connection with any machines sharing a directory on the system. This query returns all the entires for the computer name in the registry that should be monitored.

osQuery.label: registry_computer_name_modifications
osQuery.name: Registry Computer Name Modifications
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\controlset001\control\computername\%%" AND name LIKE "computername")
OR (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\ControlSet001\Services\tcpip\parameters" AND name LIKE "hostname");

queries.title: Registry Computer Name Modifications Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs with ID 1100 indicating that the Event Log service was shutdown. This could indicate that an attacker is trying to evade detection on the endpoint. These filelds will be displayed in the output:
 * datetime - system time at which the event occurred
 * subject_user_name - user who initialted the service shutdown
 * target_user_name - target user name where service was shutdown
 * target_domain_name - target domain name where service was shutdown
 * data - data associated with the event

osQuery.label: windows_eventlog_service_shutdown_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Log Service Shutdown Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_domain_name,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 1100;

queries.title: Windows Event Log Service Shutdown Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves system kernel panic logs from the host:
* path - location of log file
* time - formatted time of the event
* registers - a space delimited line of register:value pairs
* frame_backtrace - backtrace of the crashed module
* module_backtrace - modules appearing in the crashed module's backtrace
* dependencies - module dependencies existing in crashed module's backtrace
* name - process name corresponding to crashed thread
* os_version - version of the operating system
* kernel_version - version of the system kernel
* system_model - physical system model
* uptime - system uptime at kernel panic in nanoseconds
* last_loaded - last loaded module before panic
* last_unloaded - last unloaded module before panic

osQuery.label: macos_kernel_panics_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Kernel Panic Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, time, registers, frame_backtrace, module_backtrace, dependencies,
name, os_version, kernel_version, system_model, uptime,
last_loaded, last_unloaded
FROM kernel_panics
WHERE
time LIKE regex_match(time, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "time_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: System Kernel Panic Logs Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It parses shadow file content and retrieves next data:
* password_status - password status
* hash_alg - password hashing algorithm
* last_change - date of last password change (starting from UNIX epoch date)
* min - minimal number of days between password changes
* max - maximum number of days between password changes
* warning - number of days before password expires to warn user about it
* inactive - number of days after password expires until account is blocked
* expire - number of days since UNIX epoch date until account is disabled
* username

osQuery.label: linux_shadow_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shadow File Content
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
password_status, hash_alg, last_change, min, max, warning, inactive, expire, username
FROM
shadow
WHERE 
username LIKE regex_match(username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0) AND
password_status LIKE regex_match(password_status, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "password_status_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Shadow File Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves hidden local user accounts from the registry. Attackers could create such accounts in order to increase access to a host and to avoid appearing in the results of account listing using commands like "net users":
* user_name - hidden account name
* registry key path
* registry key name
* registry key data
* timestamp of the most recent registry write

osQuery.label: windows_registry_hidden_user_accounts
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
SPLIT(key, '\', 7) AS account_name,
path, name, data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SAM\SAM\Domains\Account\Users\Names\%" AND
account_name LIKE "%$" AND
account_name NOT IN ( "HomeGroupUser$" );

queries.title: Windows Registry Hidden User Accounts Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It helps to speed up the Log4j vulnerability investigation and remediation cycle by collecting next data.
* identify hosts where environmental variables indicate java installation, these hosts could be vulnerable to Log4j
* check running processes for java execution
* yara scan to find jar files that have a "JndiLookup.class" string and might be Log4j vulnerable. Note that YARA match field set to "0" indicates no match. Even if there is no match - located jar files should be check regardless.
* identify installed log4j or java programs
Comments:
* YARA does not scan files inside sub-directories of "C:\Program Files" and "C:\Program Files (x86)". Replacing "%" with "%%" to scan sub-directories will result in a slow query and should be used with caution.

osQuery.label: process_envars_classpath
osQuery.name: Process Environmental Variables
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, value
FROM (
SELECT name, value
FROM orbital_environment
UNION
SELECT name AS "name",
data AS "value"
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Environment") AS t
WHERE
value like "%java%";

osQuery.label: java_processes
osQuery.name: Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p2.name AS parent_process, p1.pid, p1.name, p1.path, p1.cmdline, p1.state, p1.uid 
FROM processes p1 
JOIN processes p2 ON p1.pid=p2.parent 
WHERE
p1.name like "%java%";

osQuery.label: yara_jar_scan_default_tomcat_directory
osQuery.name: YARA
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, matches, count, strings
FROM yara
WHERE
path IN (
SELECT 
path 
FROM file 
WHERE 
(directory LIKE "C:\Program Files\%" AND filename LIKE "%.jar") OR
(directory LIKE "C:\Program Files (x86)\%" AND filename LIKE "%.jar")
) AND
sigrule='rule suspicious_log4j_string { strings: $s1="JndiLookup.class" wide ascii nocase condition: $s1}';

osQuery.label: java_programs
osQuery.name: Programs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, version, install_location, install_source, install_date
FROM
programs
WHERE
(name LIKE "%log4j%" OR name LIKE "%java%");

queries.title: Windows Log4j Monitoring
warnings: This query can take some time to load the results due to YARA scan and file path collection. Limit the file path to speed up the query.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on last logged in user from the registry. This could be useful during the forensic investigation of a compromised host.

osQuery.label: registry_last_logged_on_user_monitoring
osQuery.name: Last Logged On User Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Authentication\LogonUI"
AND name LIKE "LastLoggedOnUser";

queries.title: Last Logged On User Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. When a file is double clicked on or started with ShellExecute then the registry is checked to see what executable actually runs. This query retrieves data about any files associated with executables in a temp directory or a location usually used for configuration data. These locations lack typical Windows security protections.

osQuery.label: low_privilege_file_associations
osQuery.name: Files Associated With Low Privilege Executables
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\%\shell\%\command"
AND (data LIKE "%TEMP%" OR data LIKE "%AppData%");

queries.title: Low Privilage File Associations

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Event log with ID "5145" is generated every time a network share object (file or folder) is accessed. This query retrieves such events from the host:
* datetime   
* subject_user_name - user that requested access to the resource
* subject_domain_name - domain name for the user that requested the access
* ip and port from which the access was requested
* share name
* share_local_path -  the full system (NTFS) path for accessed share 
* data associated with the event

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_network_access_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip,
regex_match(data, 'IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) AS port,
regex_match(data, 'ShareName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS share_name,
regex_match(data, 'ShareLocalPath":"([^"]+)', 1) AS share_local_path, 
data
FROM
windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 5145 AND
subject_user_name LIKE regex_match(subject_user_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "subject_user_name_pattern"), 0) AND
share_name LIKE regex_match(share_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "share_name_pattern"), 0) AND
ip LIKE regex_match(ip, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "ip_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Network Objects Access Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux and MacOS. It is designed to return data on devices that are actively plugged into the host system:
* usb_address - USB device used address
* usb_port - USB device used port
* vendor - USB device vendor string
* version - USB device version number
* model - USB device model string
* serial - USB device serial connection
* class - USB device class
* subclass - USB device subclass
* protocol - USB device protocol
* removable - if USB device is removable

osQuery.label: macos_linux_usb_devices_monitoring
osQuery.name: Plugged USB Devices Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
usb_address, usb_port, vendor, vendor_id,
version, model, model_id, serial,
class, subclass, protocol,
CASE removable WHEN "1" THEN "Device is removable" ELSE "Device is not removable" END "removable"
FROM usb_devices
WHERE
vendor LIKE regex_match(vendor, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "vendor_pattern"), 0) AND
model LIKE regex_match(model, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "model_pattern"), 0) AND
serial LIKE regex_match(serial, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "serial_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: USB Devices Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It searches for Windows Event Logs with ID "4625" indicating failed login events and with "Sub Status" code of "0xC0000072" indicating that the account trying to login was disabled. This could indicate malicious behaviour or that a formerly authorized account is trying to gain access to the system. All hosts where such Windows Logs are detected should be investigated.

osQuery.label: windows_events_disabled_account_usage_monitoring
osQuery.name: Disabled Accounts Usage Windows Events
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
regex_match(data, '"TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS DisabledUserName,
data AS Message,
datetime AS "Time Generated",
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4625 AND
data LIKE '%"SubStatus":"0xc0000072%';

queries.title: Disabled Accounts Usage Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. SSH is a protocol that allows authorized users to open remote shells on other computers and it is often used by the attackers for lateral movement. This query retrieves OpenSSH events from the host.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_openssh_operational
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'payload":"([^"]+)', 1) AS payload_data,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "OpenSSH/Operational" AND
eventid = 4;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: OpenSSH Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves information on all Windows Firewall port exceptions. The presence of unauthorized firewall port exceptions may indicate malicious activity.

osQuery.label: wd_firewall_open_ports
osQuery.name: Firewall Exception List
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT name as "Firewall Rule Name", 
regex_match(LOWER(data), ".*lport=([^|]+)\|.*", 1) AS "Open Local Port",
regex_match(LOWER(data), ".*rport=([^|]+)\|.*", 1) AS "Open Remote Port",
regex_match(LOWER(data), ".*lport2_10=([^|]+)\|.*", 1) AS "Open Local Port 2-10",
regex_match(LOWER(data), ".*rport2_10=([^|]+)\|.*", 1) AS "Open Remote Port 2-10",
regex_match(LOWER(data), ".*lport2_20=([^|]+)\|.*", 1) AS "Open Local Port 2-20"
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy\FirewallRules"
AND data LIKE "%active=true%" 
AND data LIKE "%action=allow%"
AND (data LIKE "%lport=%" OR data LIKE "%rport%=");

queries.title: Windows Defender Firewall Open Ports Monitoring
warnings: This does not include IP-HTTPS ports.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Some applications such as browsers are typically only launched using explorer.exe. It is therefore anomalous if the parent of such applications is not explorer.exe. While this is does not strictly indicate malicious or suspicious activity, it surfaces a possible anomaly that needs to be investigated further.

osQuery.label: parent_process_not_explorer_tmp
osQuery.name: Parent Process Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE parent_child AS 
SELECT child_processes.pid AS child_pid,
child_processes.name AS child_name,
parent_processes.pid AS parent_pid,
parent_processes.name AS parent_name 
FROM processes child_processes INNER JOIN processes parent_processes ON child_processes.parent=parent_processes.pid;

osQuery.label: parent_process_not_explorer
osQuery.name: Parent Process
osQuery.sql:
SELECT child_pid, child_name, parent_pid, parent_name 
FROM parent_child 
WHERE child_name IN ("cmd.exe", "powershell.exe", "notepad++.exe", "notepad.exe", "iexplore.exe", "firefox.exe", "chrome.exe") 
AND parent_name NOT LIKE "explorer.exe";

queries.title: Parent Process Not Explorer

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It monitors the registry to check if some options related to the configuration of Windows Defender have been disabled, which could indicate malicious activity. 
- DisablePrivacyMode prevents users, who are not administrators, from seeing the threat history data, which lists the items that Windows Defender detected as threats on the device.
- Notification_Supress, if disabled, stops notifications from Windows Defender from being displayed.
- AllowNonAdminFunctionality, if enabled, allows users who are do not have administrator privileges, to modify the settings of Windows Defender.

osQuery.label: windows_defender_user_experience_registry_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Defender User Experience Registry Configuration Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\UX Configuration"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\UX Configuration")
AND ((name IN ("DisablePrivacyMode", "Notification_Suppress") AND data = "1")
OR (name LIKE "AllowNonAdminFunctionality" AND data = "0"));

queries.title: Windows Defender User Experience Registry Configuration Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It can help in identifying exploit attempst on MSExchange Server by collecting following data:
* Indicators of compromise of CVE-2021-26855 using yara rules for CVE-2021-26855, CVE-2021-26858, CVE-2021-27065 and CVE-2022-41082
* Search for exceptions that can indicate CVE-2021-26857 attempt in Windows "Application" event logs under provider "MSExchange Unified Messaging". Users can also search these logs with custom query using regex match.

osQuery.label: cve_2021_26855
osQuery.name: CVE-2021-26855 MSExchange Server SSRF vulnerability
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, matches, count, strings
FROM yara
WHERE
path LIKE "C:\Program Files\Microsoft\Exchange Server\V15\Logging\HttpProxy\%.log" AND
sigrule='rule CVE_2021_26855_exploited { meta: author = "Talos" strings: $s1 = /Serverinfo~[^l].{1,100}\/.{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s2 = /Serverinfo~l[^o].{1,98}\/.{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s3 = /Serverinfo~lo[^c].{1,97}\/.{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s4 = /Serverinfo~loc[^a].{1,96}\/.{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s5 = /Serverinfo~loca[^l].{1,95}\/.{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s6 = /Serverinfo~.{1,100}\/[^a].{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s7 = /Serverinfo~.{1,100}\/a[^u].{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s8 = /Serverinfo~.{1,100}\/au[^t].{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s9 = /Serverinfo~.{1,100}\/aut[^o].{1,100}/ nocase ascii $s10 = /Serverinfo~.{1,100}\/auto[^d].{1,100}/ nocase ascii condition: any of them}' AND  
matches!="";

osQuery.label: cve_2021_26858
osQuery.name: CVE-2021-26858 MSExchange Server Post-Authentication Arbitrary File Write Vulnerability
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, matches, count, strings
FROM yara
WHERE
path LIKE "C:\Program Files\Microsoft\Exchange Server\V15\Logging\OABGeneratorLog\%.log" AND
sigrule='rule CVE_2021_26858_exploited { meta: author = "Talos" strings: $s1 = "Download failed and temporary file" nocase ascii condition: $s1}'  AND  
matches!="";

osQuery.label: cve_2021_27065
osQuery.name: CVE-2021-27065 MSExchange Server Post-Authentication Arbitrary File Write Vulnerability
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, matches, count, strings
FROM yara
WHERE
path LIKE "C:\Program Files\Microsoft\Exchange Server\V15\Logging\ECP\Server\%.log" AND
sigrule='rule CVE_2021_27065_exploited { meta: author = "Talos" strings: $s1 = /Set-OabVirtualDirectory/ nocase ascii condition: $s1}'  AND  
matches!="";

osQuery.label: cve_2021_26857
osQuery.name: CVE-2021-26857 MSExchange Server Deserialisation Vulnerability
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Application" AND
provider_name LIKE "%MSExchange%" AND
eventid  IN (1000, 1001, 1002)  AND
data LIKE regex_match(data, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="error_pattern"), 0);

osQuery.label: cve_2022_41082
osQuery.name: CVE-2022-41082 MSExchange Server SSRF vulnerability
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, matches, count, strings
FROM yara
WHERE
(path LIKE "C:\Program Files\Microsoft\Exchange Server\V15\Logging\HttpProxy\%.log" OR  path LIKE "C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\%%") AND
sigrule='rule CVE_2022_41082_exploited { meta: author = "Talos" strings: $s1 = /.*autodiscover\.json.*\@.*200/ nocase ascii condition: any of them }' AND  
matches!="";

queries.title: Windows MSExchange Server Vulnerabilities
warnings: This query can take some time to load the results due to YARA scans.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Protected Storage (PStore) is a Windows feature that provides applications with an interface to store user data that must be kept secure and free from modifications. This query retrieves Windows Event Logs indicating attempts to access PStore objects. Such events should be investigated for suspicious activity on the host:
* datetime the event was generated
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that requested access to network share object
* subject_domain_name - accounts's domain or computer name
* ip address from which access was performed
* TCP or UDP port which was used from remote or local machine to request the access
* share_name -  the name of accessed network share
* share_local_path - the NTFS path for accessed share
* relative_target_name - relative name of the accessed target file or folder
Note that events "5145" are not collected by default and the group policy "Detailed File Share" configuration is required for these events to be logged.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_protected_storage_access
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip_address,
regex_match(data, 'IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip_port,
regex_match(data, 'ShareName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS share_name,
regex_match(data, 'ShareLocalPath":"([^"]+)', 1) AS share_local_path,
regex_match(data, 'RelativeTargetName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS relative_target_name,  
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 5145 AND
relative_target_name LIKE "%protected_storage%";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Protected Storage Access

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves user preferences for various programs from the host:
* domain - application ID usually in com.name.product format
* key - preference top-level key
* subkey - intermediate key path, includes lists/dicts
* value - string value of most CF types
* if the value being forced/managed
* username - (optional) read preferences for a specific user
* host - "current" or "any" host, where "current" takes precedence

osQuery.label: macos_preferences_monitoring
osQuery.name: Preferences Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
domain, key, subkey, value,
CASE forced WHEN "1" THEN "True" ELSE "False" END "Value is Forced/Managed",
username, host
FROM preferences
WHERE
domain LIKE regex_match(domain, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "domain_pattern"), 0) AND
key LIKE regex_match(key, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "key_pattern"), 0) AND
subkey LIKE regex_match(subkey, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "subkey_pattern"), 0) AND
value LIKE regex_match(value, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "value_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Preferences Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The "User Shell Folders" or "Shell Folders" registry keys contain absolute paths to folders with applications that will be launched automatically on user logon. Any changes to these keys should be monitored. Malware may attempt to modify their values to point to the directories containing malicious executables to ensure persistence on the system. This query retrieves data related to "User Shell Folders" and "Shell Folders" registry keys.

osQuery.label: shell_folders_registry_key
osQuery.name: Shell Folders Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified 
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders"
OR key LIKE "%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\User Shell Folders";

queries.title: Shell Folders Registry Key Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs from the host with Event ID "4624" and logon types 10 and 3. Logon type 3 indicates network logon and it commonly appears when connecting to shared resources. Logon type 10 indicates a connection from a remote machine via Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP).
The following event log data is retrieved:
* remote IP
* remote port
* authentication package
* system time when the event occurred
* event data
* eventid
* provider name of the event
* event channel 
Search suggestions:
* Use "RemoteIP" to filter out logons from well known IP addresses.
* "AuthenticationPackage" parameter set to "NTLM" could indicate vulnerable hosts.

osQuery.label: remote_interactive_logon_event_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs for RemoteInteractive Logons
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
regex_match(data, '"IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) AS RemoteIP,
regex_match(data, '"IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) AS RemotePort,
regex_match(data, '"AuthenticationPackageName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS AuthenticationPackage,
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS LogFile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4624 AND
(data LIKE '%"LogonType":"3"%' OR data LIKE '%"LogonType":"10"%') AND
RemoteIP NOT IN ("::1", "-") AND
datetime LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern");

queries.title: Remote Interactive Logon Event Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the list of 32-bit applications from the registry that are excluded from data execution protection (DEP) designed to prevent certain types of malware from exploiting bugs in the system through the execution of code placed in the data page such as the stack:
* registry key path
* name - registry value name containing the full path of the executable
* registry data
* executable sha256 if the binary is still on disk
* last time the registry entry has been modified

osQuery.label: windows_registry_32bit_dep_exclusion
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
r.path,
r.name,
r.data,
h.sha256,
DATETIME(r.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry r
LEFT JOIN hash h ON r.name=h.path
WHERE
r.path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\appcompatflags\layers\%";

queries.title: Windows Registry 32-bit Applications with DEP exclusion

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows and MacOS. It retrieves data on certificates installed in Keychains/ca-bundles based on specified search parameters. The data retrieved include:
* common_name - certificate CommonName
* subject - certificate distinguished name
* issuer - certificate issuer distinguished name
* ca - 1 if CA: true (certificate is an authority) else 0
* self-signed - 1 if self-signed, else 0
* not_valid_before 
* not_valid_after
* sha1 - SHA1 hash of the raw certificate contents
* path - path to Keychain or PEM bundle
* serial - certificate serial number

osQuery.label: certificates_search
osQuery.name: Certificates
osQuery.sql:
SELECT common_name, subject, issuer, ca, self_signed, not_valid_before, not_valid_after, sha1, path, serial
FROM certificates 
WHERE issuer LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="cert_issuer")
AND ca LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="cert_authority")
AND self_signed LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="self_signed_value")
AND serial LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="cert_serial");

queries.title: Trusted CA Certificates Search

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Event with ID "4616" is generated when the process changed the system time. This event could indicate potential timestomping activities when generated by a not default process, and should be investigated:
* datetime - time when event was generated
* subject_user_sid - SID of account that requested the "change system time" operation
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that requested the "change system time" operation
* subject_domain_name - subject’s domain or computer name
* process_name - full path and the name of the executable for the process

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_system_time_changed
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_sid,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'ProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS process_name,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel="Security" AND
eventid=4616 AND
process_name NOT LIKE "%svchost.exe%" AND process_name NOT LIKE "%vmtoolsd.exe%"

queries.title: Windows Event Logs System Time Changed

queries.description:
This queries is applicable to Windows. It is designed to capture volatile and non-volatile data from the endpoint that can help with forensic investigations.
New queries:
* "Windows NT Domains"
* "Shim Cache"
* "Recently Opened Office Documents"
* "Windows ShellBags Content"
Removed queries:
* "Recent Files Data"
Updated queries:
* "Temp Directory Files"
* "SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes"
* "Shortcut Recent Files Data"
* "Listening Ports"
* "Network Profiles Registry Key"
* "USBSTOR Registry Key"
* "UserAssist Registry Key Monitoring"

osQuery.label: windows_nt_domains_search
osQuery.name: Windows NT Domains
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, client_site_name, dc_site_name, dns_forest_name, domain_controller_address, domain_controller_name, domain_name, status
FROM ntdomains;

osQuery.label: windows_bam_entries_monitoring
osQuery.name: Background Activity Monitor Entries
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, 
DATETIME(last_execution_time, "unixepoch") AS last_execution_time,
sid
FROM background_activities_moderator
ORDER BY last_execution_time DESC;

osQuery.label: hosts_file
osQuery.name: Hosts File Data

osQuery.label: sha256_hash_of_running_processes
osQuery.name: SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.pid, p.name, p.path, p.cmdline, p.state, p.uid AS owner_uid, u.username AS owner_username, h.sha256 
FROM processes p 
INNER JOIN hash h 
ON p.path=h.path
LEFT JOIN users u ON u.uid=p.uid;

osQuery.label: mapped_drives
osQuery.name: Mapped Drives

osQuery.label: interface_names_and_associated_ips
osQuery.name: Interface Names And Associated IPs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT interface, address, mask, type, friendly_name 
FROM interface_addresses 
WHERE interface NOT LIKE "lo%" 
AND interface!=1;

osQuery.label: system_network_state_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Network State
osQuery.sql:
SELECT disconnected, ipv4_no_traffic, ipv6_no_traffic, ipv4_subnet, ipv4_local_network, ipv4_internet, ipv6_subnet, ipv6_local_network, ipv6_internet
FROM connectivity;

osQuery.label: process_running_without_a_binary_on_disk
osQuery.name: Process Running Without A Binary On Disk

osQuery.label: shared_resources_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shared Resources
osQuery.sql:
SELECT description, install_date, status, allow_maximum, maximum_allowed, name, path, type
FROM shared_resources;

osQuery.label: application_shims
osQuery.name: Application Shims
osQuery.sql:
SELECT apps.executable, h.sha256, apps.path, apps.description, datetime(apps.install_time,"unixepoch") AS install_time, apps.sdb_id 
FROM appcompat_shims apps LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=apps.path;

osQuery.label: logon_sessions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Logon Sessions

osQuery.label: logged_in_users
osQuery.name: Logged In Users

osQuery.label: bitlocker_encryption_monitoring
osQuery.name: Bitlocker Status Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT device_id, drive_letter, persistent_volume_id, conversion_status, protection_status, encryption_method 
FROM bitlocker_info;

osQuery.label: powershell_history
osQuery.name: PowerShell History
osQuery.sql:
SELECT time, datetime, script_block_id, script_block_count, script_text, script_name, script_path
FROM orbital_powershell_events
ORDER BY datetime DESC
LIMIT 500;

osQuery.label: operating_system_attributes
osQuery.name: Operating System Attributes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version, major, minor, patch, build, platform, codename, install_date
FROM os_version;

osQuery.label: windows_executables_that_automatically_execute
osQuery.name: Windows executables that automatically execute
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT ae.name, ae.path, ae.source, h.sha256 
FROM autoexec ae LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path = ae.path;

osQuery.label: windows_av_products_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows AntiVirus Products Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
displayName, 
pathToSignedProductExe AS "Path To Signed Executable", 
pathToSignedReportingExe AS "Path To Signed Reporting Executable"
FROM AntiVirusProduct;

osQuery.label: windows_hotfixes
osQuery.name: Windows HotFixes

osQuery.label: listening_ports
osQuery.name: Listening Ports
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.path, lp.address, lp.protocol, lp.port 
FROM listening_ports lp LEFT JOIN processes p ON lp.pid = p.pid 
WHERE lp.port != 0 
AND lp.address!="127.0.0.1" 
AND lp.address!="::1" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\system32\%";

osQuery.label: startup_items
osQuery.name: Startup Items
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, args, source, type, status, username 
FROM startup_items;

osQuery.label: installed_programs_on_windows_host
osQuery.name: Installed Programs On Windows Host

osQuery.label: user_accounts
osQuery.name: User Accounts
osQuery.sql:
SELECT uid, gid, username, description, directory, shell, uuid, type 
FROM users;

osQuery.label: windows_prefetch_directory
osQuery.name: Windows Prefetch Directory
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.filename, f.path, f.uid, f.gid, 
datetime(f.atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
datetime(f.mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,
datetime(f.ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time, 
datetime(f.btime, "unixepoch") as create_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type, f.attributes, f.volume_serial, f.file_id 
FROM file f
WHERE f.path LIKE (SELECT value FROM orbital_environment WHERE name LIKE "windir") || "\Prefetch\%" 
AND f.size NOT LIKE "0";

osQuery.label: connected_networks_history_monitoring
osQuery.name: Network Profiles Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name,
data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\networklist\profiles\%"
AND name IN ("ProfileName", "DateCreated", "DateLastConnected", "Description", "Category");

osQuery.label: processes_with_network_connections
osQuery.name: Processes With Network Connections

osQuery.label: user_groups_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Groups
osQuery.sql:
SELECT g.groupname, u.username, g.comment
FROM groups g
LEFT JOIN user_groups ug ON g.gid=ug.gid
LEFT JOIN users u ON ug.uid=u.uid;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT module_id, sha256, module_path 
FROM loaded_module_hashes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_processes
osQuery.name: Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT pid, name AS process_name, path AS process_path 
FROM processes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_vs_processes
osQuery.name: Loaded Module ID To Process ID
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT lh.module_id, pmm.pid AS pid 
FROM process_memory_map pmm JOIN loaded_module_hashes lh ON lh.module_path=pmm.path;

osQuery.label: windows_connected_usb_monitoring
osQuery.name: USBSTOR Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
r2.data AS friendly_name, r1.name AS serial_number,
DATETIME(r2.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry r1
JOIN registry r2 ON r1.path=r2.key
WHERE
r1.key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR\%" AND
r2.name = "FriendlyName";

osQuery.label: scheduled_tasks_monitoring
osQuery.name: Scheduled Tasks
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, action, path, enabled, state, hidden, 
CASE
WHEN last_run_time LIKE "94%" THEN ""
ELSE DATETIME(last_run_time, "unixepoch") END last_run_time,
CASE
WHEN next_run_time < 0 THEN "" 
ELSE DATETIME(next_run_time, "unixepoch") END next_run_time, 
last_run_message, last_run_code
FROM scheduled_tasks;

osQuery.label: temp_directories_monitoring
osQuery.name: Temp Directory Files
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.path, f.filename, 
CASE f.filename
WHEN "." THEN ""
ELSE h.sha256
END sha256, 
f.uid, f.gid, f.mode, f.size, 
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time, 
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified, 
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time, 
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS creation_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type
FROM file f 
LEFT JOIN hash h ON f.path=h.path
WHERE 
f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\AppData\Local\Temp\%%" OR
f.path LIKE (SELECT value FROM orbital_environment WHERE name LIKE "windir") || "\Temp\%%";

osQuery.label: dns_cache_table_monitoring
osQuery.name: DNS Cache Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name AS "domain", type
FROM dns_cache;

osQuery.label: running_services_monitoring
osQuery.name: Running Services Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, service_type, display_name, status, pid, start_type, win32_exit_code, service_exit_code, path, module_path, description, user_account 
FROM services;

osQuery.label: trusted_root_certificates_monitoring
osQuery.name: Trusted Root Certificates Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT common_name, subject, issuer, ca, self_signed, 
DATETIME(not_valid_before, "unixepoch") as "Not Valid Before", DATETIME(not_valid_after, "unixepoch") as "Not Valid After",
sha1, serial, sid, username
FROM certificates
WHERE path LIKE "CurrentUser\Trusted Root Certification Authorities";

osQuery.label: chrome_extensions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Chrome Browser Extensions Monitoring

osQuery.label: windows_environment_variables_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Environment Variables Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, value
FROM
(SELECT name, value
FROM orbital_environment
UNION
SELECT name AS "name", data AS "value"
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Environment") AS t;

osQuery.label: userassist_monitoring
osQuery.name: UserAssist Registry Key Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
path, 
CASE last_execution_time
WHEN "0" THEN "0" 
ELSE DATETIME(last_execution_time, "unixepoch") 
END last_execution_time, 
count, 
sid
FROM userassist
ORDER BY last_execution_time DESC;

osQuery.label: wmi_cli_event_consumer_monitoring
osQuery.name: WMI CommandLineEventConsumer Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, command_line_template, executable_path, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_cli_event_consumers;

osQuery.label: wmi_event_filters_monitoring
osQuery.name: WMI Event Filters Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, query, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_event_filters;

osQuery.label: wmi_script_event_consumers_monitoring
osQuery.name: WMI ActiveScriptEventConsumer Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, scripting_engine, script_file_name, script_text, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_script_event_consumers;

osQuery.label: windows_shimcache_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shim Cache
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
entry,
path,
CASE modified_time
WHEN "0" THEN "0"
ELSE DATETIME(modified_time, "unixepoch")
END modified_time,
h.sha256
FROM shimcache 
LEFT JOIN hash h USING(path);

osQuery.label: windows_office_mru
osQuery.name: Recently Opened Office Documents
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
om.application, om.version, om.path,
DATETIME(om.last_opened_time, "unixepoch") AS last_opened_time,
om.sid, u.username, h.sha256
FROM
office_mru om
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=om.path
JOIN users u ON u.uuid=om.sid;

osQuery.label: windows_shellbags_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows ShellBags Content
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
u.username,
sid,
source,
path as directory_name,
DATETIME(modified_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_modified_time,
DATETIME(created_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_created_time,
DATETIME(accessed_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_accessed_time,
mft_entry,
mft_sequence
FROM shellbags
LEFT JOIN users u ON shellbags.sid=u.uuid;

queries.title: Forensic Snapshot Windows 0.0.9
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The SmartScreen filter in Microsoft Edge provides warning messages and blocks potentially malicious websites by default. Malware may attempt to disable this filter by setting the "PhishingFilter" registry key equal to 0. This query retrieves all hosts where the SmartScreen filter has been disabled.

osQuery.label: ie_phishing_filter_disabled_monitoring
osQuery.name: IE Phishing Filter Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\PhishingFilter"
AND name LIKE "EnabledV9"
AND data!="1";

queries.title: IE Phishing Filter Disabled Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. A shim is a small library which transparently intercepts an API, changes the parameters passed, handles the operation itself, or redirects the operation elsewhere. Shims can also be used for running programs on different software platforms than they were developed for. While shims normally serve a legitimate purpose, they can be used in a malicious manner such as argument replacement, shellcode injection, and others. This query returns information on shims installed on the system. Data retrieved are:
* executable - name of the executable that is being shimmed 
* sha256 - hash of the executable that is being shimmed 
* path - path to the SDB database
* description 
* installation time
* sdb_id - unique GUID of the SDB

osQuery.label: application_shims
osQuery.name: Application Shims
osQuery.sql:
SELECT apps.executable, h.sha256, apps.path, apps.description, datetime(apps.install_time,"unixepoch") AS install_time, apps.sdb_id 
FROM appcompat_shims apps LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=apps.path;

queries.title: Application Shims Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves the screenlock status for the current logged in user:
* "Password is Required After Sleep/Screensaver" - if a password is required after sleep or the screensaver begins
* grace_period - the amount of time in seconds the screen must be asleep or the screensaver on before a password is required on-wake. 0 = immediately; -1 = no password is required on-wake

osQuery.label: macos_screenlock_monitoring
osQuery.name: Screenlock Status
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
CASE enabled WHEN "1" THEN "True" ELSE "False" END "Password is Required After Sleep/Screensaver",
grace_period
FROM screenlock
WHERE
"Password is Required After Sleep/Screensaver" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "enabled_pattern");

queries.title: Screenlock Status Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves information on the current logon session based on the pattern specified: 
* logon id - a locally unique identifier (LUID) that identifies a logon session
* user - the account name of the security principal that owns the logon session
* logon domain - the name of the domain used to authenticate the owner of the logon session
* authentication package - the authentication package used to authenticate the owner of the logon session
* logon type - logon method
* logon sid - user SID
* logon time
* logon server - the name of the server used to authenticate the owner of the logon session
* dns domain name - the DNS name for the owner of the logon session
* upn - the user principal name (UPN) for the owner of the logon session
* logon script 
* home directory - the home directory for the logon session
* home directory drive - the drive location of the home directory of the logon session

osQuery.label: logon_sessions_search
osQuery.name: Logon Sessions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT logon_id, user, logon_domain, authentication_package, logon_type, logon_sid, datetime(logon_time, "unixepoch") as Logon_Time, logon_server, dns_domain_name, upn, logon_script, home_directory, home_directory_drive
FROM logon_sessions
WHERE user LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars where n="user_name")
AND logon_domain LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars where n="logon_domain_name")
AND logon_type LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars where n="logontype")
AND logon_sid LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars where n="logonsid")
AND Logon_Time LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars where n="logontime")
AND logon_server LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars where n="logon_server_name");

queries.title: Logon Sessions Search

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. The "CWDIllegalInDllSearch" registry entry lets the user modify the behaviour of the DLL search path algorithm in order to prevent search order hijacking. It can be set for all applications on the host, or for specific applications only. The settings for this registry key are listed below:
* 0xFFFFFFFF - removes the current working directory from the default DLL search order
* 0 - uses the default DLL search path. This entry should be investigated.
* 1 - blocks a DLL load from the current working directory if the current working directory is set to a WebDAV folder
* 2 - blocks a DLL load from the current working directory if the current working directory is set to a remote folder

osQuery.label: registry_cwd_illegal_in_dll_search_monitoring
osQuery.name: CWDIllegalInDllSearch Regisry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Control\Session Manager" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\%")
AND name="CWDIllegalInDllSearch";

techniques.T1574.008: Path Interception by Search Order Hijacking

queries.title: CWDIllegalInDllSearch Registry Entry Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Services Event Logs for specific Event IDs:
* 7034 - Service crashed unexpectedly
* 7035 - Service sent a Start/Stop control
* 7036 - Service started or stopped
* 7040 - Start type changed (Boot, On Request, Disabled)
* 7045 - A service was installed on the system (Win2008R2+)
* 4697 - A service was installed on the system (from Security log)

osQuery.label: windows_services_events_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Services Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
(channel = "System" AND eventid IN (7034, 7035, 7036, 7040, 7045)) OR
(channel = "Security" AND eventid = 4697) AND
datetime LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern");

queries.title: Windows Services Event Logs Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieves data on dynamically loaded objects, modules and libraries by executing processes.

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT module_id, sha256, module_path 
FROM loaded_module_hashes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_processes
osQuery.name: Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT pid, name AS process_name, path AS process_path 
FROM processes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_vs_processes
osQuery.name: Loaded Module ID To Process ID
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT lh.module_id, pmm.pid AS pid 
FROM process_memory_map pmm JOIN loaded_module_hashes lh ON lh.module_path=pmm.path;

queries.title: Loaded Modules Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows event with ID "4719" is generated when the computer's audit policy was changed. Adversaries may modify group policy in order to escalate privileges or to gain access to restricted resources. This query retrieves events generated when audit policy has been deactivated which might indicate suspicious activity:
* datetime the event was generated
* subject_user_sid - SID of account that made a change to local audit policy
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that made a change to local audit policy
* subject_domain_name - the account's domain or computer name
* full event log data

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_audit_policy_deactivation
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectUserSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_sid,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4719 AND
regex_match(data, '"AuditPolicyChanges":"([^"]+)', 1) LIKE "%8448%";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: Audit Policy Deactivation

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Events with ID "4769" are generated when a Kerberos service ticket was requested and could help to identify an unauthorized service ticket requests. This query retrieves such event logs. Note that the audit policy "Audit Kerberos Service Ticket Operations" must be enabled in the domain environment to log such events.
Query limitations:
* Status "0x0" means no errors were found and events with such status are filtered out by default. 
* Status "0x20" means that a ticket granting service has expired. These are informational messages that are not relevant to security and are filtered by default as well.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_kerberos_service_ticket_requests
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) as target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'ServiceName":"([^"]+)', 1) as service_name,
regex_match(data, 'IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) as ip_address,
regex_match(data, 'IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) as ip_port,
regex_match(data, 'Status":"([^"]+)', 1) as status,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE 
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4769 AND
status NOT IN ("0x0", "0x20");

queries.title: Windows Event Logs for Kerberos Service Ticket Request

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs with ID "4656" that are generated every time a handle to an object was requested. The object could be a file system, kernel, registry object, or a file system object on removable storage or a device. The Group Policy "Handle Manipulation" must be enabled on the host to log such events. Data displayed:
* datetime - system time at which the event occurred
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that requested a handle to an object
* subject_domain_name - subject’s domain or computer name
* object_type - the type of an object that was accessed during the operation
* object_name - name and other identifying information for the object for which access was requested
* process_name - full path and the name of the executable for the process through which the access was requested
* data associated with the event
Search suggestion:
* To find all processes that requested access to lsass.exe set "object_name_pattern" to ".*lsass\.exe.*". Process "WmiPrvSE.exe" accesses "lsass.exe" quite frequently, to reduce the output set "process_name_pattern" to "^((?!WmiPrvSE).)*$".

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_object_handles
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, '.*SubjectUserName":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, '.*SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, '.*ObjectType":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS object_type,
regex_match(data, '.*ObjectName":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS object_name,
regex_match(data, '.*ProcessName":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS process_name,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4656 AND
object_name LIKE regex_match(object_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "object_name_pattern"), 0) AND
process_name LIKE regex_match(process_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "process_name_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Object Handle Access

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. It retrieves data on system OS:
* distribution or product name
* OS version
* major release version
* minor release version
* optional patch release 
* OS build 
* OS platform 
* OS version codename 
* OS install date

osQuery.label: operating_system_attributes
osQuery.name: Operating System Attributes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version, major, minor, patch, build, platform, codename, install_date
FROM os_version;

queries.title: Operating System Attributes

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves all shared folders available to other systems via Server Message Block (SMB) or Apple Filing Protocol (AFP):
* the shared name of the folder as it appears to other users
* absolute path of shared folder on the local system

osQuery.label: macos_shared_folders_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shared Folders
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, path
FROM shared_folders
WHERE
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Shared Folders Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves information about installed event taps the system:
* "Process Being Tapped" - name of the process that being tapped
* "Tapping Process" - name of the process that created the event tap
* "Event Tap Status" - if event tap is enabled
* event_tap_id - unique ID for the tap
* event_tapped - the mask that identifies the set of events to be observed
Search suggestion:
* To list all active event taps on the host set parameter "enabled_pattern" to "%Enabled%"

osQuery.label: macos_event_taps_monitoring
osQuery.name: Installed Event Taps
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
p1.name AS "Process Being Tapped",
p2.name AS "Tapping Process",
CASE et.enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled " ELSE "Disabled" END "Event Tap Status",
et.event_tap_id,
et.event_tapped
FROM event_taps et
JOIN processes p1 ON p1.pid = et.process_being_tapped
JOIN processes p2 ON p2.pid = et.tapping_process
WHERE
"Process Being Tapped" LIKE regex_match(p1.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "process_being_tapped_pattern"), 0) AND 
"Tapping Process" LIKE regex_match(p2.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "tapping_process_pattern"), 0) AND
"Event Tap Status" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "enabled_pattern");

queries.title: Installed Event Taps Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Event Viewer application is designed to view and manage event logs on a local or remote system. The Event Viewer (eventvwr.exe) process runs at an elevated integrity level. When "eventvwr.exe" is launched, it tries to launch "eventvwr.msc". The default handler for ".mcs" files is "mmc.exe" and the file-association is defined in "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE" hive. However, this can be over-ridden by defining a new file association in the "HKEY_CURRENT_USER" hive which can be created by a low integrity process.
