In effect, a low integrity process can set this key and point to a malicious executable instead of "mmc.exe". As a result, whenever "eventvwr.exe" is launched, the malicious process will be launched at an elevated integrity. This technique can be used to bypass UAC and elevate privileges in addition to establishing persistence.

osQuery.label: uac_bypass_event_viewer_registry_key
osQuery.name: Event Viewer Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" 
OR key LIKE "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command";

queries.title: User Access Control Bypass Through Event Viewer

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Microsoft's old authentication protocols such as LM and NTLM use weak encryption schemes making Windows authentication vulnerable to packet sniffing and can lead to leaked credentials. The registry key "LMCompatibilityLevel" is used to set Authentication Level of the LAM Manager. All systems there this registry key is not set to 5 should be reviewed.

osQuery.label: weak_authentication_monitoring
osQuery.name: LM/NTLM Authentication Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified, 
CASE data 
WHEN "0" THEN "Send LM response and NTLM response. Never use NTLMv2 session security."
WHEN "1" THEN "Use NTLMv2 session security if negotiated."
WHEN "2" THEN "Send NTLM authentication only."
WHEN "3" THEN "Send NTLMv2 authentication only."
WHEN "4" THEN "Refuse LM authentication."
WHEN "5" THEN "Refuse LM and NTLM authentication. Accept only NTLMv2."
END "Authentication Level Description"
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Control\Lsa"
AND name LIKE "LMCompatibilityLevel"
AND data!=5;

queries.title: Weak Authentication Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. Gatekeeper is a MacOS security feature designed to prevent installation of malicious applications, specifically applications that are not signed by Apple or a developer enrolled in Apple's developer program. This query retrieves Gatekeeper details from the host:
* gatekeeper_status - if Gatekeeper is enabled
* version - version of gatekeeper's gke.bundle
* allow_execution_from_identified_developers - if Gatekeeper allows execution from identified developers
* opaque_version - version of Gatekeeper' gkopaque.bundle
Search suggestions:
* In a corporate environment is might be unusual to have Gatekeeper disabled on the system. To find all hosts where Gatekeeper is disabled set parameter "gatekeeper_status_pattern" to "Disabled".

osQuery.label: macos_gatekeeper_monitoring
osQuery.name: Gatekeeper Details
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
CASE assessments_enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" ELSE "Disabled" END "gatekeeper_status",
version,
CASE dev_id_enabled WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled" ELSE "Disabled" END "allow_execution_from_identified_developers",
opaque_version 
FROM gatekeeper 
WHERE
"gatekeeper_status" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "gatekeeper_status_pattern") AND
"allow_execution_from_identified_developers" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "dev_id_pattern");

queries.title: Gatekeeper Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Events with ID "4769" are generated when a Kerberos service ticket was requested and could help to identify service ticket requests where vulnerable cryptographic suite was used for issued tickets. This query retrieves low encryption tickets events.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_kerberos_ticket_low_encryption
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) as target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'ServiceName":"([^"]+)', 1) as service_name,
regex_match(data, 'IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) as ip_address,
regex_match(data, 'IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) as ip_port,
regex_match(data, 'Status":"([^"]+)', 1) as status,
CASE regex_match(data, 'TicketEncryptionType":"([^"]+)', 1)
WHEN "0x12" THEN "AES256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96"
WHEN "0x1" THEN "DES-CBC-CRC"
WHEN "0x3" THEN "DES-CBC-MD5"
WHEN "0x11" THEN "AES128-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96"
WHEN "0x17" THEN "RC4-HMAC"
WHEN "0x18" THEN "RC4-HMAC-EXP"
else "Unknown"
END ticket_encryption_type,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4769 AND
ticket_encryption_type NOT LIKE "AES256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: Kerberos Ticket with Low Encryption

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The "Recent" folder on Windows stores shortcuts to all recent files for the current user. This query retrieves data on entries stored inside this folder.

osQuery.label: windows_most_recent_files_monitoring
osQuery.name: Recent Files Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.path, f.filename, f.uid, f.gid, f.mode, f.size,
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time,
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified,
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time,
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS creation_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type
FROM file f
WHERE f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\%";

queries.title: Windows Recent Files Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It lists the hosts based on the uptime specified by the user.

osQuery.label: host_uptime_search
osQuery.name: Host Uptime
osQuery.sql:
SELECT days, hours, minutes, seconds
FROM uptime
WHERE days LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="host_uptime_days")
AND hours LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="host_uptime_hours")
AND minutes LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="host_uptime_minutes")
AND seconds LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="host_uptime_seconds");

queries.title: Host Uptime Search

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It determines whether the registry key controlling Windows Adobe Type Manager Library (ATMFD) has been reset. A workaround to avoid OpenType Font related vulnerabilities such as CVE-2016-7210, CVE-2015-2432, CVE-2015-2458 through CVE-2015-2462 on Windows 8 or higher, is to disable ATMFD through the registry. An attacker can try to undo this workaround by resetting the registry key value. Having this registry reset on a host is highly suspicious.

osQuery.label: opentype_font_driver_vulnerability
osQuery.name: OpenType Font Driver Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, type, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows\%" 
AND name = "DisableATMFD" 
AND data != "1";

queries.title: OpenType Font Driver Vulnerability

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. It retrieves:
* ProcessID
* Process Command Line
* Local IP Address
* Local Port
* Remote IP Address
* Remote Port

osQuery.label: processes_with_network_connections
osQuery.name: Processes With Network Connections
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT pos.pid, p.name, p.cmdline, pos.local_address, pos.local_port, pos.remote_address, pos.remote_port 
FROM processes p 
JOIN process_open_sockets pos USING (pid)
WHERE 
pos.remote_address NOT IN ("", "0.0.0.0", "127.0.0.1", "::", "::1", "0");

queries.title: Processes With Network Connections

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. DNS zone transfer is a process by which a DNS server passes a copy of part of its database to another DNS server. It maybe be performed by the attacker in order to get a list of all hosts for the particular domain and to find potential attack vectors. This query retrieves events from the host related to DNS zone transfer. Found events should be reviewed for suspicious IPs attempting to perform the zone transfer.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_dns_zone_transfer
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'param1":"([^"]+)', 1) AS parameter1,
regex_match(data, 'param2":"([^"]+)', 1) AS parameter2,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = 'DNS Server' AND
eventid = 6004 AND
parameter2 NOT LIKE "%localhost%";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: DNS Zone Transfer Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS) is a Windows component used to transfer files asynchronously between a client and a server. It is often used by adversaries to download, execute or clean up after running malicious code. This query retrieves Windows Events with ID "59" that are generated when BITS download started on the host:
* datetime - system time at which the event occurred
* bits job name
* bits download url
* event data
* process ID which emitted the event record
* process name if the process is still running 
* process command line if the process is still running 
* user ID for the user owning the process 
* username for the user owning the process

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_bits_transfer_job
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
datetime, 
regex_match(data, 'name":"([^"]+)', 1) AS bits_job_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'url":"([^"]+)', 1), "\/", "/") AS url,
data,
pid,
p.name,
p.cmdline, 
u.uid, 
u.username 
FROM windows_eventlog
LEFT JOIN processes p USING(pid)
LEFT JOIN users u ON u.uid=p.uid 
WHERE 
channel="Microsoft-Windows-Bits-Client/Operational" AND
eventid = 59 AND
bits_job_name LIKE regex_match(bits_job_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "bits_job_name_pattern"), 0) AND
url LIKE regex_match(url, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "url_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs BITS Transfer Jobs

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) provides a graphical interface to connect to another computer over a network connection and by default is set to use port 3389. Unsecure RDP services are often exploited by malicious actors for lateral movement, discovery, or persistence. Suspicious RDP activity can be hidden by changing the default port value. This query retrieves RDP port registry configuration where the default setting was changed. Retrieved data should be examined for suspicious RDP port values.

osQuery.label: registry_rdp_port_monitoring
osQuery.name: RDP Port Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\WinStations\RDP-Tcp"
AND name LIKE "PortNumber"
AND data != 3389;

queries.title: RDP Registry Port Configuration Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves data on application schemes and handlers on the host.
Retrieved data should be examined for: 
* handlers that are not OS default handlers
* handlers that are enabled but do not exist on the OS by default
* handlers that are not protected

osQuery.label: macos_app_schemes_monitoring
osQuery.name: Application Schemes And Handlers Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT scheme, handler,
CASE enabled WHEN "1" THEN "True" WHEN "0" THEN "False" END "Default OS Handler", 
CASE external WHEN "1" THEN "True" WHEN "0" THEN "False" END "Handler does not Exists on OS by Default",
CASE protected WHEN "1" THEN "True" WHEN "0" THEN "False" END "Handler Protected by OS"
FROM app_schemes
WHERE
scheme LIKE regex_match(scheme, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "scheme_pattern"), 0) AND
handler LIKE regex_match(handler, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "handler_pattern"), 0) AND
"Default OS Handler" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="enabled_by_default") AND 
"Handler does not Exists on OS by Default" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="installed_by_default") AND 
"Handler Protected by OS" LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="protected_handler");

queries.title: Application Schemes And Handlers Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. The main purpose of "ShellBags" on Windows is to improve user experience and remember preferences while browsing folders. However, "ShellBags" registry keys also contain information that is useful for forensic investigators helping to identify if a specific directory has been present on the host at some point in time and to connect specific user account to specific folder. This query retrieves data recorded under "ShellBags" registry hives from the host:
* username
* user SID
* source - source registry hive
* directory name
* directory modified time
* directory created time
* directory accessed time
* master file table (MFT) record number(entry)
* master file table (MFT) sequence

osQuery.label: windows_shellbags_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows ShellBags Content
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
u.username,
sid,
source,
path as directory_name,
DATETIME(modified_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_modified_time,
DATETIME(created_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_created_time,
DATETIME(accessed_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_accessed_time,
mft_entry,
mft_sequence
FROM shellbags
LEFT JOIN users u ON shellbags.sid=u.uuid
WHERE
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "directory_name_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows ShellBags Content

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves registry "Run" keys for applications that are installed in the "%LOCALAPPDATA%" and "%APPDATA%" directories. Since these directories do not require administrative privileges to be accessed, adversaries could replace legitimate binaries located in here with malicious code and use existing registry entries to establish persistence on the system.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_appdata_run_key
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, data,
datetime(registry.mtime, "unixepoch") AS registry_last_modified, 
regex_match(lower(data), ".*(c:\\.*)\.exe", 1) || ".exe" as binary, 
h.sha256,
datetime(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS file_last_modified
FROM registry
JOIN hash h ON h.path=binary
JOIN file f ON f.path=binary 
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" AND 
data like "%appdata%";

queries.title: Windows Registry AppData Run Key Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. Netscape Plugin Application Programming Interface (NPAPI) is an application programming interface (API) that allows browser plugins to be developed. NPAPI can work in Mozilla FireFox, Google Chrome, and Apple Safari, and is also cross platform. This query lists all C/NPAPI browser plugin details for all users on the host:
* uid - the local user ID that owns the plugin
* username 
* name - plugin display name
* identifier - plugin identifier
* version - plugin short version
* sdk - build SDK used to compile plugin
* description 
* development_region - plugin language-localization
* native - plugin requires native execution
* path - path to plugin bundle
* enabled - if plugin is enabled

osQuery.label: macos_browser_plugins_monitoring
osQuery.name: C/NPAPI Browser Plugins
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
bp.uid, u.username, bp.name, bp.identifier, bp.version, bp.sdk, bp.description, bp.development_region, bp.native, bp.path,
CASE bp.disabled
WHEN "1" THEN "False"
ELSE "True" END enabled
FROM browser_plugins bp
JOIN users u ON u.uid=bp.uid
WHERE
u.username LIKE regex_match(u.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0) AND
bp.name LIKE regex_match(bp.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
enabled LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "enabled_status");

queries.title: C/NPAPI Browser Plugins Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on shared resources on the system:
* description - a textual description of the object.
* install_date - indicates when the object was installed. Lack of a value does not indicate that the object is not installed.
* status - string that indicates the current status of the object.
* allow_maximum - number of concurrent users for this resource has been limited. If True, the value in the MaximumAllowed property is ignored.
* maximum_allowed - limit on the maximum number of users allowed to use this resource concurrently. The value is only valid if the AllowMaximum property is set to FALSE.
* name - alias given to a path set up as a share on a computer system running Windows.
* path - local path of the Windows share.
* type - type of resource being shared. Types include: disk drives, print queues, interprocess communications (IPC), and general devices.

osQuery.label: windows_open_shares
osQuery.name: Windows Open Shares

queries.title: Windows Open Shares Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It is used to monitor HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Classes\Folder\shell\open\command key which can be modified to execute a command or file when Windows' sdclt.exe is run. This technique can be used to establish persistence or to elevate privileges on the host.

osQuery.label: sdclt_bypass_registry_delegateexecute_key
osQuery.name: DelegateExecute Registry Key Modifications
osQuery.sql:
SELECT datetime(mtime, "unixepoch", "UTC") AS last_modified, key AS reg_key, path as reg_path, name, data 
FROM registry
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Classes\Folder\shell\open\command"
AND (name = "DelegateExecute" OR data != '')  ;

queries.title: UACBypass by Abusing DelegateExecute Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows and MacOS. It retrieves data on host platform information based on user specified parameters:
* code vendor, i.e. "VMware, Inc." 
* code version
* code update date in a format YY-MM-DD
* major and minor BIOS revision, i.e. "255.255"

osQuery.label: platform_information
osQuery.name: Platform Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT vendor, version, date, revision, address, size, volume_size, extra
FROM platform_info
WHERE vendor LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="code_vendor")
AND version LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="code_version")
AND date LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="code_update_date")
AND revision like (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="major_minor");

queries.title: Platform Information Search

queries.description: This query is applicable to MacOS and Linux. This query can reveal the environment variables alongwith processes which have attempted to bypass security controls using environment variables.

osQuery.label: macos_bypass_security_using_env_variables
osQuery.name: Security Bypass Using Environment Variables
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
key,
value,
p.pid,
p.path,
p.cmdline,
p.parent AS parent_pid,
pp.cmdline AS parent_cmd
FROM
processes p
LEFT JOIN process_envs pe ON p.pid = pe.pid
LEFT JOIN processes pp ON p.parent = pp.pid
WHERE
(
key = 'HISTFILE'
AND NOT VALUE LIKE '/Users/%/.%_history'
)
OR (
key = 'LD_PRELOAD'
AND NOT p.path LIKE '%/firefox'
AND NOT pe.value = 'libfakeroot.so'
AND NOT pe.value LIKE 'libmozsandbox.so%'
AND NOT pe.value LIKE ':/snap/%'
)
OR (
key = 'DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES' 
)
OR (
key = 'DYLD_FRAMEWORK_PATH' 
);

queries.title: Security Bypass Using Environment Variables Monitoring
warnings: This query is known to require a higher than average output time.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Scheduled tasks allow the user to automatically perform certain actions like running a program or a script on the system. Malware can use scheduled tasks to run malicious code and to establish persistence on the host. This query retrieves data on scheduled tasks that are set to execute regsvr32.exe which is highly unusual.

osQuery.label: windows_scheduled_tasks_regsvr32_execution
osQuery.name: Scheduled Tasks Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, action, path, enabled, state, hidden, 
DATETIME(last_run_time, "unixepoch") AS last_run_time, 
DATETIME(next_run_time, "unixepoch") AS next_run_time, 
last_run_message, last_run_code 
FROM scheduled_tasks 
WHERE 
action LIKE regex_match(LOWER(action), ".*regsvr32(\.exe)?.*", 0);

queries.title: Scheduled Tasks Executed Regsvr32.exe

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieves information about all modules loaded by a process whose name matches the specified pattern. Data retrieved:
* process_name
* process_loaded_module

osQuery.label: process_module_search
osQuery.name: Process Modules
osQuery.sql:
SELECT distinct p.name as process_name, pmm.path as process_loaded_module
FROM processes p 
LEFT JOIN process_memory_map pmm
ON p.pid=pmm.pid
WHERE process_name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="process");

queries.title: Process Module Search

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. DCSync is an attack that allows an attacker to simulate the behaviour of Domain Controller (DC) in order to retrieve password data via domain replication. Once an attacker has access to a privileged account with domain replication rights, the attacker can utilize protocols to mimic a domain controller. Windows Events with ID "5136" are generated every time an Active Directory object is modified. The "LDAP Display Name" value of "ntSecurityDescriptor" indicates which object attributes have been modified. The "ObjectValue" values of interest are:
* "1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2" DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All extended right
* "1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2" DS-Replication-Get-Changes extended right
* "89e95b76-444d-4c62-991a-0facbeda640c" DS-Replication-Get-Changes-In-Filtered-Set extended right
This query searches for such Windows Event Logs. System where these logs were found should be investigated for potential compromise since these events indicate DCSync rights have been granted to a standard user.

osQuery.label: windows_dcsync_granted_to_standard_user_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
data AS Message,
regex_match(data, '"AttributeValue":"([^"]+)', 1) AS Object_Value,
regex_match(data, '"AttributeLDAPDisplayName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS LDAP_Display_Name,
datetime AS TimeGenerated,
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 5136 AND
LDAP_Display_Name LIKE "%ntSecurityDescriptor%" AND (
data LIKE "%1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2%" AND 
data LIKE  "%1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2%" AND
data LIKE "%89e95b76-444d-4c62-991a-0facbeda640c%") AND
datetime LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern");

queries.title: DCSync Granted to Standard User Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. When the "DisableDomainCreds" registry key is set to 1, it permits Windows to cache credentials on the host. Passwords that are cached could be accessed by malicious software or actors. It is a recommended practice to disable this ability on the host when not needed. This query retrieves hosts where credentials caching is enabled. The group policy of such hosts should be reviewed to disable caching where possible.

osQuery.label: network_cred_storage_monitoring
osQuery.name: DisableDomainCreds Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, type, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Control\Lsa"
AND name LIKE "DisableDomainCreds"
AND data!=0;

queries.title: Network Credentials Storage Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. It searches for hosts where specific process, like "AMP for Endpoints", is not running which could indicate a problem inside the environment.

osQuery.label: windows_macos_linux_process_not_running
osQuery.name: System Info
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
hostname, uuid, cpu_type, cpu_brand, hardware_vendor, hardware_model, computer_name, local_hostname
FROM system_info
WHERE
NOT EXISTS (
SELECT
name 
FROM processes
WHERE 
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "process_name_pattern"), 0));

queries.title: Find Hosts Where Certain Process not Running

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves data on known hosts set up on the system:
* username of the local user that owns the known hosts file
* uid of the local user that owns the known hosts file
* key - parsed authorized keys line
* key_file - path to known_hosts file

osQuery.label: macos_linux_known_hosts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Known Hosts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
users.username, uid, key, key_file
FROM known_hosts
JOIN users USING(uid)
WHERE
users.username LIKE regex_match(users.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0) AND
key LIKE regex_match(key, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "key_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Known Hosts Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux and MacOS. It retrieves details on system mounted devices and filesystems. Data displayed:
* mounted device name
* mounted device alias
* mounted device path
* mounted device type
* block size in bytes
* device used blocks
* free blocks 
* available blocks 
* used inodes
* free inodes
* device flags

osQuery.label: macos_linux_mounts_monitoring.yaml
osQuery.name: System Mounted Devices and Filesystems Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT device, device_alias, path, type, blocks_size, blocks, blocks_free, blocks_available, inodes, inodes_free, flags
FROM mounts
WHERE
device LIKE regex_match(device, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "device_pattern"), 0) AND
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: System Mounted Devices And Filesystems Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. A security zone is a group of web sites with the same security level. This query retrieves user authentication settings for each zone configured on the host:
* path - path to the registry key from which the data collected
* security zone name
* security zone authentication 
* data - actual data stored in the registry key
* last_modified - time the registry key was last updated

osQuery.label: windows_registry_network_settings
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
path, 
CASE split(path, '\', 7)
WHEN "0" THEN "My Computer"
WHEN "1" THEN "Local Intranet Zone"
WHEN "2" THEN "Trusted sites Zone"
WHEN "3" THEN "Internet Zone"
WHEN "4" THEN "Restricted Sites Zone"
ELSE "Unknown"
END zone_name,
CASE
WHEN data LIKE "0" THEN "Automatically logon with current username and password."
when data like "65536" then "Prompt for user name and password."
when data like "131072" then "Automatic logon only in the Intranet zone."
when data like "196608" then "Anonymous logon."
when data like "" then ""
ELSE "Unknown"
END zone_authentication,
data, 
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings\Zones\%" AND
name LIKE "1A00";

queries.title: Windows Registry Network Settings

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs for "Account Locked Out" events and counts how many such events were registered on the host. Hosts where multiple account lockout events were detected over a short period of time should be investigated for possible attempts to brute force the account password.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_account_lockout_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_domain_name,
split(datetime, "T", 0) as day,
count(regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1)) AS lockout_count
FROM
windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4740
GROUP BY target_user_name, target_domain_name, day;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Account Lockout Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. A configuration file is used by Sysmon to store information about which events will be monitored. Clearing the rule entry in the registry will force Sysmon to reload the configuration file and could temporarily stop the logging of the events depending on how the configuration is being maintained.

osQuery.label: sysinternals_sysmon_configuration
osQuery.name: Sysinternals Sysmon Configuration
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, type, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") as last_modified 
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SysmonDrv\Parameters"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Services\SysmonDrv\Parameters"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet002\Services\SysmonDrv\Parameters";

queries.title: Sysinternals Sysmon Configuration Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. It retrieves names and IP addresses of network interfaces that are active.

osQuery.label: ip_address_of_active_interfaces
osQuery.name: IP Address of Active Interfaces
osQuery.sql:
SELECT interface_addresses.address, interface_addresses.interface 
FROM interface_addresses INNER JOIN 
interface_details ON interface_details.interface = interface_addresses.interface 
WHERE (interface_details.type = 6 OR interface_details.type = 71);

queries.title: IP Address of Active Interfaces

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It collects information related to pkexec binary modifications and settings to help identify hosts potentially vulnerable to CVE-2021-4034.
First query checks pkexec's permission bits. It is recommended to remove SUID bit from pkexec as a temporary mitigation for CVE-2021-4034 in case the path cannot be applied on the host:
* file path
* file permission bits
* last access time
* last modification time
* last status change time
* file creation time  
Second query checks for pkexec file events. On a patched system this file might be updated
* file path
* change action: update, remove, etc.
* file permission bits
* time of file event
Third query checks DEB packages for PolKit installations. All versions below "0.120" are vulnerable to CVE-2021-4034:
* package display name
* package supplied version
* package source
* package architecture
* package revision
* package status
* package maintainer

osQuery.label: linux_pkexec_permission_bits
osQuery.name: File Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, mode,
datetime(atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,
datetime(ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time,
datetime(btime, "unixepoch") as create_time
FROM file
WHERE 
path LIKE "/usr/bin/pkexec";

osQuery.label: linux_pkexec_file_events
osQuery.name: File Events
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
target_path, action, mode, time
FROM file_events
WHERE
target_path like "/usr/bin/pkexec";

osQuery.label: linux_deb_packages_polkit
osQuery.name: Dev Packages
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, version, source, 
arch, revision, status, maintainer
FROM deb_packages
WHERE
name LIKE "%polkit%";

queries.title: Linux pkexec Settings and Events Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The "Aedebug" key allows administrators to use the remote debugger in the event of the system crash. Malware may specify malicious code as a debugger which will be launched every time the system crashes. This query retrieves data related to the "Aedebug" registry key.

osQuery.label: aedebug_registry_key
osQuery.name: Aedebug Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\AeDebug"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432NODE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\AeDebug";

queries.title: Aedebug Registry Key Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. AutoRun is a feature of the Windows operating system. It automates the procedures for installing and configuring products designed for Windows-based platforms that are distributed on CD-ROMs. AutoPlay is a new feature introduced in Windows XP that detects content such as pictures, music, or video files on removable media and removable devices. AutoPlay then automatically launches applications to play or display that content. Both features could be abused by malware for lateral movement or initial access and its settings should be monitored. This query retrieves next settings for AutoRun and AutoPlay from the registry.
"NoDriveTypeAutoRun" values:
* 0xb5 (Recommended) - Sets bit value of "10110101", meaning AutoRun for CD/DVD, USB and network drives is turned off
"NoAutorun" values:
* 1 - disables AutoRun  
"DisableAutoplay" values:
* 1 - disables AutoPlay

osQuery.label: windows_registry_autorun_and_autoplay
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoDriveTypeAutoRun" OR
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoAutorun" OR
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\AutoplayHandlers\DisableAutoplay") AND
data!="";

queries.title: Registry AutoRun and AutoPlay Settings

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves data on Safari browser extensions installed on the host for all users:
* uid - the local user that owns the extension
* username - the local user that owns the extension 
* name - extension display name
* identifier - extension identifier
* version - extension long version
* update_url - extension-supplied update URI
* author - optional extension author
* path - path to extension XAR bundle
* description - ptional extension description text

osQuery.label: macos_safari_extensions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Safari Extension Details
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
se.uid, u.username, se.name, se.identifier, se.version, se.update_url, se.author, se.path, se.description 
FROM safari_extensions se
JOIN users u ON u.uid=se.uid
WHERE 
se.uid IN (SELECT uid FROM users) AND
u.username LIKE regex_match(u.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0) AND 
se.name LIKE regex_match(se.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
se.update_url LIKE regex_match(se.update_url, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "update_url_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Safari Addons Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Session Manager Subsystem (smss.exe) is executed during the startup process of Windows host and it launches anything present in the BootExecute key. Malware will often attempt to modify this registry value to gain persistence on the system. This query retrieves data stored in the BootExecute registry key.

osQuery.label: registry_key_bootexecute
osQuery.name: Registry Key BootExecute
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified 
FROM registry 
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet%\Control\Session Manager")
AND name LIKE "BootExecute";

queries.title: Registry Key BootExecute Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) is a vendor agnostic interface which can communicate with the endpoint in order to prevent execution of malware. Adversaties could abuse/bypass AMSI by registering a provider for persistence/defense evasion on the host. This query retrieves information on system AMSI providers:
* clsid - AMSI provider ID
* provider name
* provider module path
* user who owns the provider module
* user id
* provider module creation time
* provider module version
* provider module SHA256

osQuery.label: windows_registry_amsi_providers
osQuery.name: AMSI Providers Data
osQuery.sql:
WITH 
b1 AS (
SELECT
SPLIT(key, "\", 5) AS clsid,
data AS provider_name 
FROM registry 
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\AMSI\Providers\%%"),
b2 AS (
SELECT 
data AS module_path, 
SPLIT(key, "\", 4) as clsid 
FROM registry 
WHERE key IN (
SELECT 
"HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Classes\CLSID\" || SPLIT(key, "\", 5) || "\InProcServer32" AS clsid 
FROM registry
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\AMSI\Providers\%%") AND 
name = "(Default)") 
SELECT
b1.clsid, 
b1.provider_name, 
b2.module_path, 
u.username, 
f.uid,
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS file_creation_time, 
f.product_version, 
h.sha256 
FROM b1 
JOIN b2 USING(clsid) 
JOIN file f ON f.path=b2.module_path 
LEFT JOIN users u ON f.uid=u.uid 
JOIN hash h ON h.path=b2.module_path;

queries.title: Microsoft Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) Providers Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) is used to manage Windows systems both locally and remotely. It is often used by malicious actors for establishing persistence on the host. This query retrives data on WMI consumers with type "ActiveScriptEventConsumer" designed to run a predefined script for specific events: 
* name - Unique identifier for the event consumer.
* scripting_engine - Name of the scripting engine to use, for example, 'VBScript'. This property cannot be NULL.
* script_file_name - Name of the file from which the script text is read, intended as an alternative to specifying the text of the script in the ScriptText property.
* script_text - Text of the script that is expressed in a language known to the scripting engine. This property must be NULL if the ScriptFileName property is not NULL.
* class - The name of the class.
* relative_path - Relative path to the class or instance.

osQuery.label: wmi_script_event_consumers
osQuery.name: WMI Script Event Consumers
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, scripting_engine, script_file_name, script_text, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_script_event_consumers;

queries.title: WMI Script Event Consumers Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Word macros are a powerful way to automate common tasks in Microsoft Office but they are often used by malware for evasion, execution and persistence. Microsoft disables macros by default but users can easily turn them back on. This query retrieves hosts where registry key "BlockContentExecutionFromInternet" was set to 0 therefore enabling macro execution on the host or where registry key "EnableUnsafeClientMailRules" was set to 1 which enables rule actions for starting an application or running a macro.

osQuery.label: registry_office_security_monitoring
osQuery.name: Office Security Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Policies\Microsoft\office\%\%\security"
AND (name LIKE "EnableUnsafeClientMailRules" AND data IN ("1", "0x00000001")) 
OR (name LIKE "BlockContentExecutionFromInternet" AND data IN ("0", "0x00000000"));

queries.title: Microsoft Office Macros Registry Keys Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It can detect COM hijack attempts via TreatAs registry key manipulation. This query will retrieve following data:
  * last_modification_time - ScriptletURL update time
  * key - full path of ScriptletURL key
  * target_key - target class TreatAs path
  * target_data - target class data
  * ScriptletURL - (Default) string data in ScriptletURL key

osQuery.label: com_hijacking_attempts
osQuery.name: COM Hijack Attempts
osQuery.sql:
WITH b1 AS
(SELECT DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modification_time, key, SPLIT(path,"\",5) AS clsid, name, data AS ScriptletURL
FROM registry 
WHERE  key LIKE 'HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Classes\CLSID\%\ScriptletURL'),
b2 AS
(SELECT key, SPLIT(path,"\",5) AS orig_clsid, name, data AS clsid
FROM registry 
WHERE 
key LIKE 'HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Classes\CLSID\%\TreatAs')
Select b1.last_modification_time, b1.key, b2.key AS target_key, b2.clsid AS target_data, b1.ScriptletURL from b1 join b2 ON b1.clsid=b2.clsid;

queries.title: COM Hijacking via TreatAs Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. When a web browser encounters a URL whose protocol scheme is registered in "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Classes\Protocols" key, URL Moniker will create a COM object specified in the registry, pass in the URL provided, and begin to read data from the protocol handler object. Malware, such as Powerliks, registers the custom malicious protocol handler in order to establish persistence on the host. It is also possible to modify the existing protocol handlers registry configuration to execute malicious code.

osQuery.label: protocol_handlers_clsid_tmp
osQuery.name: Protocol Handlers CLSID Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE protocol_handlers_clsid AS
SELECT 
data AS protocol_clsid
FROM registry
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Classes\Protocols\%%" AND 
name="CLSID";

osQuery.label: protocol_handlers_keys_tmp
osQuery.name: Protocol Handlers Keys Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE protocol_handlers_keys AS
SELECT 
'HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\CLSID\' || protocol_clsid || '\InProcServer32' AS protocol_key
FROM protocol_handlers_clsid;

osQuery.label: windows_protocol_handlers_monitoring
osQuery.name: Protocol Handlers DLLs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT r.data AS protocol_dll, h.sha256 AS sha256
FROM registry r
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=r.data
WHERE 
key IN (SELECT protocol_key FROM protocol_handlers_keys) AND 
name="(Default)";

queries.title: Windows Protocol Handlers Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It is designed to retrieve information about installations of the trusted developer utilities "WinDbg.exe", "cbd.exe", "tracker.exe", and "MSBuild.exe". Retrieved information includes:
    * path - absolute file path
    * uid - owning user ID
    * gid - owning group ID
    * size - size of the file in bytes
    * atime - time of last access
    * mtime - time of last modification
    * ctime - time of last status change
    * btime - birth or create time
    * hard_links - number of hard links
    * symlinks - will be 1 if the path is a symlink, 0 otherwise
    * attributes - file attribute string, R=Read-only, A=Archive, S=System, H=Hidden
    * file_id
    * sha256
    These utilities may not be necessary within a given environment and should be removed if not used.

osQuery.label: trusted_developer_utilities
osQuery.name: Trusted Developer Utilities
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.path, f.uid, f.gid, f.size,
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") as create_time, 
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time,
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.attributes, f.file_id, h.sha256
FROM file f 
LEFT JOIN hash h on h.path = f.path 
WHERE ((f.path LIKE "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\%\%\MSBuild\%%" AND (f.filename LIKE "MSBuild.exe" OR f.filename LIKE "tracker.exe"))
OR (f.path LIKE "C:\Program Files (x86)\Windows Kits\%\Debuggers\%%" AND (f.filename LIKE "WinDbg.exe" OR f.filename LIKE "cdb.exe")));

queries.title: Trusted Developer Utilities
warnings: This query only searches the default install path
This query may be slow to return results due to SHA256 hashing and file path searching

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Events with ID "5136" are generated every time an Active Directory object is modified. An attacker may attempt to add a Service Principal Name (SPN) to the user account in order to retrieve the user password hash for offline analysis:
* datetime - timestamp the event was generated
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that requested the "modify object" operation
* subject_domain_name - account's domain or computer name
* object_dn - distinguished name of the object that was modified
* object_class - class of the object that was modified
* attribute_LDAP_display_name - the object attribute that was modified
* attribute_value - the value which was added
* data - full event data  
Note that to collect "5136" events the group policy "Directory Service Changes" must be configured. These events are not collected by default on Domain Controllers.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_spn_set_on_user_account
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectDN":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_dn,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectClass":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_class,
regex_match(data, 'AttributeLDAPDisplayName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS attribute_LDAP_display_name,
regex_match(data, 'AttributeValue":"([^"]+)', 1) AS attribute_value,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 5163 AND
attribute_LDAP_display_name LIKE "%serviceprincipalname%" AND
object_class LIKE "user";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: SPN Set on User Account

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Important Windows services such as svchost.exe that come with Windows OS installation are expected to run from specific directories such as "%windir%\system32" and "%windir%\syswow64". Malware often attempts to disguise itself by using names of legitimate windows process for malicious files. One way of detecting it is to check where the suspicious executable is running from.

osQuery.label: process_not_launched_from_default_location_syswow64
osQuery.name: Process Default Location
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.path, p.pid, p.parent, p.cmdline 
FROM processes p 
WHERE p.name IN ("dllhost.exe", "svchost.exe") 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\system32\%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\syswow64\%" 
AND p.path!="";

queries.title: Process Not Launched From Default Location Syswow64

queries.description: This query is applicable to Mac OS. It offers the capability to view display information of the screens currently in use by the computer at a point in time.

osQuery.label: connected_display
osQuery.name: Connected Display Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, vendor_id, serial_number, pixels, resolution, connection_type, display_type
FROM connected_displays;

queries.title: Connected Display Info

below591.label: connected_display
below591.name: Connected Display Information
below591.sql: SELECT 'ALERT: Your current version of OSQuery does not support this query.' AS name, 'N/A' AS vendor_id, 'N/A' AS serial_number, 'N/A' AS pixels, 'N/A' AS resolution, 'N/A' AS connection_type, 'N/A' AS display_type;

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on all services running on the host.

osQuery.label: running_services_search
osQuery.name: Running Services Data

queries.title: Running Services Search
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. Systemd is a system and service manager for Linux distributions that manages many aspects of the host, from services to mounted devices and system states. Systemd unit files define which binaries/commands systemd will run and are often used by malicious actors for persistence. This query retrieves information on systemd units currently in use from the host:
* id - unique unit identifier
* description - unit description
* load_state - reflects whether the unit definition was properly loaded
* active_state - the high-level unit activation state, i.e. generalization of SUB
* sub_state - the low-level unit activation state, values depend on unit type
* following - the name of another unit that this unit follows in state
* object_path - the object path for this unit
* job_id - next queued job id
* job_type - job type
* job_path - the object path for the job
* fragment_path - the unit file path this unit was read from, if there is any
* user - the configured user, if any
* source_path - path to the (possibly generated) unit configuration file

osQuery.label: linux_systemd_units_monitoring
osQuery.name: Systemd Units
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
id, description, load_state, active_state, sub_state, following,
object_path, job_id, job_type, job_path,
fragment_path, user, source_path
FROM
systemd_units
WHERE
id LIKE regex_match(id, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "id_pattern"), 0) AND 
fragment_path LIKE regex_match(fragment_path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "fragment_path_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Systemd Units Monitoring
warnings: This query only retrieves data for systemd services executing at the system-level. It does not include systemd services executing in a single user's context.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs generated when local, global or universal security group was created:
* datetime - time the event was generated
* eventid
* target_user_name - the name of the group that was created
* target_domain_name - domain or computer name of the created group
* target_sid - SID of created group
* subject_user_sid - SID of account that requested the "create group" operation
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that requested the "create group" operation
* subject_domain_name - subject's domain or computer name
* sid_history - contains previous SIDs used for the object if the object was moved from another domain
* data - event data

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_security_enabled_global_group_created
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs Global Group Created
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
eventid,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_sid,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_sid,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'SidHistory":"([^"]+)', 1) AS sid_history,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid IN (4754, 4727, 4731);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Security Enabled Group Created

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves data on all Homebrew packages installed on the system:
* name - package name
* path - package intall path
* version - current "linked" version

osQuery.label: homebrew_packages_monitoring
osQuery.name: Installed Homebrew Packages
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, version 
FROM homebrew_packages
WHERE 
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="package_name"), 0) AND 
version LIKE regex_match(version, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="package_version"), 0);

queries.title: Homebrew Packages Monitoring

queries.description: This queries is applicable to Linux. It is designed to capture volatile and non-volatile data from the endpoint that can help with forensic investigations.

osQuery.label: hosts_file
osQuery.name: Hosts File Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
address, hostnames 
FROM etc_hosts 
WHERE 
hostnames NOT IN ("localhost", "::1", "fe00::0", "ff00::0", "ff02::1", "ff02::2");

osQuery.label: sha256_hash_of_running_processes
osQuery.name: SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.pid, p.name, p.path, p.cmdline, p.state,  h.sha256 
FROM processes p 
INNER JOIN hash h 
ON p.path=h.path;

osQuery.label: interface_names_and_associated_ips
osQuery.name: Interface Names And Associated IPs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
interface, address, mask, type, friendly_name 
FROM interface_addresses 
WHERE 
interface NOT LIKE "lo%" AND 
interface!=1;

osQuery.label: process_running_without_a_binary_on_disk
osQuery.name: Process Running Without A Binary On Disk
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
pid, name, path, cmdline, state 
FROM processes 
WHERE 
on_disk=0;

osQuery.label: logged_in_users
osQuery.name: Logged In Users
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
user, type as "login_type", tty as "device_name", host as "remote_hostname", DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") AS time, pid, sid  
FROM logged_in_users
WHERE 
user!="";

osQuery.label: disk_encryption_monitoring
osQuery.name: Disk Encryption Status and Details
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
de.name, de.uuid, de.encrypted, de.type, u.username, de.user_uuid, de.encryption_status 
FROM disk_encryption de
LEFT JOIN users u 
ON de.uid=u.uid;

osQuery.label: operating_system_attributes
osQuery.name: Operating System Attributes

osQuery.label: listening_ports
osQuery.name: Listening Ports
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.name, p.path, lp.address, lp.protocol, lp.port 
FROM listening_ports lp LEFT JOIN processes p ON lp.pid = p.pid 
WHERE 
lp.port != 0 
AND lp.address!="127.0.0.1" 
AND lp.address!="::1" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/libexec/%";

osQuery.label: user_accounts
osQuery.name: User Accounts

osQuery.label: usb_devices
osQuery.name: USB Devices

osQuery.label: processes_with_network_connections
osQuery.name: Processes With Network Connections
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT pos.pid, p.name, p.cmdline, pos.local_address, pos.local_port, pos.remote_address, pos.remote_port 
FROM processes p 
JOIN process_open_sockets pos USING (pid)
WHERE 
pos.remote_address NOT IN ("", "0.0.0.0", "127.0.0.1", "::", "::1", "0");

osQuery.label: user_groups_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Groups
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
g.groupname, u.username, g.comment
FROM groups g
LEFT JOIN user_groups ug ON g.gid=ug.gid
LEFT JOIN users u ON ug.uid=u.uid;

osQuery.label: mounted_devices_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Mounted Devices and Filesystems

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE loaded_modules AS 
SELECT 
DISTINCT(path) 
FROM process_memory_map;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE loaded_module_hashes AS 
SELECT 
d.rowid AS module_id, h.sha256 AS sha256, d.path AS module_path 
FROM hash h 
INNER JOIN loaded_modules d ON d.path=h.path;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_processes
osQuery.name: Processes

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_vs_processes
osQuery.name: Loaded Module ID To Process ID
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT lh.module_id, pmm.pid AS pid 
FROM process_memory_map pmm 
JOIN loaded_module_hashes lh ON lh.module_path=pmm.path;

osQuery.label: arp_cache
osQuery.name: ARP Cache
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
address, mac, interface, permanent
FROM
arp_cache;

osQuery.label: chrome_extensions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Chrome Browser Extensions Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, ce.name, ce.identifier, ce.version, ce.description, ce.locale, ce.update_url, ce.persistent, ce.path
FROM users u
CROSS JOIN chrome_extensions ce USING (uid);

osQuery.label: crontab
osQuery.name: Crontab
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
event, minute, hour, day_of_month, month, day_of_week,
command, path
FROM
crontab;

osQuery.label: macos_linux_dns_resolvers_monitoring
osQuery.name: DNS Resolvers

osQuery.label: etc_services
osQuery.name: Services File
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, port, protocol, aliases, comment
FROM
etc_services;

osQuery.label: etc_protocols
osQuery.name: Protocols File
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, number, alias, comment
FROM
etc_protocols;

osQuery.label: linux_iptables_monitoring
osQuery.name: IP Tables Configuration Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
filter_name, chain, policy, target, protocol, 
src_ip AS "source ip", src_mask AS "source mask", src_port AS "source port", 
dst_ip AS "destination ip", dst_mask AS "destination mask", dst_port AS "destination port",
iniface AS "input interface",
outiface AS "output interface",
match AS "matching rule",
packets, bytes
FROM iptables;

osQuery.label: linux_kernel_modules_monitoring
osQuery.name: Kernel Modules

osQuery.label: macos_linux_known_hosts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Known Hosts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
u.username, kn.uid, kn.key, kn.key_file
FROM users u
JOIN known_hosts kn USING(uid);

osQuery.label: macos_linux_last_logins_logouts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Logins/Logouts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
l.username, l.tty, l.pid,
p.name AS "process name",
l.type,
DATETIME(l.time, "unixepoch") AS "entry timestamp",
l.host as hostname
FROM last l
LEFT JOIN processes p ON p.pid = l.pid;

osQuery.label: platform_information
osQuery.name: Platform Information

osQuery.label: macos_linux_process_envs_monitoring
osQuery.name: Process Environment Variables Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
p.name AS "Process Name", p.pid,
pe.key AS "Environment Variable Name",
pe.value AS "Environment Variable Value"
FROM process_envs pe
JOIN processes p ON p.pid=pe.pid;

osQuery.label: shadow
osQuery.name: Shadow File
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
password_status, hash_alg, last_change,
min, max, warning, inactive,
expire, username 
FROM shadow;

osQuery.label: shell_history
osQuery.name: Shell History
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, sh.time, sh.command, sh.history_file
FROM users u
CROSS JOIN shell_history sh USING (uid);

osQuery.label: ssh_configs_monitoring
osQuery.name: SSH Configuration Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, sc.block, sc.option, sc.ssh_config_file, h.sha256
FROM users u
JOIN ssh_configs sc USING(uid)
JOIN hash h ON h.path = sc.ssh_config_file;

osQuery.label: startup_items
osQuery.name: Startup Items

osQuery.label: macos_linux_sudoers_monitoring
osQuery.name: Sudoers Data

osQuery.label: macos_linux_writable_suid_bin_monitoring
osQuery.name: Writable Suid Binaries Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.path, sb.username, sb.groupname, f.mode, sb.permissions
FROM suid_bin sb
LEFT JOIN file f ON sb.path = f.path
WHERE
sb.permissions LIKE regex_match(sb.permissions, "(S|G)", 0) AND
f.mode LIKE regex_match(f.mode, ".*(2|3|6|7)$", 0);

osQuery.label: system_information
osQuery.name: System Information

osQuery.label: ssh_user_keys_monitoring
osQuery.name: User SSH Keys Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, usk.path, usk.encrypted, sha256
FROM users u
JOIN user_ssh_keys usk
USING (uid)
JOIN hash h ON h.path=usk.path;

osQuery.label: linux_systemd_units_monitoring
osQuery.name: Systemd Units
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
id, description, load_state, active_state, sub_state, following,
object_path, job_id, job_type, job_path,
fragment_path, user, source_path
FROM
systemd_units;

queries.title: Forensic Snapshot Linux 0.0.9
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves information from plist files on the host:
* key - preference top-level key
* subkey - intermediate key path, includes lists/dicts
* value - string value of most CF types
* path - (required) read preferences from a plist
Search suggestion:
To retrieve all entries for the root login preference update the query to show the settings for plists located in the next directories:
* "/Library/Preferences/com.apple.loginwindow.plist"
* "/Library/Preferences/loginwindow.plist"
* "/Users/%/Library/Preferences/com.apple.loginwindow.plist"
* "/Users/%/Library/Preferences/ByHost/com.apple.loginwindow.%.plist"
To check the Event Monitor Daemon (emond) property list for configuration changes set path to:
* "/System/Library/LaunchDaemons/com.apple.emond.plist"

osQuery.label: macos_plist_monitoring
osQuery.name: Plist Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
key, subkey, value, path 
FROM plist 
WHERE
key LIKE regex_match(key, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "key_pattern"), 0) AND
path LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern");

queries.title: Plist Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The "SafeDLLSearch" registry key forces the system to search the "%systemroot%" directory before searching the current directory or the rest of the path. This prevents "search order hijacking", where a malicious author abuses the order in which Windows searches for programs that are not given a path. This query retrieves the "SafeDLLSearch" registry key data. Systems should be investigated when this registry key is set to "0" or when the registry key is not present on the host but the group policy configuration for "SafeDLLSearch" is enabled.

osQuery.label: registry_safe_dll_search_monitoring
osQuery.name: SafeDLLSearch Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\%ControlSet%\Control\Session Manager" 
AND name="SafeDllSearchMode";

techniques.T1574.008: Path Interception by Search Order Hijacking

queries.title: Safe DLL Search Disabled

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) is a tracing facility designed to log kernel or application-defined events to a log file. Malware can attempt to disable ETW by modifying its settings in the registry in order to avoid detection on the host. This query collects registry data from the hosts where ETW event providers are disabled or failed to start.

osQuery.label: global_logger_event_tracing_session_disabled
osQuery.name: Global Logger Event Tracing Session Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\WMI\Autologger\%\%"
AND ( 
(name="Enabled" AND  data!="1") OR
(name = "Status" AND data!="0"));

queries.title: Global Logger Event Tracing Session Disabled
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves NT domain information on a Windows host:
* name - the label by which the object is known
* client_site_name - the name of the site where the domain controller is configured
* dc_site_name - the name of the site where the domain controller is located
* dns_forest_name - the name of the root of the DNS tree
* domain_controller_address - domain controller IP 
* domain_controller_name - the name of the discovered domain controller
* domain_name   
* status

osQuery.label: windows_nt_domains_search
osQuery.name: Windows NT Domains
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, client_site_name, dc_site_name, dns_forest_name, domain_controller_address, domain_controller_name, domain_name, status
FROM ntdomains
WHERE name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="host_name")
AND client_site_name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="dc_config_site")
AND dc_site_name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="dc_location_site")
AND dns_forest_name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="dns_root_tree")
AND domain_controller_address LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="dc_ip")
AND domain_controller_name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="dc_name")
AND domain_name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="domain")
AND status LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="domain_status");

queries.title: Windows NT Domains Search

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows event with ID "5007" is generated when Microsoft Defender Configuration has changed. Adversaries may disable or modify security tools settings to avoid possible detection. All events returned by the query should be reviewed for suspicious activity.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_defender_configuration_changed
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
datetime, 
regex_match(data, '"Old Value":"([^"]+)', 1) AS old_value, 
regex_match(data, '"New Value":"([^"]+)', 1) AS new_value, 
data 
FROM windows_eventlog 
WHERE
channel = "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender/Operational" AND 
eventid = 5007 AND
old_value LIKE regex_match(old_value, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "old_value_pattern"), 0) AND
new_value LIKE regex_match(new_value, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "new_value_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: Windows Defender Configuration Changes
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves accounts that are used for logging into MS Office from registry. Retrieved information should be reviewed for unusual entries that do not belong to the organization and could be used for data exfiltration.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_ms_office_accounts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
SPLIT(path,'\',1) AS user_sid,
data AS email_address,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE 
path IN (
SELECT path || '\EmailAddress' 
FROM registry 
WHERE key IN (
SELECT path || '\Common\Identity\Identities' AS reg_path 
FROM registry 
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Office" AND 
name LIKE regex_match(name, '[0-9]*\.?[0-9]*', 0))) AND
email_address LIKE regex_match(email_address, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "email_address_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Registry MS Office Accounts Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows event with ID "5136" is generated when an Active Directory object is modified. This query searches for "5136" events where a computer account updated organizational unit (OU) permissions - this activity is highly unusual and could indicate an attacker attempting to access protected files through OU permissions.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_permission_change_on_ou_by_computer
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectUserSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_sid,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, '"ObjectClass":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_class,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 5136 AND
object_class LIKE "organizationalUnit" AND
subject_user_name LIKE "%$";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: Permission Change on Organizational Unit by Computer

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Events generated by application errors and crashes.

osQuery.label: windows_bugcheck_events_monitoring
osQuery.name: Application Errors and Crashes Windows Events Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS TimeGenerated,
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
channel AS Logfile,
provider_name AS SourceName,
"N/A" AS Type,
"N/A" AS TimeWritten
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "System" AND 
eventid IN (41, 1001);

queries.title: Application Errors and Crashes Windows Events Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It can detect installed versions of 3CX Desktop Apps and their vulnerability status.

osQuery.label: 3cx_desktop_app_compromise_monitoring
osQuery.name: 3CX Desktop App Version Status
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version,
CASE
WHEN  version < "18.12.422" THEN "Vulnerable"
ELSE "Not Vulnerable"
END "3CX Desktop App Version Status" FROM programs
WHERE name LIKE "%3CX%" ;

queries.title: 3CX Desktop App Vulnerable Versions Monitoring

darwin.label: 3cx_desktop_app_compromise_monitoring
darwin.name: 3CX Desktop App Version Status
darwin.sql:
SELECT name, bundle_version,
CASE
WHEN  bundle_version < "18.12.422" THEN "Vulnerable"
ELSE "Not Vulnerable"
END "3CX Desktop App Version Status" FROM apps
WHERE name LIKE "%3CX%";

linux.label: 3cx_desktop_app_compromise_monitoring
linux.name: 3CX Desktop App Version Status
linux.sql:
SELECT name, version,
CASE
WHEN  version < "18.12.422" THEN "Vulnerable"
ELSE "Not Vulnerable"
END "3CX Desktop App Version Status" FROM deb_packages
WHERE name LIKE "%3CX%" ;

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It can detect the mof file loading by mofcomp.exe from the Windows event log.  Following data is retrieved:
 * datetime - Date and time of the event as stated in eventlogs
 * new_process_name - Newly created process name e.g certutil.exe
 * new_process_path - Full path of the new process
 * parent_process_name - Parent process name only e.g. cmd.exe
 * parent_process_path - Full path of the new process
 * new_process_commandline - New process commandline
 * data - Eventlog data

osQuery.label: windows_mofcomp_loads_mof_monitoring
osQuery.name: Mofcomp.exe Loading MOF File Events
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
LOWER(replace(regex_match(data, 'NewProcessName":"([^"]+)\\([^"]+)', 2), "\\", "\")) AS new_process_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'NewProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "\\", "\") AS new_process_path, 
LOWER(replace(regex_match(data, 'ParentProcessName":"([^"]+)\\([^"]+)', 2), "\\", "\")) AS parent_process_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'ParentProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "\\", "\") AS parent_process_path,
replace(regex_match(data, 'CommandLine":"(.+?),"', 1), "\\", "\") AS new_process_commandline,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4688 AND
new_process_commandline LIKE regex_match(LOWER(new_process_commandline), '.*mofcomp(\.exe)?.*\.mof.*', 0);

queries.title: Windows Mofcomp Loading MOF File Monitoring
warnings: This query searches process data from Windows eventlog's audit logs. If the Windows's process event/audit logging is disabled, then this query will not return any data even if this activity occured on the host.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Early Launch Anti-Malware (ELAM) drivers is a feature introduced with Windows 8 that lets anti-malware software to start before other third-party components. ELAM drivers are initialized first and allowed to control the initialization of subsequent boot drivers, potentially not initializing unknown boot drivers. This query retrieves:
* EarlyLaunch_DriverLoadPolicy - driver load policy stored in the registry
* EarlyLaunch_DriverLoadPolicy_Text - string representation of the driver load policy stored in the registry
* EarlyLaunch_Service_Name - service name
* EarlyLaunch_Service_SignaturesVersion - service signature version stored in the registry
* EarlyLaunch_Service_Type - service type stored in the registry
* EarlyLaunch_Service_StartMode - service start mode stored in the registry
* EarlyLaunch_DriverFile_Path - ELAM driver location
* EarlyLaunch_DriverFile_ProductVersion - ELAM driver version
* EarlyLaunch_DriverFile_ModificationTime - last modified timestamp for the ELAM driver file
* EarlyLaunch_DriverBkupFile_Path - path for the backup ELAM driver
* EarlyLaunch_DriverBkupFile_ProductVersion - back ELAM driver version
* EarlyLaunch_DriverBkupFile_ModificationTime - last modified timestamp for the back ELAM driver file

osQuery.label: windows_elam_drivers_monitoring
osQuery.name: ELAM Drivers Data
osQuery.sql:
WITH el_drivers AS (
SELECT
SPLIT(key, '\', 4) AS Reg_Svc_Name,
key Reg_Svc_Key,
path Reg_Svc_Path,
data AS Reg_Svc_Group,
DATETIME(mtime, 'unixepoch') AS Reg_Svc_ModTime
FROM registry
WHERE path LIKE 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\%\Group'
AND data = 'Early-Launch'),
el_drvr_dtls AS (
SELECT 
DISTINCT el_drivers.Reg_Svc_Name AS Reg_Svc_Name,
el_drivers.Reg_Svc_Key AS Reg_Svc_Key,
el_drivers.Reg_Svc_Path AS Reg_Svc_Path,
el_drivers.Reg_Svc_Group AS Reg_Svc_Group,
el_drivers.Reg_Svc_ModTime AS Reg_Svc_ModTime,
MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'DisplayName' THEN data END) AS Reg_Svc_DisplayName,
MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'ImagePath' THEN data END) AS Reg_Svc_ImagePath,
MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'SignaturesVersion' THEN data END) AS Reg_Svc_SignaturesVersion,
CASE
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END) = '1' THEN 'Kernel device driver'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END) = '2' THEN 'File system driver (which is also a kernel device driver)'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END) = '4' THEN 'Set of arguments for an adapter'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END) = '10' THEN 'A Win32 program that can be started by the service controller and that obeys the service control protocol.'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END) = '20' THEN 'A Win32 service that can share a process with other Win32 services.'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END) = '60' THEN 'USER_SHARE_PROCESS TEMPLATE'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END) = 'E0' THEN 'USER_SHARE_PROCESS INSTANCE'
    ELSE MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Type' THEN data END)
END Reg_Svc_Type,
CASE 
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Start' THEN data END) = '0' THEN 'Loaded (but not started) by the boot loader. Then started during kernel initialization.'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Start' THEN data END) = '1' THEN 'Started during kernel initialization after services whose start parameter is 0.'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Start' THEN data END) = '2' THEN 'Automatically. Started by smss.exe (session manager) or services.exe (services controller).'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Start' THEN data END) = '3' THEN 'Manually. Started by the Service Control Manager (SCM).'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Start' THEN data END) = '4' THEN 'Disabled'
    WHEN MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Start' THEN data END) = '5' THEN 'Delayed start.'
    ELSE MAX(CASE WHEN reg_srvc.name = 'Start' THEN data END) 
END Reg_Svc_Start
FROM el_drivers
JOIN registry reg_srvc
ON reg_srvc.key = el_drivers.Reg_Svc_Key
GROUP BY el_drivers.Reg_Svc_Name),
el_drvr_bckp_dtls AS (
SELECT
directory AS File_Bkup_Directory,
path AS File_Bkup_Path,
filename AS File_Bkup_Name,
product_version AS File_Bkup_ProductVersion,
DATETIME(mtime, 'unixepoch') AS File_Bkup_ModTime
FROM file 
WHERE directory = (SELECT data FROM registry WHERE path = 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\EarlyLaunch\BackupPath'))
SELECT (
SELECT data 
FROM registry 
WHERE path = 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Policies\EarlyLaunch\DriverLoadPolicy') AS EarlyLaunch_DriverLoadPolicy,
CASE (
SELECT data 
FROM registry 
WHERE path = 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Policies\EarlyLaunch\DriverLoadPolicy')
WHEN '1' THEN 'Good and unknown'
WHEN '3' THEN 'Good, unknown and bad but critical'
WHEN '7' THEN 'All'
WHEN '8' THEN 'Good only'
ELSE 'No Value Configured'
END EarlyLaunch_DriverLoadPolicy_Text,
el_drvr_dtls.Reg_Svc_Name AS EarlyLaunch_Service_Name,
el_drvr_dtls.Reg_Svc_SignaturesVersion AS EarlyLaunch_Service_SignaturesVersion,
el_drvr_dtls.Reg_Svc_Type AS EarlyLaunch_Service_Type,
el_drvr_dtls.Reg_Svc_Start AS EarlyLaunch_Service_StartMode,
drvr_file.path AS EarlyLaunch_DriverFile_Path,
drvr_file.product_version AS EarlyLaunch_DriverFile_ProductVersion,
DATETIME(drvr_file.mtime, 'unixepoch')  AS EarlyLaunch_DriverFile_ModificationTime,
el_drvr_bckp_dtls.File_Bkup_Path AS EarlyLaunch_DriverBkupFile_Path,
el_drvr_bckp_dtls.File_Bkup_ProductVersion AS EarlyLaunch_DriverBkupFile_ProductVersion,
el_drvr_bckp_dtls.File_Bkup_ModTime AS EarlyLaunch_DriverBkupFile_ModificationTime
FROM el_drvr_dtls
JOIN file drvr_file
ON drvr_file.path = 'C:\Windows\' || el_drvr_dtls.Reg_Svc_ImagePath
JOIN el_drvr_bckp_dtls
ON drvr_file.filename = el_drvr_bckp_dtls.File_Bkup_Name 
WHERE
EarlyLaunch_Service_Name LIKE regex_match(EarlyLaunch_Service_Name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = 'elam_service_pattern'), 0);

queries.title: Early Launch Anti-Malware Drivers Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Temporary directories are used by applications to temporarily store data. Malware often uses this location during its installation or unpacking, or for payloads download. This query retrieves data on all files located in the user temp directory.
Note that this query assumes the default user profile location under "C:\Users".

osQuery.label: temp_directories_monitoring
osQuery.name: Temp Directory Files
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.path, f.filename, 
CASE filename
WHEN "." THEN ""
ELSE h.sha256
END sha256,
f.uid, f.gid, f.mode, f.size, 
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time, 
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified, 
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time, 
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS creation_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type
FROM file f LEFT JOIN hash h ON f.path=h.path
WHERE 
f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\AppData\Local\Temp\%%" OR
f.path LIKE (SELECT value FROM orbital_environment WHERE name LIKE "windir") || "\Temp\%%";

queries.title: Temporary Directory Files Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Defender Firewall rules configured on the host and stored in the registry.

osQuery.label: windows_firewall_rules_configuration_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Firewall Rules Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT name AS "Rule Name", data AS "Rule Logic", DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS "Last Updated" 
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy\FirewallRules" 
AND 
data LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="firewall_rule");

queries.title: Windows Firewall Rules Configuration Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows events related to driver being loaded on the host. Malware, such as HermeticWiper, may attempt to install a custom driver to perform malicious operations:
* datetime event was generated
* driver image path
* full event data
Note that this query, by default, retrieves all driver loading events from the host. For example, if you need to collect only February 2022 events to narrow down your search - set the parameter "datetime_pattern" to "2022-02-.*".

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_driver_loaded
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'ImagePath":"(.*)","ServiceType":', 1) as image_path,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "System" AND
eventid = 7045 AND
image_path LIKE "%.sys%" AND
datetime LIKE regex_match(datetime, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Driver Loaded Windows Event Logs

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on the host CPU in the environment:
* device id - the device id of the cpu
* model 
* manufacturer
* processor type - the processor type, such as Central, Math or Video
* availability: unknown (2), running (3), warning (4), power off (7), off line (8), off duty (9), degraded (10), not installed (11), install error (12), not configured (20)
* cpu status: unknown (0),  disabled by user via BIOS setup (2), disabled by BIOS (3), idle (4)
* number of cores 
* logical processors
* current clock speed - the current frequency of the CPU
* max clock speed - the maximum possible frequency of the CPU

osQuery.label: information_search_on_central_processing_unit
osQuery.name: Central Processing Unit Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT device_id, model, manufacturer, processor_type, availability, cpu_status, number_of_cores, logical_processors, current_clock_speed, max_clock_speed
FROM cpu_info;

queries.title: Information Search on Central Processing Unit

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It is used to monitor registry keys which contain commands that are performed when an executable file is ran. The value of these keys can be overwritten to contain malicious commands or to point to malicious executables.
