This technique can be used to establish persistence on the host or to elevate privileges.

osQuery.label: sdclt_bypass_registry_isolatedcommand_key
osQuery.name: IsolatedCommand Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
(key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Classes\exefile\shell\%\command" OR
(key LIKE "HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\exefile\shell\%\command" ))
AND name IN ("", "IsolatedCommand") );

queries.title: Exe File Registry Key IsolatedCommand Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. "Shell Folders" and "User Shell Folders" registry keys are also known as "startup keys" and are commonly used by adversaries to change the location of the startup folder. This query retrieves hosts where non-standard "Startup" directories were found.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_shell_folders_startup_location
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data, 
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
key LIKE "%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders"
OR key LIKE "%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\User Shell Folders") AND
name LIKE "%Startup%" AND
data NOT IN (
'%ProgramData%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup',
'%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup',
'C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup');

queries.title: Windows Registry Suspicious Startup Location

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It lets the user to check for Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) anomalies on the host. Address Resolution Protocol is used to find the physical address associated with an IP address. Endpoints create a local cache to avoid constant ARP lookups. Usually an IP address will be associated to a single physical address only. The same IP address being mapped to multiple physical addresses should be considered anomalous.

osQuery.label: arp_cache_inspection
osQuery.name: ARP Cache
osQuery.sql:
SELECT address, mac, count(*) as count 
FROM arp_cache GROUP BY mac,address
HAVING COUNT(mac) >= (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="ip_to_mac_count")
AND address LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="ip_address")
AND mac LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="mac_address");

queries.title: ARP Cache Inspection

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves information about user account settings on the system. The data displayed are: 
* Username
* SID - security identifier 
* Disabled - True if disabled, otherwise False
* Domain - name of the Windows domain to which as user account belongs
* LocalAccount - if True, the account is defined on the local computer
* PasswordChangeable - if True, the password on this user account can be changed
* PasswordExpires - if True, the password on this user account expires
* PasswordRequired - if True, a password is required on a Windows user account

osQuery.label: user_account_settings_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Account Settings
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
Name AS "User_name",
SID,
Disabled,
Domain,
LocalAccount,
Lockout,
PasswordChangeable,
PasswordExpires,
PasswordRequired
FROM Win32_UserAccount;

queries.title: User Account Settings Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. The following data for system network state are retrieved:
* disconnected - 1 if the all interfaces are not connected to any network
* ipv4_no_traffic - 1 if any interface is connected via IPv4, but has seen no traffic
* ipv6_no_traffic - 1 if any interface is connected via IPv6, but has seen no traffic
* ipv4_subnet - 1 if any interface is connected to the local subnet via IPv4
* ipv4_local_network - 1 if any interface is connected to a routed network via IPv4
* ipv4_internet - 1 if any interface is connected to the Internet via IPv4
* ipv6_subnet - 1 if any interface is connected to the local subnet via IPv6
* ipv6_local_network - 1 if any interface is connected to a routed network via IPv6
* ipv6_internet - 1 if any interface is connected to the Internet via IPv6

osQuery.label: system_network_state_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Network State
osQuery.sql:
SELECT disconnected, ipv4_no_traffic, ipv6_no_traffic, ipv4_subnet, ipv4_local_network, ipv4_internet, ipv6_subnet, ipv6_local_network, ipv6_internet
FROM connectivity;

queries.title: System Network State Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves hidden scheduled tasks running on the host that are not related to Windows.

osQuery.label: hidden_scheduled_tasks_monitoring
osQuery.name: Hidden Scheduled Tasks Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT st.name, st.action, st.path, st.enabled, st.state, st.last_run_time, st.next_run_time, st.last_run_code  
FROM scheduled_tasks st
WHERE st.path NOT LIKE "\Microsoft\Windows%"
AND st.hidden=1;

queries.title: Hidden Scheduled Tasks Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. sdclt.exe is used in the context of Windows backup and restore mechanisms. The backup and restore operation is part of the control panel, meaning that sdclt.exe process will start the control panel as well. When sdclt.exe starts it looks for the "App Paths\control.exe" registry key which, by default, does not exist. An attacker could created or modify this registry key in order to establish persistence on the host. This query retrieves data on the "App Paths\control.exe" registry key.

osQuery.label: sdclt_bypass_registry_control_key
osQuery.name: SDCLT Control Registry Key

queries.title: SDCLT Control Registry Key Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. The Keychain is a system-level password repository designed to make saving and retrieving passwords easier for the computer user. This query retrieves generic details about keychain items:
* label - generic item name
* description - optional item description
* comment - optional keychain comment
* created - date item was created
* modified -date of last modification
* type - eychain item type (password, certificate, public/private key, symmetric key)
* path - path to keychain containing item

osQuery.label: macos_keychain_items_monitoring
osQuery.name: Keychain Items Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
label, description, comment, created,
modified, type, path
FROM
keychain_items
WHERE
label LIKE regex_match(label, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "label_pattern"), 0) AND
type LIKE regex_match(type, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "type_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Keychain Items Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves process termination events from the host:
* datetime the event was generated
* process that was terminated
* process responsible for termination
* termination status code
* full event data
Search suggestions:
* Adversaries could attempt to dump lsass.exe memory for offline credential extraction by calling "RtlReportSilentProcessExit" Windows API. All events where lsass.exe process was terminated should be investigated for suspicious activity. Set parameter "terminated_process_pattern" to ".*lsass.exe.*" to check your environment for such events.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_process_termination
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'param1":"([^"]+)', 1) AS terminated_process,
regex_match(data, 'param2":"([^"]+)', 1) AS terminated_by,
regex_match(data, 'param3":"([^"]+)', 1) AS termination_code,
data 
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Application" AND
eventid = 3001 AND 
data like "%PROCESSTERMINATION%" AND
terminated_process LIKE regex_match(LOWER(terminated_process), (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "terminated_process_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Process Termination

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the list of applications disallowed to run on the host:
* Registry key "DisallowRun" must be set to "1" to prevent application listed under this key from running.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_disallow_run_status
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\DisallowRun" AND
type!="subkey";

osQuery.label: windows_registry_disallow_run_apps
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\DisallowRun\%";

queries.title: Windows Registry Application Disallowed to Run

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the registered Subject Interface Package (SIP) providers from the registry. SIP providers are used by the Windows cryptographic system to validate file signatures on the host. Adversaries could use malicious SIP provider to bypass signature validation checks or to inject code into critical processes.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_sip_providers
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
path LIKE 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography\Providers\Trust\FinalPolicy\%\$dll' OR
path LIKE 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Microsoft\Cryptography\Providers\Trust\FinalPolicy\%\$dll' OR
path LIKE 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography\OID\EncodingType 0\CryptSIPDllPutSignedDataMsg\%\Dll' OR
path LIKE 'HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Microsoft\Cryptography\OID\EncodingType 0\CryptSIPDllPutSignedDataMsg\%\Dll') AND
data NOT LIKE 'C:\Windows\System32%' AND
data NOT LIKE 'C:\Windows\SysWOW64%' AND
data NOT LIKE 'C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework%' AND
data LIKE '%\%';

queries.title: Windows Registry SIP Providers Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS and Linux. It displays sysctl names, values, and settings information where sysctl is a function that retrieves and sets system information such as system kernel version number, maximum limits, and security settings:
* name - full sysctl Management Information Base (MIB) name
* oid - control MIB
* subsystem - subsystem ID, control type
* current_value - value of setting
* config_value - the MIB value set in /etc/sysctl.conf
* type - data type
Modifications in sysctl.conf file could change the security settings of the system and therefore should be monitored. To find all kernel related settings on MacOS set parameters:
* "subsystem_pattern" to "kern"
* "name_pattern" to ".*(boot|secure|single).*".

osQuery.label: macos_linux_system_controls
osQuery.name: System Controls Settings
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, oid, subsystem, current_value, config_value, type
FROM system_controls 
WHERE
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
subsystem LIKE regex_match(subsystem, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "subsystem_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: System Controls Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. Apple System Log (ASL) contains a variety of information that can be of use to forensic analysts such as login data, program errors, system boots, reboots and shutdowns and many more. This query retrieves ASL system events from the host:
* time - time event generated
* host - sender's address (set by the server)
* sender - sender's identification string. Default is process name.
* facility - ender's facility. Default is "user".
* pid - sending process ID encoded as a string. Set automatically.
* gid - GID that sent the log message (set by the server)
* uid - UID that sent the log message (set by the server)
* logging level
* message - message text
* extra - extra event columns in JSON format

osQuery.label: macos_asl_monitoring
osQuery.name: ASL System Events Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") as time,
host, sender, facility, pid, gid, uid,
CASE level
WHEN "0" THEN "Emergency Level"
WHEN "1" THEN "Alert Level"
WHEN "2" THEN "Critical Level"
WHEN "3" THEN "Error Level"
WHEN "4" THEN "Warning Level"
WHEN "5" THEN "Notice Level"
WHEN "6" THEN "Info Level"
WHEN "7" THEN "Debug Level"
ELSE "Unknown" END "Logging Level",
message, extra
FROM asl
WHERE
host LIKE regex_match(host, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "host_pattern"), 0) AND
"Logging Level" LIKE regex_match("Logging Level", (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "logging_level_pattern"), 0) AND
message LIKE regex_match(message, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "message_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Apple System Log (ASL) System Events Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on Windows Remote Management service running on the host:
* service name
* service display name
* service status: Running, Stopped, Paused, Start_Pending, Stop_Pending, Continue_Pending, Pause_Pending
* service process ID 
* service start type: Boot_Start, System_Start, Auto_Start, Demand_Start, Disabled
* service path
* service module path: path to service DLL
* service user account

osQuery.label: winrm_service_monitoring
osQuery.name: WinRM Services

queries.title: Windows Remote Management Service Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable for Windows and MacOS. It retrieves information about startup items such as:
* name - Name or identifier of the startup item.
* path - Path to the executable launched due to startup.
* args - Commandline Arguments provided to startup executable.
* source - The source of startup item such as an auto-run registry key, a launch daemon or an item in the startup folder.
* status - If Startup Item is Active/Enabled or otherwise.
* type - Startup Item or Login Item.
* username - The user associated with the startup item.

osQuery.label: startup_items
osQuery.name: Startup Items
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, args, source, type, status, username 
FROM startup_items;

queries.title: Startup Items

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows events that are related to new rules that have been added to the Windows Firewall:
* timestamp event generated
* modifying application
* modifying application path
* modifying service name if present
* rule id
* rule name
* rule's remote ports value
* rule's remote addresses value
* rule's local ports value
* rule's local addresses value
* full event data
Comments: The most common modifying applications, such as Windows Defender and Service Control Manager, as well as the most common rule names, such as "Microsoft Edge (mDNS-In)", were filtered out to reduce the output. Further filtering might be needed to limit the output depending on the environment where the data has been collected.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_windows_firewall_rule_added
osQuery.name: Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'ModifyingApplication":"([^"]+)', 1) as modifying_application,
regex_match(data, 'ApplicationPath":"([^"]+)', 1) as application_path,
regex_match(data, 'ServiceName":"([^"]+)', 1) as service_name,
regex_match(data, 'RuleId":"([^"]+)', 1) as rule_id,
regex_match(data, 'RuleName":"([^"]+)', 1) as rule_name,
regex_match(data, 'RemotePorts":"([^"]+)', 1) as remote_ports,
regex_match(data, 'RemoteAddresses":"([^"]+)', 1) as remote_addresses,
regex_match(data, 'LocalPorts":"([^"]+)', 1) as local_ports,
regex_match(data, 'LocalAddresses":"([^"]+)', 1) as local_addresses,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE 
channel = "Microsoft-Windows-Windows Firewall With Advanced Security/Firewall" AND 
eventid = 2004 AND 
rule_name NOT LIKE "%@{Microsoft%" AND
rule_name NOT LIKE "Microsoft Edge (mDNS-In)" AND
modifying_application NOT LIKE "%svchost%" AND
modifying_application NOT LIKE "%netsh.exe%" AND
modifying_application NOT LIKE "%msmpeng%";

queries.title: Windows Firewall Rule Added Events Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves from the registry host's default browser.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_default_browser
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, split(path, '\', 8) AS protocol, 
name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\Associations\URLAssociations\https\UserChoice\ProgId" OR 
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Shell\Associations\URLAssociations\http\UserChoice\ProgId") AND
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Registry Default Browser

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Scheduled tasks allow the user to automatically perform certain actions like running a program or a script on the system. Malware can use scheduled tasks to run malicious code and to establish persistence on the host. This query retrieves data on scheduled tasks that are set to execute code from directories such as "AppData\Roaming" or "AppData\Local\Temp".

osQuery.label: scheduled_tasks_from_appdata_monitoring
osQuery.name: Scheduled Tasks Data

queries.title: Scheduled Tasks From AppData Directory Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The "Userinit" registry key defines which programs are run by Winlogon when a user logs in to the system. The "Shell" registry key starts "explorer.exe", and the "Notify" registry key loads dynamic link libraries (DLLS) that handle Winlogon events. Malware will often attempt to modify these values to establish persistence on the system.

osQuery.label: winlogon_registry_key
osQuery.name: Winlogon Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified 
FROM registry 
WHERE (
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\winlogon" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\Wow6432Node\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\winlogon" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\winlogon\Notify\%%" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\wow6432node\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\winlogon\Notify\%%" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Notify\%%")
AND name IN ("GinaDLL", "Shell", "Appsetup", "TaskMan", "UIHost", "Userinit", "VMApplet", "System");

queries.title: Winlogon Registry Key Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows binary "CompatTelRunner.exe" is designed to run a variety of telemetry tasks and it relies on the registry to instruct on which commands to run. Attackers could use the "TelemetryController" registry key to establish persistence on the host or to gain System privileges. This query retrieves executables stored under this registry key which should be examined for suspicious entries.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_telemetrycontroller_binaries
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM
registry
WHERE
path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\AppCompatFlags\TelemetryController\%\Command" AND
data LIKE "%.exe%" AND
data NOT LIKE "%system32\CompatTelRunner.exe%";

queries.title: Windows Registry TelemetryController Binaries Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Disk encryption is a critical data protection mechanism and is often required as a part of security policy compliance. It may be implemented in software or hardware. Many solid state devices offer hardware encryption features. However these often do not provide adequate levels of protection and are vulnerable. Moreover, BitLocker, which is a part of Windows, leverages SSD hardware encryption by default when it is available. As such, these options may be in use, without explicit knowledge of administrators. This query monitors for systems that are running BitLocker and that have been configured to the default SSD hardware encryption.

osQuery.label: hardware_encryption
osQuery.name: Hardware Encryption

queries.title: Hardware Encryption Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to MacOS and Linux. It lists the files present in the directory '/etc' that were modified in the last 10 days.

osQuery.label: macos_linux_configuration_files_modification_monitoring
osQuery.name: Configuration Files Modification Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
file.path, hash.sha256, 
file.size, file.uid,
DATETIME(file.atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time, 
DATETIME(file.ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time, 
DATETIME(file.mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,  
((strftime('%s', 'now') - file.mtime) / 86400) AS last_modification_time_in_days
From 
file
LEFT JOIN hash ON file.path = hash.path
WHERE last_modification_time_in_days < 10
AND file.type = 'regular'
AND file.path LIKE '/etc/%%';

queries.title: Configuration Files Modification Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data from the Windows WMI class "SoftwareLicensingProduct" that exposes product-specific properties of the Software Licensing service:
* name - product name
* license_status - license status of a product application
* licensefamily - dependency identifier for the set of SKUs used to determine license relationships for add-ons
* productkeyid - product ID which determines the type of support and channel the software originated from (Retail, OEM, Volume License, Academic)
Comments:
"OOBGrace" or Initial Grace starts the first time you start you computer after you install the OS, and has a duration of 30 days.
"OOTGrace" or Out of Tolerance grace begins when cumulative hardware changes on an activated computer push it beyond a tolerance level, or when a KMS client goes for 180 days without contacting a KMS.
"NonGenuineGrace" occurs only on a computer that has the Windows Genuine ActiveX Control installed and then fails Genuine Validation.
"Notification" means that the product is not fully activated. The grace period has lapsed and the product must be reactivated for maximum functionality.

osQuery.label: windows_wmi_software_licensing_product
osQuery.name: SoftwareLicensingProduct
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name,
CASE licensestatus
WHEN "0" THEN "Unlicensed"
WHEN "1" THEN "Licensed"
WHEN "2" THEN "OOBGrace"
WHEN "3" THEN "OOTGrace"
WHEN "4" THEN "NonGenuineGrace"
WHEN "5" THEN "Notification"
WHEN "6" THEN "ExtendedGrace"
ELSE "Unknown"
END license_status,
licensefamily,
productkeyid
FROM SoftwareLicensingProduct;

queries.title: Windows WMI Software Licensing Product
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs related to user account modifications:
* 4720 - a user account was created
* 4722 - a user account was enabled
* 4723 - an attempt was made to change an account's password
* 4724 - an attempt was made to reset an account's password
* 4725 - a user account was disabled
* 4726 - a user account was deleted
* 4738 - a user account was changed
* 4740 - a user account was locked out
* 4767 - a user account was unlocked
* 4781 - a user account name was changed
Windows Event Logs for the ID's listed below should be investigated for potential system compromise.

osQuery.label: account_events_monitoring
osQuery.name: Account Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "Timewritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE 
channel = "Security" AND
eventid IN (4720, 4722, 4723, 4724, 4725, 4726, 4738, 4740, 4767, 4781);

queries.title: Windows Events For Account Modifications Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data from Windows crash logs based on the pattern provided. The following data is retrieved:
* datetime - timestamp of the crash
* module - path of the crashed module within the process
* path - path of the executable file for the crashed process
* pid - process ID
* tid - thread ID of the crashed thread
* version - file version info of the crashed process
* process_uptime - uptime of the process in seconds
* exception_code - the Windows exception code
* exception_message - the NTSTATUS error message associated with the exception code
* exception_address - address (in hex) where the exception occurred
* command_line - command-line string passed to the crashed process
* username - username of the user who ran the crashed process
* machine_name - name of the host where the crash happened
* type - type of crash log
* crash_log - path  of the log file

osQuery.label: windows_crashes
osQuery.name: Windows Crashes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT datetime, module, path, pid, tid, version, process_uptime, exception_code, exception_message, exception_address, command_line, username, machine_name, type, crash_path
FROM windows_crashes;

queries.title: Windows Crashes Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It uses Application events from the "MSSQL$MICROSOFT##WID" to collect SQL statements run against an AD FS database:
* datetime - time event was generated
* sql statement run against AD FS database
* data - event data

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_sql_audit_events
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'statement":"([^"]+)', 1) AS sql_statement,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Application" AND
eventid = 33205;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs SQL Audit

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on patches applied on the host: 
* OS name
* hotfix_id - the KB ID of the patch
* caption - short description of the patch
* description - fuller description of the patch
* fix_comments - additional comments about the patch
* installed_by - the system context in which the patch was installed
* install_date - indicates when the patch was installed. Lack of a value does not indicate that the patch was not installed.
* installed_on - the date when the patch was installed

osQuery.label: windows_hotfixes
osQuery.name: Windows HotFixes
osQuery.sql:
WITH 
b1 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, name FROM os_version),
b2 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, hotfix_id, caption, description, fix_comments, installed_by, install_date, installed_on FROM patches)
SELECT b1.name, b2.hotfix_id, b2.caption, b2.description, b2.fix_comments, b2.installed_by, b2.install_date, b2.installed_on
FROM b1 JOIN b2 USING(one)
WHERE 
b2.hotfix_id LIKE regex_match(b2.hotfix_id, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "hotfix_search_pattern"), 0) AND
b1.name LIKE regex_match(b1.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "os_name_pattern"), 0)
ORDER BY b2.installed_on DESC;

queries.title: Windows HotFixes Monitoring

starting540.label: windows_hotfixes
starting540.name: Windows HotFixes
starting540.sql:
WITH b1 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, name FROM os_version), b3 AS (SELECT  1 as one, regex_match(title, 'KB[^\)]+',0) AS hotfix_id, support_url, title, result_code, date from windows_update_history where hotfix_id != ""),
b2 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, hotfix_id, caption, description, fix_comments, installed_by, install_date, installed_on FROM patches)
SELECT * FROM(
SELECT  b1.name, b2.hotfix_id, b2.caption, b2.description, b2.fix_comments, b2.installed_by, b2.install_date, b2.installed_on from b2 JOIN b1 USING(one)
UNION 
SELECT  b1.name, b3.hotfix_id, b3.support_url as "caption", b3.title as "description", b3.result_code as "fix_comments", "" as "installed_by", "" as "install_date", strftime("%m/%d/%Y",b3.date, 'unixepoch') AS 'installed_on' from b3 JOIN  b1 USING(one))
WHERE hotfix_id LIKE regex_match(hotfix_id, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "hotfix_search_pattern"), 0)
AND name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "os_name_pattern"), 0)
group by hotfix_id ORDER BY installed_on DESC;

warnings: Query results do not include patches applied via MSI or downloaded from Windows Update (e.g. Service Packs)

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The "AppCertDLLs" registry key lists DLLs that will be loaded into every process that calls such Windows API functions like CreateProcess, CreateProcessAsUser, CreateProcessWithLoginW, CreateProcessWithTokenW, or WinExec. Malware, for example Honeybee or Punchbuggy, can add an entry to the "AppCertDLLsi" registry key pointing to malicious code in order to establish persistence on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_appcertdlls_registry_monitoring
osQuery.name: AppCertDLLs Registry Key

queries.title: Windows AppCertDLLs Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The DPAPI (Data Protection Application Programming Interface) Key is used to encrypt user details. Malicious actors may attempt to backup the backup DPAPI key and use it decrypt any domain user master key. This query checks for Windows Event Logs indicating that a backup is attempted for the DPAPI Master Key.

osQuery.label: windows_events_backup_of_backup_key_monitoring
osQuery.name: Backup of DPAPI Key Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
data AS Message,
datetime AS "Time Generated",
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4692;

queries.title: Windows Events For Backup of DPAPI Key Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. By default, Windows caches the last logon credentials for users who log on interactively on the system to provide system availability in case no domain controller (DCs) being available for authentication or the host is disconnected from the network. This query retrieves the "CachedLogonsCount" value from the registry.
Search suggestions:
* Setting the parameter "cached_logons_count_value" to "0" will list all hosts that do not cache logon credentials. Domain users using systems where this value is set to "0" will not be able to log on if the network is disconnected or DC is offline.
* By default the "CachedLogonsCount" is set to "10". Searching for hosts where this value is set to anything but "10" could help to identify non compliant hosts.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_cached_logons_count
osQuery.name: CachedLogonCount Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" AND
name LIKE "CachedLogonsCount" AND
data LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "cached_logons_count_value");

queries.title: Registry Cached Logon Count Monitoring

queries.description: EOL 2022-08. This query is applicable to Windows. CVE-2020-0601 is a vulnerability in the way Windows validates Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) certificates. Unpatched vulnerability allows attackers to spoof a code-signing certificate that could be used to sign a malicious executable. This query retrieves information on all the hosts where the CVE-2020-0601 patch has been downloaded and checks if the patch was applied or not.

osQuery.label: patch_install_date_utc_tmp
osQuery.name: Installed Patches Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE patch_install_date_utc AS 
SELECT 
hotfix_id, 
SPLIT(installed_on, "/", 2) || "-" || 
SUBSTR(("0" || CAST(SPLIT(installed_on, "/", 0) AS TEXT)), -2) || "-" ||  
SUBSTR(("0" || CAST(SPLIT(installed_on, "/", 1) AS TEXT)), -2) || 
" 00:00:01" AS install_date_utc
FROM patches
WHERE 
hotfix_id IN ("KB4534306", "KB4534271", "KB4534276", "KB4534293", "KB4534273", "KB4528760");

osQuery.label: restart_check_table_tmp
osQuery.name: Restart Check Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE restart_check_table AS 
SELECT hotfix_id,
CASE WHEN DATETIME(((SELECT local_time FROM time) - (SELECT total_seconds FROM uptime)),"unixepoch") > install_date_utc
THEN 'true'
ELSE 'false'
END AS restart_check
FROM patch_install_date_utc;

osQuery.label: cve_2020_0601_patch_monitoring
osQuery.name: CVE-2020-0601 Patch Status
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.csname, p.hotfix_id, p.caption, p.description, p.fix_comments, p.installed_by, p.installed_on,
CASE WHEN rct.restart_check="true" THEN "patch applied" ELSE "patch not applied" END AS patch_status
FROM patches p
JOIN restart_check_table rct ON p.hotfix_id = rct.hotfix_id;

queries.title: The Certificate Validation Vulnerability CVE-2020-0601 Patch Monitoring (Deprecated)

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. The Keychain is a system-level password repository designed to make saving and retrieving passwords easier for the computer user. This query retrieves data on applications that have access control list (ACL) entries in the keychain:
* keychain_path - the path of the keychain 
* authorizations - a space delimited set of authorization attributes
* path - the path of the authorized application
* description - the description included with the ACL entry
* label - an optional label tag that may be included with the keychain entry

osQuery.label: macos_keychain_acls_monitoring
osQuery.name: Applications Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
keychain_path, authorizations, path, description, label
FROM keychain_acls
WHERE
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Application with ACL in the Keychain Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It returns the mutex-name when a shared mutex matching the provided pattern exists on the endpoint. A named mutex is an operating system resource that allows synchronization of threads from different programs.

osQuery.label: process_mutex_search
osQuery.name: Process Mutex
osQuery.sql:
SELECT object_name 
FROM winbaseobj 
WHERE object_type="Mutant"
AND object_name LIKE
(SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="mutex");

queries.title: Process Mutex Search
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Keylogging is the most often used method to capture user input and to acquire credentials for new access opportunities. PowerSploit"s module Get-Keystrokes was designed to log the keys pressed, time and the active window. This query triggers when PowerShell script containing Get-Keystrokes is seen.

osQuery.label: get_keystrokes_module_detected
osQuery.name: Get-Keystrokes PowerShell Events

queries.title: Get-Keystrokes Module Detected

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on executables that are set to run automatically on the host. The following data is retrieved: 
* name 
* path
* source table of the autoexec item - this table could be queried for more information about the suspicious executable

osQuery.label: autoexec_param
osQuery.name: Autoexec Table
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, source
FROM autoexec
WHERE name NOT LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="not_program_name"),0)
AND path NOT LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="not_file_path")
AND path LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="file_path")
AND name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="program_name");

queries.title: Search For Automatically Executing Binaries

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves PowerShell command lines that are longer than the specified command length. Attackers often use long, obfuscated powershell commands for "file-less" attacks.

osQuery.label: powershell_commands
osQuery.name: PowerShell Commands

queries.title: PowerShell Commands Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It is designed to retrieve the following data related to launchd daemons or agent services running on the host:
    * path - Path to daemon or agent plist
    * name - File name of plist (used by launchd)
    * label - Daemon or agent service name
    * program - Path to target program
    * run_at_load - Should the program run on launch load
    * keep_alive - Should the process be restarted if killed
    * disabled - Skip loading this daemon or agent on boot
    * username - Run this daemon or agent as this username
    * groupname - Run this daemon or agent as this group
    * stdout_path - Pipe stdout to a target path
    * stderr_path - Pipe stderr to a target path
    * start_interval - Frequency of running in seconds
    * program_arguments - Command line arguments passed to program
    * watch_paths - Key that launches daemon or agent if path is modified
    * queue_directories - Similar to watch_paths but only with non-empty directories
    * inetd_compatibility - Run this daemon or agent as it was launched from inetd
    * start_on_mount - Run daemon or agent every time a filesystem is mounted
    * root_directory - Key used to specify a directory to chroot to before launch
    * working_directory - Key used to specify a directory to chdir to before launch
    * process_type - Key describes the intended purpose of the job

osQuery.label: macos_launchd_monitoring
osQuery.name: LaunchAgents and LaunchDaemons Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
path, name, label, program,
run_at_load, keep_alive, disabled,
username, groupname, stdout_path, stderr_path,
start_interval, program_arguments, watch_paths,
queue_directories, inetd_compatibility,
start_on_mount, root_directory,
working_directory, process_type
FROM launchd
WHERE
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0) AND
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
program LIKE regex_match(program, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "program_pattern"), 0) AND
username LIKE regex_match(username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0) AND
program_arguments LIKE regex_match(program_arguments, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "program_arguments_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: LaunchAgents and LaunchDaemons Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the list of features that allows an adversary to hide or remove configuration infromation or to aid in persistence and execution:

"UseLogonCredential" - Store logon credentials. Recommended value is "1".
"LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy" - Disable UAC Remote Restrictions. Recommended value is "1".
"EnableLinkedConnections" -  Enable OS to share network connections between different privilege levels. Recommended value is "1".
"LongPathsEnabled" - Enable long path values for file paths, names, and namespaces.
"DisableRegistryTools" - Disable modification of registry entry via Windows registry tool.
"DisableCMD" -  Disable Windows CMD application. Recommended value is "1".
"DisableTaskmgr" -  Disable Windows Task Manager application. Recommended value is "1".
"DisableNotificationCenter" - Disable Windows notification center. Recommended value is "1".
"NoLogOff" - Disable Windows logOff button. Recommended value is "1".
"StartMenuLogOff" - Disable Windows logOff button. Recommended value is "1".
"DisableChangePassword" -   Disable Windows change password feature. Recommended value is "1".
"DisableLockWorkstation" - Disable Windows lock workstation feature Recommended value is "1".
"NoDesktop" - Hide all icons on Desktop Group policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoRun" - Remove Run menu from Start Menu Group policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoFind" - Remove Search menu from Start Menu Group policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoControlPanel" - Disable Control Panel group policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoFileMenu" - Remove File menu from Windows Explorer group policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoClose" - Disable and remove the Shut Down command group policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoSetTaskbar" - Disable changes to Taskbar and Start Menu Settings group policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoTrayContextMenu" - Disable context menu for taskbar Group Policy. Recommended value is "1".
"NoPropertiesMyDocuments" - Hide Properties from "My Documents icon" Group Policy. Recommended value is "1".
"HideClock" - Hide Clock Group Policy. Recommended value is "1".
"HideSCAHealth" - Remove security and maintenance icon Group Policy. Recommended value is "1".
"HideSCANetwork" - Remove the networking icon Group Policy. Recommended value is "1".
"HideSCAPower" - Remove the battery icon Group Policy. Recommended value is "1".
"HideSCAVolume" - Remove the volume icon Group Policy. Recommended value is "1".
"Notification_Suppress" - Suppress the windows defender notification. Recommended value is "1".
"fAllowToGetHelp" -  Allow rdp remote assistance feature. Recommended value is "1".
"DisableEnhancedNotifications" - Disable windows error reporting settings. Recommended value is "1".
"DisallowRun" - "Prevent user running specific computer programs". Recommended value is "1".
"DisableRestrictedAdmin" - Enabling Restricted Admin Mode via Command_Prompt,enables an attacker to perform a pass-the-hash attack using RDP. Recommended value is "0".
"shutdownwithoutlogon" -  Disable the Windows shutdown button. Recommended value is "0".
"ToastEnabled" -  Disable the windows toast notification. Recommended value is "0".
"UseActionCenterExperience" -  Disable the windows security center notification. Recommended value is "0".
"ShowInfoTip" - Show tips feature. Recommended value is "0".
"ShowCompColor" - Show compress color feature. Recommended value is "0".
"EnableModuleLogging" -  Disable PowerShell module logging. Recommended value is "0".
"EnableScriptBlockLogging" - Disable PowerShell script logging. Recommended value is "0".
"EnableTranscripting" - PowerShell Transcription will record detailed information from the processing of PowerShell commands and scripts. Disable PowerShell Transcription. Recommended value is "0".
"EnableScripts" - Disable PowerShell script logging. Recommended value is "0".

osQuery.label: windows_registry_features_disable_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE 
(( path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest\UseLogonCredential" 
OR path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\EnableLinkedConnections"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\FileSystem\LongPathsEnabled"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\policies\system\DisableRegistryTools"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System\DisableCMD"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\DisableTaskmgr"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer\DisableNotificationCenter"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoLogOff"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\StartMenuLogOff"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\DisableChangePassword"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\DisableLockWorkstation"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoDesktop"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoRun"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoFind"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoControlPanel"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoFileMenu"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoClose"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoSetTaskbar"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\NoTrayContextMenu"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\\Policies\Explorer\NoPropertiesMyDocuments"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\HideClock"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\HideSCAHealth"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\HideSCANetwork"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\HideSCAPower"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\HideSCAVolume"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\UX Configuration\Notification_Suppress"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\fAllowToGetHelp"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Reporting\DisableEnhancedNotifications"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\DisallowRun")  
AND  data != "" AND  data != "0") 
OR ((
path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\system\currentcontrolset\control\lsa\DisableRestrictedAdmin"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\shutdownwithoutlogon"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\PushNotifications\ToastEnabled"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\ImmersiveShell\UseActionCenterExperience"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\ShowInfoTip"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced\ShowCompColor"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\ModuleLogging\EnableModuleLogging"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\ScriptBlockLogging\EnableScriptBlockLogging"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\Transcription\EnableTranscripting"
OR path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\EnableScripts")
AND data = "0") 
OR (
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\DisallowRun\%");

queries.title: Disable Windows Features Monitoring via Registry

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux and MacOS. It retrieves data for known and remembered Wi-Fi networks for the system. Data displayed:
* ssid - service set identifier 
* network name 
* security type - type of security on this network: Open, WPA/WPA2, etc.
* last connected - last time this network was connected to
* passpoint - status of  WiFi Alliance (WFA) protocol that helps with authentication for Wi-Fi hotspots
* roaming - if roaming is supported
* roaming_profile - Single, Multi, Dual
* captive_portal - if this network has a captive portal
* auto_login - if auto login is enabled
* temporarily_disabled - if this network is temporarily disabled
* disabled - if this network is disabled

osQuery.label: wifi_networks_monitoring
osQuery.name: Wi-Fi Networks Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT ssid, network_name, security_type, 
DATETIME(last_connected,"unixepoch") AS "Last Connected",
CASE passpoint WHEN "1" THEN "Passpoint is supported" ELSE "Passpoint is not supported" END "passpoint",
CASE possibly_hidden WHEN "1" THEN "Network is possibly a hidden network" ELSE "Network is not hidden" END "possibly_hidden",
CASE roaming WHEN "1" THEN "Romaing is supported" ELSE "Romaing is not supported" END "roaming",
roaming_profile,
CASE captive_portal WHEN "1" THEN "Captive portal" ELSE "Not a captive portal" END "captive_portal",
CASE auto_login WHEN "1" THEN "Auto login is enabled" ELSE "Auto login is disabled" END "auto_login",
CASE temporarily_disabled WHEN "1" THEN "Network is temporarily disabled" ELSE "Network is enabled" END "temporarily_disabled",
CASE disabled WHEN "1" THEN "Network is disabled" ELSE "Network is enabled" END "disabled" 
FROM wifi_networks
WHERE 
network_name LIKE regex_match(network_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "network_name_pattern"), 0) AND 
security_type LIKE regex_match(security_type, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "security_type_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: WiFi Networks Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on processes with no threads. Normally, every process will have at least one thread running in its context. If a process is running with no threads, this indicates that this process cannot be deleted for some reason, for example, due to a buggy driver code. Such processes could impact the system performance and therefore should be investigated.

osQuery.label: windows_zero_thread_processes_monitoring
osQuery.name: Zero Thread Process Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.pid, p.name, p.path, p.cmdline, p.threads, h.sha256 
FROM processes p 
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=p.path
WHERE p.threads=0;

queries.title: Processes With Zero Threads Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. WMI class "Win32_ShadowCopy" contains information about VSS snapshots created on the host. This query collects such data from the host:
* DeviceObject - Windows object manager name for an underlying storage device that supports the original volume
* VolumeName - name of the original volume for which a shadow copy is made
* DriveLetter - drive letter assigned to a volume (NULL for volumes without drive letters) 
* ExposedLocally - the shadow copy is exposed on the local computer with a drive letter or mount point 
* ExposedName - file system name of a shadow copy when it is exposed
* ExposedPath - path to a shadow copy when it is exposed
* ExposedRemotely - the shadow copy is exposed on a remote computer with a network share
* ID - unique identifier for a shadow copy on the system
* install_date - date and time the object was installed
* OriginatingMachine - name of the computer that hosts the original volume
* Persistent - the shadow copy is persistent across reboots
* ServiceMachine - name of the computer that services the shadow copy

osQuery.label: windows_wmi_win32_shadow_copy_monitoring
osQuery.name: Win32_ShadowCopy Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
DeviceObject,
VolumeName,
Win32_Volume.DriveLetter,
CASE ExposedLocally
WHEN "0" THEN "False"
ELSE "True"
END exposed_locally,
ExposedName,
ExposedPath,
CASE ExposedRemotely
WHEN "0" THEN "False"
ELSE "True"
END exposed_remotely,
ID,
DATETIME(InstallDate, "unixepoch") AS install_date,
OriginatingMachine,
CASE Persistent
WHEN "0" THEN "False"
ELSE "True"
END persistent_across_reboots,
ServiceMachine
FROM Win32_ShadowCopy
JOIN Win32_Volume ON Win32_ShadowCopy.VolumeName=Win32_Volume.DeviceID
WHERE
install_date LIKE regex_match(install_date, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "install_date_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: WMI: Win32_ShadowCopy Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves such user account data as:
* username 
* user ID
* "Account Created" - time when the account was first created
* failed_login_count - the number of failed login attempts using an incorrect password. Count resets after a correct password is entered.
* "Last Failed Login Attempt" - the time of the last failed login attempt. Resets after a correct password is entered
* password_last_set_time - the time the password was last changed

osQuery.label: macos_account_policy_data_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Account Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
users.username, uid, DATETIME(creation_time, "unixepoch") as "Account Created", failed_login_count,
CASE failed_login_timestamp WHEN "0.0" THEN "0.0" ELSE DATETIME(failed_login_timestamp, "unixepoch") END "Last Failed Login Attempt",
DATETIME(password_last_set_time,  "unixepoch") AS "Password Last Changed"
FROM account_policy_data
JOIN users USING(uid)
WHERE
users.username LIKE regex_match(users.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: User Account Data Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. By default, the default file associating for .bat files is a command interpreter (cmd.exe). This could be changed by updating the ".bat\PersistentHandler" registry key allowing an application of your choice to be launched every time .bat file is executed. Malware can attempt to perform this modification in order to gain persistence or execution on infected host. This query retrieves systems where .bat file association is no longer set to default cmd.exe. These systems should be reviewed for potential security breach.

osQuery.label: registry_bat_persistencehandler_monitoring
osQuery.name: Batch Files PersistentHandler Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified 
FROM registry 
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\.bat\PersistentHandler" OR key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Classes\.bat\PersistentHandler") 
AND (data <> "{5e941d80-bf96-11cd-b579-08002b30bfeb}");

queries.title: Batch File Default Behavior Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Malware often attempts to modify the registry to add itself to the list of authorized applications in the Windows Firewall. This allows it to bypass Firewall rules and establish a command and control channel. Therefore, the list of authorized applications for Windows Firewall should be monitored.

osQuery.label: firewall_authorized_applications
osQuery.name: Firewall Authorized Applications
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT 
regex_match(LOWER(data), ".*\|app=([^|]+)\|.*", 1) AS "Authorized Application",
datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy\FirewallRules"
AND 
data LIKE "%action=allow%" AND 
data LIKE "%active=true%" AND 
data LIKE "%app=%";

queries.title: Firewall Authorized Applications Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It lists process and port information for processes accepting non-local incoming connections. The result include name of the process, full path for the process, local address, port, and protocol.

osQuery.label: listening_ports
osQuery.name: Listening Ports
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.path, lp.address, lp.protocol, lp.port 
FROM listening_ports lp LEFT JOIN processes p ON lp.pid = p.pid 
WHERE lp.port != 0 
AND lp.address!="127.0.0.1" 
AND lp.address!="::1" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\system32\%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/libexec/%";

queries.title: Listening Ports Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It retrieves the iptables configuration on the host. Data returned: 
* filter_name - packet matching filter table name
* chain - INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD 
* policy - policy that applies to this rule
* target - target that applies for this rule: ACCEPT, DROP
* protocol - protocol number identification
* source ip
* source mask
* source port
* destination ip
* destination mask
* destination port 
* input interface for the rule
* output interface for the rule
* match - matching rule that applies
* packets - number of matching packets for this rule
* bytes - number of matching bytes for this rule

osQuery.label: linux_iptables_monitoring
osQuery.name: IP Tables Configuration Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT filter_name, chain, policy, target, protocol, 
src_ip AS "source ip", src_mask AS "source mask", src_port AS "source port", 
dst_ip AS "destination ip", dst_mask AS "destination mask", dst_port AS "destination port",
iniface AS "input interface",
outiface AS "output interface",
match AS "matching rule",
packets, bytes
FROM iptables 
WHERE
chain LIKE regex_match(LOWER(chain), LOWER((SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="chain_type")), 0) AND 
src_ip LIKE regex_match(src_ip, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="source_ip"), 0) AND 
src_port LIKE regex_match(src_port, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="source_port"), 0) AND 
dst_ip LIKE regex_match(dst_ip, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="destination_ip"), 0) AND 
dst_port LIKE regex_match(dst_port, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="destination_port"), 0) AND 
iniface LIKE regex_match(LOWER(iniface), LOWER((SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="input_interface")), 0);

queries.title: IP Tables Configuration Monitoring
warnings: This query does not cover ip6tables only iptables.
This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux and MacOS. It retrieves all files currently opened by a specified process running on the host. Data displayed:
* name - process name
* pid - process ID 
* fd - process-specific file descriptor number
* path - filesystem path of descriptor

osQuery.label: macos_linux_process_open_files_monitoring
osQuery.name: Process Open Files Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.name, p.pid, pof.fd, pof.path
FROM process_open_files pof
LEFT JOIN processes p USING(pid)
WHERE
pof.path != "/proc/kmsg" AND
p.name LIKE regex_match(p.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "process_name_pattern"), 0) AND
pof.path LIKE regex_match(pof.path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "file_path_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Process Open Files Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.
By default this query retrieves results for every process running on the host. Use the parameter option for specific processes.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows event with ID "4771" is generated every time the Key Distribution Center fails to issue a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT). This issue may occur when the user's password has expired, or the wrong password was provided. Multiple events where the wrong password was provided can indicate a brute-force attack and should be investigated:
* datetime the event was generated
* target_user_name - the name of account, for which (TGT) ticket was requested 
* target_sid - security identifier of account object for which (TGT) ticket was requested
* ip - IP address of the computer from which the TGT request was requested
* port - source port number of client network connection
* failure_code - hexadecimal failure code of failed TGT issue operation
* full event data
Note that to collect these events the group policy "Kerberos Authentication Service" must be configured. These events are not collected by default in a domain environment.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_kerberos_preauthentication_fail
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_sid,
regex_match(data, 'IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip,
regex_match(data, 'IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) AS port,
regex_match(data, 'Status":"([^"]+)', 1) AS failure_code,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4771 AND
failure_code LIKE "0x18";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Wrong Password Kerberos Pre-Authentication Fails

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs generated when Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) database "ntds.dit" was accessed. Such events could indicate an adversary attempting to create a "ntds.dit" copy for offline analysis or modification and should be investigated:
* datetime event was generated
* event id
* full event data

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_ntds_database_access
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
eventid,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Application" AND
eventid IN (326, 325, 327) AND
data LIKE "%ntds%";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs NTDS Database Access

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows and it is designed to detect powershell scripts checking for the presence of common VMWare and VirtualBox process names. Malware often uses such scripts to determine if it is running within a sandboxed environment and adjusts its behavior. This query returns:
* datetime - system time at which the Powershell script event occurred
* script_block_count - the total number of script blocks for this script
* script_block_id - the unique GUID of the powershell script to which this block belongs
* script_name - the name for the powershell script
* script_path - the path for the powershell script
* script_text - the text content of the powershell script
* time - time stamp the event was received by the osquery event publisher

osQuery.label: powershell_sandbox_detection
osQuery.name: Powershell Events
osQuery.sql:
SELECT datetime, time, script_block_count, script_block_id, script_name, script_path, script_text
FROM orbital_powershell_events 
WHERE script_text LIKE "%get-process%" AND
(script_text LIKE "%VBoxTray%" OR 
script_text LIKE "Proxifier%" OR 
script_text LIKE "%prl_cc%" OR 
script_text LIKE "%prl_tools%" OR 
script_text LIKE "%vmusrvc%" OR 
script_text LIKE "%vmsrvc%" OR 
script_text LIKE "%vmtoolsd%");

queries.title: Powershell Sandbox Detection

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. WMI class "SoftwareLicensingService" contains data about the Software Licensing Service. This query retrieves information related to Windows Product Activation key. Data displayed are:
* OA3xOriginalProductKey - Product Key
* OA3xOriginalProductKeyDescription - Product Key Description
* Version -  The version of the Software Licensing service

osQuery.label: windows_software_properties_monitoring
osQuery.name: Software Licensing Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT OA3xOriginalProductKey, OA3xOriginalProductKeyDescription, Version
FROM SoftwareLicensingService;

queries.title: Software Licensing Service Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Microsoft Office contains templates that are part of common Office applications and are used to customize styles. These templates are loaded each time the Office application starts. Malware could modify or replace the Office base templates in order to establish persistence on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_office_default_templates_monitoring
osQuery.name: Office Default Templates Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.path, f.filename, h.sha256, f.uid, f.gid, f.mode, f.size,
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time,
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified,
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time,
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS creation_time
FROM file f
JOIN hash h ON f.path=h.path
WHERE (
f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Templates\Normal.dotm" OR
f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Excel\XLSTART\PERSONAL.XLSB");

queries.title: Windows Office Default Templates Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on AntiVirus solutions installed on the system. The following data is displayed:
* Display name 
* Path to signed product executable
* Path to signed reporting executable

osQuery.label: windows_av_products_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows AntiVirus Products Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
displayName, 
pathToSignedProductExe AS "Path To Signed Executable", 
pathToSignedReportingExe AS "Path To Signed Reporting Executable"
FROM AntiVirusProduct;

queries.title: Windows AntiVirus Products Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves registry data on the system current mapped network drives. Malware often uses network shares to probe for other machines on the network, collect data about the target network, and to spread on other hosts.

osQuery.label: registry_network_shares_monitoring
osQuery.name: Network Registry Key

queries.title: Registry Network Shares Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves data on Network File System (NFS) shares set up on the host:
* share - filesystem path to the share
* options - options string set on the export share
* readonly - if the share is exported readonly

osQuery.label: macos_nfs_shares_monitoring
osQuery.name: NTF Shares
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
share, options,
CASE readonly
WHEN "1" THEN "True"
ELSE "False" END readonly
FROM nfs_shares
WHERE
readonly LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "readonly_status");

queries.title: Network File System Mounts Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves events indicating that a process requested access to the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (lsass.exe) process memory. Even though computer accounts do require access to lsass process object for legitimate reasons events when the access was requested by the user should be reviewed for potential compromise.
Seach suggestion:
* To view event logs 4633 that were generated by computer accounts set the field "subject_user_name" value to ".*".
Comment:
* Events with ID 4633 are not enabled by default. Policy "Audit Kernel Object" must be configured to generate such events.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_lsass_dump_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'ProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "//", "/") AS process_name,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4663 AND
subject_user_name LIKE regex_match(subject_user_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "subject_user_name_pattern"), 0) AND
process_name LIKE regex_match(process_name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "process_name_pattern"), 0) AND
object_name LIKE "%lsass%";

queries.title: Windows Event Logs for LSASS Dump Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It can detect COM objects' execution by powershell when powershell is executed from command-line.  Following data is retrieved:
* datetime - Date and time of the event as stated in eventlogs
* new_process_name - Newly created process name e.g certutil.exe
* new_process_path - Full path of the new process
* parent_process_name - Parent process name only e.g. cmd.exe
* parent_process_path - Full path of the new process
* new_process_commandline - New process commandline
* data - Eventlog data

osQuery.label: windows_powershell_execute_com_object
osQuery.name: COM object executions by powershell
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
LOWER(replace(regex_match(data, 'NewProcessName":"([^"]+)\\([^"]+)', 2), "\\", "\")) AS new_process_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'NewProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "\\", "\") AS new_process_path, 
LOWER(replace(regex_match(data, 'ParentProcessName":"([^"]+)\\([^"]+)', 2), "\\", "\")) AS parent_process_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'ParentProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "\\", "\") AS parent_process_path,
replace(regex_match(data, 'CommandLine":"(.+?),"', 1), "\\", "\") AS new_process_commandline,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4688 AND
new_process_commandline LIKE regex_match(LOWER(new_process_commandline), '.*gettypefromclsid.*[a-f0-9]{8}-[a-f0-9]{4}-[a-f0-9]{4}-[a-f0-9]{4}-[a-f0-9]{12}.*shellexecute.*',0);

queries.title: Powershell Execute COM Object Monitoring
warnings: This query searches process data from Windows eventlog's audit logs. If the Windows's process event/audit logging is disabled, then this query will not return any data.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the status of the optional features such as "Hyper-V" or "Windows Search" that are present on the system. The data displayed are:
* Caption - an optional feature display name
* Name - name of the feature
* FeatureStatus - Enabled, Disabled, Absent, Unknown

osQuery.label: windows_optional_features_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Optional Features Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT Caption, Name, 
CASE InstallState
WHEN "1" THEN "Enabled"
WHEN "2" THEN "Disabled"
WHEN "3" THEN "Absent"
WHEN "4" THEN "Unknown"
END FeatureStatus
FROM Win32_OptionalFeature
WHERE 
FeatureStatus LIKE regex_match(FeatureStatus, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="feature_status"), 0)
AND 
Name LIKE regex_match(Name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="name_value"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Optional Features Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Event with ID "10016" generated when Microsoft components try to access DCOM components without the required permissions. In most cases this event can be safely ignored; however, events with ID "10016" where the application was not granted Remote Launch permissions could indicate a lateral movement attempt and should be investigated. This query returns such events.

osQuery.label: windows_dcom_remote_launch_permission_denied_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
regex_match(data, '"param6":"([^"]+)', 1) AS UserName,
regex_match(data, '"param7":"([^"]+)', 1) AS UserDomain,
regex_match(data, '"param8":"([^"]+)', 1) AS UserSID,
regex_match(data, '"param4":"([^"]+)', 1) AS CLSID,
regex_match(data, '"param5":"([^"]+)', 1) AS APPID,
data AS Message,
datetime AS TimeGenerated,
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "System" AND
eventid = 10016 AND
data LIKE "%Remote Launch%" AND
datetime LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern");

queries.title: Windows Events for Remote Launch Permissions Denied Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. A Windows Event Log with eventid "4624" and "LogonType" 9 is generated on the system when a new logon session provided different credentials for outbound connections. For example, if a local process requires access to a resource on some remote system, but the process owner does not have the authorization to do so, then the process must supply the appropriate credentials in order to access this resource. This can be achieved by spawning a process using the "runas.exe /netonly" option, which allows the user to specify particular credentials for the process to use for remote access. When a process attempts to access a remote resource using credentials which are different from that of the process owner, then an Event Log from above will be generated on the host (local) system.

osQuery.label: new_credentials_logons_monitoring
osQuery.name: New Credentials Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4624 AND
data LIKE '%"LogonType":"9"%';

queries.title: New Credentials Logons Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves remote Windows logon events with "LogonID" field matching the "LogonID" field for Windows Events "4697" generated when a new service was installed on the host. The group policy "Security System Extension" must be enabled to log events "4697". These events are not logged by default.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_remote_service_created
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectLogonId":"([^"]+)', 1) AS logon_id,
regex_match(data, 'LogonType":"([^"]+)', 1) AS logon_type,
regex_match(data, 'IpAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip_address,
regex_match(data, 'IpPort":"([^"]+)', 1) AS ip_port, 
data 
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4624 AND
logon_id IN (
SELECT 
regex_match(data, 'SubjectLogonId":"([^"]+)', 1) AS logon_id 
FROM windows_eventlog 
WHERE channel="Security" AND eventid = 4697) AND
logon_type IN (3, 7, 10);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Service Created Remotely

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. On Windows, the Service Control Manager (SCM) implemented by services.exe launches Service Host (services.exe) processes, each of which can host one or more Windows services by loading the service DLLs. Malware can masquerade as "svchost.exe" by renaming itself or by launching it for process hollowing. One way to distinguish between a legitimate and fake service host process is to check for the parent process that started the suspicious executable. In case of svchost.exe, the parent should always be services.exe.

osQuery.label: unusual_svchost_parent_process_tmp
osQuery.name: Svchost Parent Process Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE 
TEMPORARY TABLE parent_child AS 
SELECT 
child_processes.pid AS child_pid,
child_processes.name AS child_name,
parent_processes.pid AS parent_pid,
parent_processes.name AS parent_name 
FROM processes child_processes 
INNER JOIN processes parent_processes ON child_processes.parent=parent_processes.pid;

osQuery.label: unusual_svchost_parent_process
osQuery.name: Svchost Parent Process
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
child_pid, child_name, parent_pid, parent_name 
FROM parent_child 
WHERE 
LOWER(child_name) IN ("svchost.exe") AND 
parent_name != "services.exe";

queries.title: Unusual Svchost Parent Process

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieves data on Python package specified by the user.

osQuery.label: python_packages_search
osQuery.name: Python Packages
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version, summary, author, license, path, directory
FROM python_packages
WHERE name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="python_package_name")
AND author LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="python_package_author")
AND license LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="python_package_license");

queries.title: Python Packages Search

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Every time an application is run on the host, a prefetch file which contains the information about the files loaded by the application is created by the Windows Operating System (OS). This prefetch file will be used for optimizing the loading time of the application next time it will be loaded. SuperFetch feature attempts to predict which applications the user will launch next and preloads all of the necessary data into memory. The "PrefetchParameters" registry key contains settings for both: "Prefetch" and "SuperFetch". The regular user have no reason to modify these settings from its default values. This query retrieves data related to Prefetch and SuperFetch registry settings.

osQuery.label: prefetch_registry_key
osQuery.name: Prefetch Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management\PrefetchParameters" 
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet002\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management\PrefetchParameters"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management\PrefetchParameters";

queries.title: Prefetch Registry Key Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows event with ID "4765" is generated when an account's SID history was modified. An attacker may attempt to update a compromised user's SID history in order to gain access to certain domain resources:
* datetime the event was generated
* source_user_name - name of the account performing the SID history update
* source_sid - account's security identifier
* target_user_name - name of the account whose SID history was updated
* target_domain_name - account's domain
* full event data

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_sid_history_updated
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SourceUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS source_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SourceSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS source_sid,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_domain_name,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4765;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: SID History Updated

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It monitors Firefox addons installed on the host. The following data retrieved:
* username - username of a user that owns the addon
* name - addon display name
* identifier - addon identifier
* creator - addon-supported creator string
* type - extension, addon, webapp
* version
* description - addon-supplied description string
* source_url - URL that installed the addon
* visible - if addon is shown in the browser
* active - if addon is active
* disabled - if addon is application-disabled
* autoupdate - if addon applied background updates
* native - if addon includes binary components
* location - global, profile location
* path - path to plugin bundle

osQuery.label: windows_macos_linux_firefox_addons_monitoring
osQuery.name: Firefox Browser Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, f.name, f.identifier, f.creator, f.type, f.version, f.description, f.source_url,
CASE f.visible WHEN "1" THEN "Addon is shown in browser" ELSE "Addon is not shown is browser" END "visible",
CASE f.active WHEN "1" THEN "Addon is active" ELSE "Addon is not active" END "active",
CASE f.disabled WHEN "1" THEN "Application-disabled" ELSE "Not application-disabled" END "disabled",
CASE f.autoupdate WHEN "1" THEN "Addon applies background updates" ELSE "Addon does not apply background updates" END "autoupdate",
"N/A" AS "native",
f.location, f.path 
FROM users u
CROSS JOIN firefox_addons f USING (uid) WHERE
u.username LIKE regex_match(u.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0) AND
f.name LIKE regex_match(f.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Firefox Addons Monitoring

before560.label: windows_macos_linux_firefox_addons_monitoring
before560.name: Firefox Browser Data
before560.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, f.name, f.identifier, f.creator, f.type, f.version, f.description, f.source_url,
CASE f.visible WHEN "1" THEN "Addon is shown in browser" ELSE "Addon is not shown is browser" END "visible",
CASE f.active WHEN "1" THEN "Addon is active" ELSE "Addon is not active" END "active",
CASE f.disabled WHEN "1" THEN "Application-disabled" ELSE "Not application-disabled" END "disabled",
CASE f.autoupdate WHEN "1" THEN "Addon applies background updates" ELSE "Addon does not apply background updates" END "autoupdate",
CASE f.native WHEN "1" THEN "Addon includes binary components" ELSE "Addon does not include binary components" END "native",
f.location, f.path 
FROM users u
CROSS JOIN firefox_addons f USING (uid) WHERE
u.username LIKE regex_match(u.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0) AND
