f.name LIKE regex_match(f.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0);

queries.description: This queries is applicable to Linux. It is designed to capture volatile and non-volatile data from the endpoint that can help with forensic investigations.

osQuery.label: hosts_file
osQuery.name: Hosts File Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
address, hostnames 
FROM etc_hosts 
WHERE 
hostnames NOT IN ("localhost", "::1", "fe00::0", "ff00::0", "ff02::1", "ff02::2");

osQuery.label: sha256_hash_of_running_processes
osQuery.name: SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.pid, p.name, p.path, p.cmdline, p.state,  h.sha256 
FROM processes p 
INNER JOIN hash h 
ON p.path=h.path;

osQuery.label: interface_names_and_associated_ips
osQuery.name: Interface Names And Associated IPs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
interface, address, mask, type, friendly_name 
FROM interface_addresses 
WHERE 
interface NOT LIKE "lo%" AND 
interface!=1;

osQuery.label: process_running_without_a_binary_on_disk
osQuery.name: Process Running Without A Binary On Disk
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
pid, name, path, cmdline, state 
FROM processes 
WHERE 
on_disk=0;

osQuery.label: logged_in_users
osQuery.name: Logged In Users
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
user, type as "login_type", tty as "device_name", host as "remote_hostname", DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") AS time, pid, sid  
FROM logged_in_users
WHERE 
user!="";

osQuery.label: disk_encryption_monitoring
osQuery.name: Disk Encryption Status and Details
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
de.name, de.uuid, de.encrypted, de.type, u.username, de.user_uuid, de.encryption_status 
FROM disk_encryption de
LEFT JOIN users u 
ON de.uid=u.uid;

osQuery.label: operating_system_attributes
osQuery.name: Operating System Attributes

osQuery.label: listening_ports
osQuery.name: Listening Ports
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.name, p.path, lp.address, lp.protocol, lp.port 
FROM listening_ports lp LEFT JOIN processes p ON lp.pid = p.pid 
WHERE 
lp.port != 0 
AND lp.address!="127.0.0.1" 
AND lp.address!="::1" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/libexec/%";

osQuery.label: user_accounts
osQuery.name: User Accounts

osQuery.label: usb_devices
osQuery.name: USB Devices

osQuery.label: processes_with_network_connections
osQuery.name: Processes With Network Connections
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT pos.pid, p.name, p.cmdline, pos.local_address, pos.local_port, pos.remote_address, pos.remote_port 
FROM processes p 
JOIN process_open_sockets pos USING (pid)
WHERE 
pos.remote_address NOT IN ("", "0.0.0.0", "127.0.0.1", "::", "::1", "0");

osQuery.label: user_groups_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Groups
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
g.groupname, u.username, g.comment
FROM groups g
LEFT JOIN user_groups ug ON g.gid=ug.gid
LEFT JOIN users u ON ug.uid=u.uid;

osQuery.label: mounted_devices_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Mounted Devices and Filesystems

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE loaded_modules AS 
SELECT 
DISTINCT(path) 
FROM process_memory_map;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE loaded_module_hashes AS 
SELECT 
d.rowid AS module_id, h.sha256 AS sha256, d.path AS module_path 
FROM hash h 
INNER JOIN loaded_modules d ON d.path=h.path;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_processes
osQuery.name: Processes

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_vs_processes
osQuery.name: Loaded Module ID To Process ID
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT lh.module_id, pmm.pid AS pid 
FROM process_memory_map pmm 
JOIN loaded_module_hashes lh ON lh.module_path=pmm.path;

osQuery.label: arp_cache
osQuery.name: ARP Cache
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
address, mac, interface, permanent
FROM
arp_cache;

osQuery.label: chrome_extensions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Chrome Browser Extensions Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, ce.name, ce.identifier, ce.version, ce.description, ce.locale, ce.update_url, ce.persistent, ce.path
FROM users u
CROSS JOIN chrome_extensions ce USING (uid);

osQuery.label: crontab
osQuery.name: Crontab
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
event, minute, hour, day_of_month, month, day_of_week,
command, path
FROM
crontab;

osQuery.label: macos_linux_dns_resolvers_monitoring
osQuery.name: DNS Resolvers

osQuery.label: etc_services
osQuery.name: Services File
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, port, protocol, aliases, comment
FROM
etc_services;

osQuery.label: etc_protocols
osQuery.name: Protocols File
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, number, alias, comment
FROM
etc_protocols;

osQuery.label: linux_iptables_monitoring
osQuery.name: IP Tables Configuration Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
filter_name, chain, policy, target, protocol, 
src_ip AS "source ip", src_mask AS "source mask", src_port AS "source port", 
dst_ip AS "destination ip", dst_mask AS "destination mask", dst_port AS "destination port",
iniface AS "input interface",
outiface AS "output interface",
match AS "matching rule",
packets, bytes
FROM iptables;

osQuery.label: linux_kernel_modules_monitoring
osQuery.name: Kernel Modules

osQuery.label: macos_linux_known_hosts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Known Hosts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
u.username, kn.uid, kn.key, kn.key_file
FROM users u
JOIN known_hosts kn USING(uid);

osQuery.label: macos_linux_last_logins_logouts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Logins/Logouts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
l.username, l.tty, l.pid,
p.name AS "process name",
l.type,
DATETIME(l.time, "unixepoch") AS "entry timestamp",
l.host as hostname
FROM last l
LEFT JOIN processes p ON p.pid = l.pid;

osQuery.label: platform_information
osQuery.name: Platform Information

osQuery.label: macos_linux_process_envs_monitoring
osQuery.name: Process Environment Variables Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
p.name AS "Process Name", p.pid,
pe.key AS "Environment Variable Name",
pe.value AS "Environment Variable Value"
FROM process_envs pe
JOIN processes p ON p.pid=pe.pid;

osQuery.label: shadow
osQuery.name: Shadow File
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
password_status, hash_alg, last_change,
min, max, warning, inactive,
expire, username 
FROM shadow;

osQuery.label: shell_history
osQuery.name: Shell History
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, sh.time, sh.command, sh.history_file
FROM users u
CROSS JOIN shell_history sh USING (uid);

osQuery.label: ssh_configs_monitoring
osQuery.name: SSH Configuration Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, sc.block, sc.option, sc.ssh_config_file, h.sha256
FROM users u
JOIN ssh_configs sc USING(uid)
JOIN hash h ON h.path = sc.ssh_config_file;

osQuery.label: startup_items
osQuery.name: Startup Items

osQuery.label: macos_linux_sudoers_monitoring
osQuery.name: Sudoers Data

osQuery.label: macos_linux_writable_suid_bin_monitoring
osQuery.name: Writable Suid Binaries Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.path, sb.username, sb.groupname, f.mode, sb.permissions
FROM suid_bin sb
LEFT JOIN file f ON sb.path = f.path
WHERE
sb.permissions LIKE regex_match(sb.permissions, "(S|G)", 0) AND
f.mode LIKE regex_match(f.mode, ".*(2|3|6|7)$", 0);

osQuery.label: system_information
osQuery.name: System Information

osQuery.label: ssh_user_keys_monitoring
osQuery.name: User SSH Keys Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
u.username, usk.path, usk.encrypted, sha256
FROM users u
JOIN user_ssh_keys usk
USING (uid)
JOIN hash h ON h.path=usk.path;

osQuery.label: linux_systemd_units_monitoring
osQuery.name: Systemd Units
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
id, description, load_state, active_state, sub_state, following,
object_path, job_id, job_type, job_path,
fragment_path, user, source_path
FROM
systemd_units;

queries.title: System Snapshot Linux 0.0.9 (formerly Forensic Snapshot Linux 0.0.9)
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Context Menu is the Windows GUI that appears upon user interaction - usually in the event of the mouse right-click. Entries for the context menu are recorded in the registry under "HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT" and could be modified to establish persistence on the host. This query retrieves such entries. Collected data should be reviewed to prevent execution of malicious code.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_context_menu_entries
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
key, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
path LIKE "HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\Directory\Background\Shell\%\command\%" AND
data LIKE regex_match(data, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "data_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Registry Context Menu Entries Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Subsystem registration for the Windows Session Manager is handled by the Registry. Modifications to the value in the SubSystems\Windows subkey could be an indication that malware is attempting to gain execution with high privileges by registering itself as a Session Manager subsystem.

osQuery.label: registry_session_manager_subsystem_monitoring
osQuery.name: Session Manager SubSystem Monitoring Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key, path, name, type, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch")
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\SubSystems" AND name="Windows"
AND (data <> "%SystemRoot%\system32\csrss.exe ObjectDirectory=\Windows SharedSection=1024,20480,768 Windows=On SubSystemType=Windows ServerDll=basesrv,1 ServerDll=winsrv:UserServerDllInitialization,3 ServerDll=sxssrv,4 ProfileControl=Off MaxRequestThreads=16"
AND data <> "%SystemRoot%\system32\csrss.exe ObjectDirectory=\Windows SharedSection=1024,20480,768 Windows=On SubSystemType=Windows ServerDll=basesrv,1 ServerDll=winsrv:UserServerDllInitialization,3 ServerDll=winsrv:ConServerDllInitialization,2 ServerDll=sxssrv,4 ProfileControl=Off MaxRequestThreads=16"
AND data <> "%SystemRoot%\system32\csrss.exe ObjectDirectory=\Windows SharedSection=1024,20480,2048 Windows=On SubSystemType=Windows ServerDll=basesrv,1 ServerDll=winsrv:UserServerDllInitialization,3 ServerDll=winsrv:ConServerDllInitialization,2 ServerDll=sxssrv,4 ProfileControl=Off MaxRequestThreads=16"));

queries.title: Session Manager SubSystem Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux and MacOS. It retrieves data on logins/logouts from the host. Data displayed:
* username - entry username
* tty - entry terminal
* pid - process ID
* process name 
* type - entry type, according to ut_type types (utmp.h)
* time - entry timestamp
* hostname - entry hostname

osQuery.label: macos_linux_last_logins_logouts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Logins/Logouts Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
l.username, l.tty, l.pid, 
p.name AS "process name", 
l.type, 
DATETIME(l.time, "unixepoch") AS "entry timestamp", 
l.host as hostname
FROM last l
LEFT JOIN processes p ON p.pid = l.pid
WHERE 
l.username LIKE regex_match(l.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Logins/Logouts Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Volume Shadow Copies Service (VSS) is a Windows service that provides functionality for backing up critical system files to assist with the system recovery. Volume Shadow Copy (VSC) is a system snapshot at a given point in time. The "FilesNotToSnapshot" registry key contains the list of files that should be deleted from the newly created VSC. Malware may attempt to add specific files to the "FilesNotToSnapshot" list in order to evade detection. This query retrieves all files specified in this list.

osQuery.label: registry_backup_restore_monitoring
osQuery.name: BackupRestore Registry Key

queries.title: Backup/Restore Registry Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. The table "windows_security_products" enumerates the registered Windows security products. This query monitors if the registered Windows security products are disabled or not. The following data is retrieved: 
  * name - name of security product
  * type - type of security product
  * state - state of protection
  * state_timestamp - timestamp for the product state
  * remediation_path - remediation path
  * signatures_up_to_date - 1 if product signatures are up to date, else 0

osQuery.label: windows_security_products_monitoring
osQuery.name: Enumerates Windows Security Products
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, type, state, state_timestamp,remediation_path, signatures_up_to_date 
FROM windows_security_products
WHERE
state LIKE regex_match(state, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "state_value"), 0);

techniques.T1562.001: Impair Defenses - Disable or Modify Tools

queries.title: Windows Security Products Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data from the Application Compatibility Cache (Shim Cache):
* entry - execution order
* path to the executed file
* file modified time
* sha256 of the executed file if it is still present on the host

osQuery.label: windows_shimcache_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shim Cache
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
entry,
path,
CASE modified_time
WHEN "0" THEN "0"
ELSE DATETIME(modified_time, "unixepoch")
END file_modified_time,
h.sha256
FROM shimcache
LEFT JOIN hash h USING(path)
WHERE
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0) AND
file_modified_time LIKE regex_match(file_modified_time, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "file_modified_time_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Shim Cache Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Malware could disable the Task Manager to prevent the user from terminating suspicious programs. This query monitors the registry for such modifications.

osQuery.label: disabled_task_manager_registry
osQuery.name: Disabled Task Manager Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System" 
OR key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System") 
AND name LIKE "DisableTaskMgr" AND data = "1";

queries.title: Disabled Task Manager Registry Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. CVE-2021-36934 exists due to non-administrative users being granted read access to files in the "%windir%\system32\config" directory which allows local privilege escalation (LPE). This query retrieves NTFS access control lists (ACL) for "%windir%\system32\config" directory for groups that should not have access to it by default:
* path to the file or directory
* type of access mode for the access control entry
* principal - user or group to which the access control entry (ACE) applies
* access - specific permissions that indicate the rights described by the ACE
* inherited_from - the inheritance policy of the ACE

osQuery.label: windows_ntfs_acl_permissions_cve_2021_36934
osQuery.name: NTFS ACL Permissions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, type, principal, access, inherited_from
FROM ntfs_acl_permissions
WHERE
path = (SELECT value FROM orbital_environment WHERE name = "windir") || "\System32\config" AND
principal LIKE regex_match(principal, "^((?!(SYSTEM|Administrators|TrustedInstaller|CREATOR OWNER)).)*$", 0);

techniques.T1222.001: Windows File and Directory Permissions Modification

queries.title: CVE-2021-36934: Unprivileged User Access to System32\config Files

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Log with Event ID "4796" generated when Directory Services Restore Mode (DSRM) password is changed. Such event is present only on domain controllers and every log should be investigated for suspicious activity.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_dsrm_password_changed
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventCode,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4794;

queries.title: Directory Services Restore Mode (DSRM) Password Changed

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Winlogon\Shell registry key is responsible for launching explorer.exe. This key could be modified so that Command Prompt or any other application opens up at every login, and automatically executes a rogue program. This query retrieves data related to this key.

osQuery.label: winlogon_shell_registry
osQuery.name: Winlogon Shell Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch")
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\winlogon"
AND name LIKE "shell";

queries.title: Winlogon Shell Registry Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. User Access Control (UAC) remote restrictions feature defends the system from loopback attacks and from executing code laterally within the target network. Malware may attempt to disable this feature through registry modifications in order to perform the pass the hash attack for lateral movement in the target environment.

osQuery.label: uac_remote_restrictions_disabled
osQuery.name: UAC Remote Restrictions Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\microsoft\windows\currentversion\policies\system"
AND name LIKE "LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy"
AND data LIKE "1";

queries.title: User Access Control Restrictions Disabled

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Events with ID "4768" are generated when a Kerberos authentication ticket (TGT) was requested. This event could be used to monitor actions of high-value accounts, non-active accounts or external accounts. The Group Policy "Audit Kerberos Authentication Service" must be enabled to log these events.
Search suggestions:
* Track all 4768 events where "ip_address" is not from your internal IP range
* Set parameter "ip_address_pattern" to "^((?!(127.0.0.1|-|::1)).)*$" to filter out local authentication events 
* "ip_address" set to "::1" means local authentication. If the list of accounts allowed to log on to the domain controller is known use this field and "target_user_name" to check for possible violations
* use "status" field to monitor events where TGT requests failed. By default, "status" equal to "0x0" (no error) is filtered out.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_kerberos_tgt_requests
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, '.*TargetUserName":"([^"]+).*', 1) as target_user_name,
regex_match(data, '.*TargetDomainName":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS target_domain_name,
regex_match(data, '.*ServiceName":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS service_name,
regex_match(data, '.*Status":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS status,
regex_match(data, '.*IpAddress":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS ip_address, 
regex_match(data, '.*IpPort":"([^"]+).*', 1) AS ip_port,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4768 AND
status LIKE regex_match(status, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "status_pattern"), 0) AND
ip_address LIKE regex_match(ip_address, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "ip_address_pattern"), 0) AND 
ip_port LIKE regex_match(ip_port, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "ip_port_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Events for Kerberos Authentication Ticket Requests

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows and MacOS. It retrieves data from the active route table on the host based on the pattern specified. The data returned are:
* destination IP address
* netmask length 
* gateway IP
* source IP address
* flags - shows a collection of information about the route stored as binary choices (currently implemented only for Linux and MacOS) 
* interface
* mtu - maximum transmission unit for the route
* metric - cost of route, lower is preferred<F3>
* type - type of route (local, gateway, dymanic, modified, statis, blackhole, router, proxy)

osQuery.label: routes_table
osQuery.name: Routes Table
osQuery.sql:
SELECT destination, netmask, gateway, source, flags, interface, mtu, metric, type
FROM routes
WHERE destination NOT IN ( "::1","::","fe80::", "ff00::")
AND destination NOT LIKE "255.%" 
AND destination NOT LIKE "224.%" 
AND destination NOT LIKE "127.%";

queries.title: System Route Table Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. When a user logs on a domain, Windows caches their credentials to be able to potentially authenticate them in the absence of the domain controller. If a user attempts to logon with domain account while the domain controller is not available, the local cache is used and an event of type id "4624" and "LogonType" set to 11 is generated if the logon is successful.

osQuery.label: cachedinteractive_logons_monitoring
osQuery.name: CachedInteractive Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type" 
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE 
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4624 AND
data LIKE '%"LogonType":"11"%';

queries.title: Cached Interactive Logons Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. XProtect is a built-in anti-malware protection. This query retrieves all XProtect signatures from the host:
* name - description of XProtected malware
* launch_type - launch services content type
* identity - XProtect identity (SHA1) of content
* filename - use this file name to match
* filetype - use this file type to match
* optional - match any of the identities/patterns for this XProtect name
* uses_pattern - uses a match pattern instead of identity

osQuery.label: macos_xprotect_entries_monitoring
osQuery.name: XProtect Signatures
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, launch_type, identity, filename, filetype, optional, uses_pattern
FROM xprotect_entries
WHERE
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
filename LIKE regex_match(filename, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "filename_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Database of XProtect Signatures Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves package install history from the host:
* package_id - label packageIdentifiers
* package installation time
* name - package display name
* version - package display version
* source - install source: usually the installer process name
* content_type - package content_type (optional)

osQuery.label: macos_package_install_history_monitoring
osQuery.name: Package Install History Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
package_id, DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") AS "install_time", name, version, source, content_type
FROM
package_install_history
WHERE
package_id LIKE regex_match(package_id, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "package_id_pattern"), 0) AND
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
install_time LIKE regex_match(install_time, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "install_time_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Package Install History Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Port Monitors consist of Dynamic Link Libraries. They are responsible for providing a communications path between the user-mode print spooler and the kernel-mode port drivers, and loaded by the print spooler service on boot. The registry key "Print\Monitors" is the location where these libraries are specified. Malware could modify these registry keys and add malicious DLLs to it in order to establish persistence on the host. This query retrieves port monitors registry data from the system.

osQuery.label: windows_printer_port_monitors
osQuery.name: Printer Port Monitors Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified 
FROM registry
WHERE (
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors\Local Port" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors\Standard TCP/IP Port" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors\USB Monitor" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Print\Monitors\WSD Port")
AND name="Driver";

queries.title: Windows Printer Port Monitors

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. File extensions are registered with default handlers in the Registry. An adversary may modify the default value for the .cmd handler in order to execute malicious code whenever .cmd file is executed. This query retrieves systems where file association for .cmd files has been changed.

osQuery.label: registry_cmd_persistenthandler_monitoring
osQuery.name: Command Line PersistentHandler Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key, path, name, type, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch")
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Classes\.cmd\PersistentHandler" OR key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\Classes\.cmd\PersistentHandler")
AND (data <> "{5e941d80-bf96-11cd-b579-08002b30bfeb}");

queries.title: Command Line PersistentHandler Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. "CVE-2020-1206" is an information disclosure vulnerability that exists in the way that the Microsoft Server Message Block 3.1.1 (SMBv3) protocol handles certain requests. An attacker who successfully exploited the vulnerability could obtain information to further compromise the user's system. This query retrieves "CVE-2020-1206" vulnerability status on the host based on Windows version and patches installed on the system.

osQuery.label: windows_cve_2020_1206_monitoring
osQuery.name: CVE-2020-1206 Vulnerability Status
osQuery.sql:
WITH 
b1 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, name FROM os_version),
b2 AS (SELECT DISTINCT 1 AS one, CASE WHEN EXISTS(SELECT hotfix_id FROM patches WHERE (hotfix_id LIKE "KB4560960" OR hotfix_id LIKE "KB4557957")) THEN "True" ELSE "False" END "PatchesApplied" FROM patches),
b3 AS (SELECT 1 AS one, build FROM os_version)
SELECT b1.name, b2.PatchesApplied, b3.build, 
CASE WHEN b2.PatchesApplied LIKE "False" AND b3.build IN ("18363", "19041", "18362") 
THEN "Vulnerable" 
ELSE "Not Vulnerable" 
END "CVEStatus" 
FROM b1 JOIN b2 USING(one) JOIN b3 USING(one);

queries.title: CVE-2020-1206 Vulnerability Status Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. DCSync is an attack that allows an attacker to simulate the behaviour of Domain Controller (DC) in order to retrieve password data via domain replication. Once an attacker has access to a privileged account with domain replication rights, the attacker can utilize protocols to mimic a domain controller. When Mimikatz is used for DCSync it has specific behaviour that can be detected with Windows Event Logs. Specifically, Windows Event with ID "4662", Properties set to "1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2" or to "Replicating Directory Changes All", and "Account Name" not set to "NT AUTHORITY" or not ending with "$" could help identify Mimikatz being used to run DCSync attack. This query searches for hosts where such events were logged. Found systems should be investigated.

osQuery.label: windows_dcsync_mimikatz_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
data AS Message,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS Account_Name,
regex_match(data, '"Properties":"([^"]+)', 1) AS Access_Type,
datetime AS "Time Generated",
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4662 AND
(Access_Type LIKE "%Replicating Directory Changes All%" OR Access_Type LIKE "%1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2%") AND
Account_Name NOT LIKE "NT Authority" AND
Account_Name NOT LIKE "%$" AND
datetime LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern");

queries.title: Mimikatz DCSync Events Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Local Security Authority (LSA) process is responsible for enforcing the security policy on the system. At system startup, LSA loads Windows Security Support Provider (SSP) DLLs in order to perform security related operations. Malware can persist across reboots by adding a malicious SSP DLL. This query returns a list of registered SSP DLLs - any change in the list should be investigated.

osQuery.label: windows_security_support_providers_monitoring
osQuery.name: SSP Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE (
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\OSConfig")
AND name LIKE "Security Packages";

queries.title: Security Support Providers Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on "Installed Components" registry key. This key contains subkeys that are GUIDs, each GUID contains "Version" and "StubPath" values. Every time the user logs in, the content of this key will be compared with the same key under "HKEY_CURRENT_USER" (HKCU). If the same key does not exist or the "Version" value is less than that in "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE" (HKLM) then the "StubPath" command will be run and the key will be copied under HKCU so it is not run again. Malware could modify "StubPath" value to run at the next user logon.

osQuery.label: registry_installed_components_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry Installed Components Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components\%"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432NODE\Microsoft\Active Setup\Installed Components\%";

queries.title: Registry Installed Components Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows.It is designed to capture volatile and non-volatile data from the endpoint that can help with forensics investigations.

osQuery.label: hosts_file
osQuery.name: Hosts File Data

osQuery.label: sha256_hash_of_running_processes
osQuery.name: SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.pid, p.name, p.path, h.sha256 
FROM processes p INNER JOIN hash h ON p.path=h.path;

osQuery.label: mapped_drives
osQuery.name: Mapped Drives

osQuery.label: interface_names_and_associated_ips
osQuery.name: Interface Names And Associated IPs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT interface, address 
FROM interface_addresses 
WHERE interface NOT LIKE "lo%" 
AND interface!=1;

osQuery.label: process_running_without_a_binary_on_disk
osQuery.name: Process Running Without A Binary On Disk

osQuery.label: application_shims
osQuery.name: Application Shims
osQuery.sql:
SELECT apps.executable, h.sha256, apps.path, apps.description, datetime(apps.install_time,"unixepoch") AS install_time, apps.sdb_id 
FROM appcompat_shims apps LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=apps.path;

osQuery.label: logged_in_users
osQuery.name: Logged In Users

osQuery.label: powershell_history
osQuery.name: PowerShell History
osQuery.sql:
SELECT script_text, datetime
FROM orbital_powershell_events
ORDER BY datetime DESC
LIMIT 500;

osQuery.label: operating_system_attributes
osQuery.name: Operating System Attributes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT platform, name, major, minor, build 
FROM os_version;

osQuery.label: windows_executables_that_automatically_execute
osQuery.name: Windows executables that automatically execute
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT ae.name, ae.path, ae.source, h.sha256 
FROM autoexec ae LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path = ae.path;

osQuery.label: windows_hotfixes
osQuery.name: Windows HotFixes

osQuery.label: listening_ports
osQuery.name: Listening Ports
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.path, lp.address, lp.protocol, lp.port 
FROM listening_ports lp LEFT JOIN processes p ON lp.pid = p.pid 
WHERE lp.port != 0 
AND lp.address!="127.0.0.1" 
AND lp.address!="::1" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\system32\%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/sbin/%" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "/usr/libexec/%";

osQuery.label: startup_items
osQuery.name: Startup Items
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, args, source, type, status, username 
FROM startup_items;

osQuery.label: user_accounts
osQuery.name: User Accounts
osQuery.sql:
SELECT uid, gid, username, description, directory, shell, uuid, type 
FROM users;

osQuery.label: windows_prefetch_directory
osQuery.name: Windows Prefetch Directory
osQuery.sql:
SELECT filename, uid, gid, 
datetime(atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,
datetime(ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time, 
datetime(btime, "unixepoch") as create_time,
hard_links, symlink, type, attributes, volume_serial, file_id 
FROM file 
WHERE path LIKE "C:\Windows\Prefetch\%" 
AND size NOT LIKE "0";

osQuery.label: processes_with_network_connections
osQuery.name: Processes With Network Connections

osQuery.label: windows_open_shares
osQuery.name: Windows Open Shares

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT module_id, sha256, module_path 
FROM loaded_module_hashes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_processes
osQuery.name: Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT pid, name AS process_name, path AS process_path 
FROM processes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_vs_processes
osQuery.name: Loaded Module ID To Process ID
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT lh.module_id, pmm.pid AS pid 
FROM process_memory_map pmm JOIN loaded_module_hashes lh ON lh.module_path=pmm.path;

queries.title: (Deprecated) Forensic Snapshot Windows 0.0.5
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. The "Explorer\Advanced" registry keys contain such user interface settings as show hidden files and folders, hide file extensions, hide recently opened programs from Start Menu and many others. Malware often modifies these keys in order to evade detection or establish persistence on the host. Registry keys that should be regularly monitored and they respective values are listed below: 
* "Hidden": 1 - show hidden files and folders, 0 - do not show hidden files and folders
* "ShowSuperHidden" and "SuperHidden": 1 - hidden and system files are displayed
* "HideFileExt": 1 - hide file extensions, 0 - do not hide file extensions 
* "Start_TrackProgs": 1 - do not hide recently opened programs from the Start Menu
* "Start_TrackDocs": 1 - do not hide recently opened documents from the Start Menu
* "Start_SearchFiles": 1 - do not slow down search by including all public folders
* "ShowSyncProviderNotifications": 1 - do not show notifications/advertisements in Windows Explorer

osQuery.label: windows_user_interface_settings
osQuery.name: Explorer Settings Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
key AS reg_key, path, name, data, 
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\microsoft\windows\currentversion\explorer\advanced" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\SOFTWARE\microsoft\windows\currentversion\explorer\advanced" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\microsoft\windows\currentversion\explorer\advanced" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\microsoft\windows\currentversion\explorer\advanced")
AND (
(name IN ("Hidden", "ShowSuperHidden", "SuperHidden") AND data!="1") OR 
name IN ("HideFileExt", "Start_TrackProgs", "Start_TrackDocs", "Start_SearchFiles","DontPrettyPath", "ShowSyncProviderNotifications"));

queries.title: Windows User Interface Settings Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows event with ID "4825" is generated when a user was denied the access to Remote Desktop even though valid credentials were provided. Such events could indicate an attacker trying to move laterally in the environment and should be investigated for suspicious activity.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_denied_rdp_connection_valid_cred
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'AccountName":"([^"]+)', 1) as account_name,
regex_match(data, 'AccountDomain":"([^"]+)', 1) as account_domain,
regex_match(data, 'ClientAddress":"([^"]+)', 1) as client_address, 
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4825;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs: Denied RDP Connection with Valid Credentials

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves names for services that are explicitly allowed by the Application Layer Firewall (ALF) to perform networking:
* process - process name explicitly allowed

osQuery.label: macos_alf_explicit_auths_monitoring
osQuery.name: ALF Services Allowed Networking
osQuery.sql:
SELECT process
FROM alf_explicit_auths
WHERE 
process LIKE regex_match(process, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "process_name"), 0);

queries.title: Application Layer Firewall (ALF) Services Allowed Networking

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. This query will retrieve the execution events of open source powershell scripts downloaded from Github from Windows EventLogs' "Security" channel . Following data is retrieved:
* datetime - Date and time of the event as stated in eventlogs
* new_process_name - Newly created process name e.g certutil.exe
* new_process_path - Full path of the new process
* new_process_commandline - New process commandline
* data - Eventlog data

osQuery.label: powershell_opensource_scripts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Command-line Powershell Scripts Executions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
datetime,
LOWER(replace(regex_match(data, 'NewProcessName":"([^"]+)\\([^"]+)', 2), "\\", "\")) AS new_process_name,
replace(regex_match(data, 'NewProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1), "\\", "\") AS new_process_path,
replace(regex_match(data, 'CommandLine":"(.+?),"', 1), "\\", "\") AS new_process_commandline,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4688 AND
new_process_commandline LIKE regex_match(new_process_commandline, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "opensource_pattern"), 0);

osQuery.label: orbital_powershell_opensource_scripts_monitoring
osQuery.name: Powershell Scripts Executions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
time, datetime, script_name, script_path, script_text, script_block_id, script_block_count 
FROM orbital_powershell_events
WHERE
script_text LIKE regex_match(script_text, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "opensource_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Powershell Open Source Script Execution Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs from the host with event ID 4798 or 4799 that has been initiated by the net.exe or net1.exe processes. An event log with event ID 4798 is generated when a process enumerates a user's security-enabled local groups on a computer or device. An event log with event ID 4799 is generated when a process enumerates the members of a security-enabled local group on the computer or device. The following event log data is retrieved:
    * time the event was received
    * event data
    * event ID
    * provider name of the event
    * event channel

osQuery.label: local_group_membership_enumeration_monitoring
osQuery.name: Local Group Membership Enumeration Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid IN (4799, 4798) AND
(data LIKE '%net1.exe%' OR data LIKE '%net.exe%');

queries.title: Local Group Membership Enumeration Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. The Windows Recycle Bin contains files that have been deleted by the user, but not yet purged from the system. Therefore, Recycle Bin is a valuable source of evidence for forensic investigator. This query lists the files located in the Recycle Bin:
* filename
* absolute file path
* owning user ID
* owning group ID
* last access time
* last modification time
* last status change time
* creation time
* number of hard links
* "1" is the path is a symlink, otherwise "0"
* type - directory or regular file
* size - size of file in bytes
* volume serial number

osQuery.label: windows_file_recycle_bin
osQuery.name: File Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.filename, f.path, f.uid, f.gid,
datetime(f.atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
datetime(f.mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,
datetime(f.ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time,
datetime(f.btime, "unixepoch") as create_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type, f.size, f.volume_serial 
FROM file f
WHERE 
f.path LIKE "C:\$Recycle.bin\%";

queries.title: Windows Recycle Bin Files Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Sysinternals is a set of utilities used to manage, diagnose, troubleshoot and monitor a Microsoft Windows environment. However, these utilities are also often used by attackers for persistence, lateral movement, and defense evasion. This query retrieves hosts where EULA for Sysinternals tools has been accepted which prevents the Microsoft License Agreement box from popping up when the tools are used. 
* registry key path
* name of the Sysinternals utility
* registry key name
* registry key data
* last_modified - the most recent registry write timestamp

osQuery.label: windows_registry_sysinternals_eula
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path,
SPLIT(key, "\", 4) AS utility,
name,
data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified 
FROM registry
WHERE key IN (
SELECT path
FROM registry
WHERE 
path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Sysinternals\%") AND
name LIKE "EulaAccepted";

queries.title: Windows Registry Sysinternals EULA Settings

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves NTFS Access Control List (ACL) for the specified directory:
* path to the file or directory
* type of access mode for the access control entry
* principal - user or group to which the access control entry (ACE) applies
* access - specific permissions that indicate the rights described by the ACE
* inherited_from - the inheritance policy of the ACE

osQuery.label: windows_ntfs_acl_permissions_monitoring
osQuery.name: NTFS ACL Permissions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, type, principal, access, inherited_from
FROM ntfs_acl_permissions
WHERE 
path IN (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern") AND
principal LIKE regex_match(principal, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "principal_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: NTFS ACL Permissions Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows event with ID "4663" indicates that a specific operation was performed on an object. Operations performed on browsers login data may indicate malicious activity on the host. This query retrieves "4663" events when login data for Mozilla, Chrome, and Opera were accessed:
* datetime the event was generated
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that made an attempt to access an object
* subject_domain_name - account's domain or computer name
* object_type - the type of object that was accessed during the operation
* object_name - name and other identifying information for the object for which access was requested
* process_name - full path and the name of the executable for the process
* process_pid - pid of the process that accessed the object
* full event data
Note that to collect "4663" events the group policy "Kernel Object" must be configured. These events are not collected by default.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_browser_login_data_access
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectType":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_type,
regex_match(data, 'ObjectName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS object_name,
regex_match(data, 'ProcessName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS process_name,
regex_match(data, 'ProcessId":"([^"]+)', 1) AS process_pid,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4663 AND
object_type LIKE "file" AND (
object_name LIKE "%login data%" OR object_name LIKE "%logins%");

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Browser Login Data Access

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieves following data based on the pattern specified:
* pid
* process name
* path to executed binary
* process command line
* process current working directory
* user id
* group id
* on disk - 1 if process path exists, 0 if not, -1 if unknown
* process total size
* process parent pid
* process threads
* sha256 for process executable

osQuery.label: process_data
osQuery.name: Process Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.pid, p.name, p.path, p.cmdline, p.cwd, p.uid, p.gid, p.on_disk, p.total_size, p.parent, p.threads, h.sha256
FROM processes p LEFT JOIN hash h on p.path=h.path
WHERE p.name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="process_name")
AND p.cmdline LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="process_cmd")
AND p.cwd LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="process_cwd");

queries.title: Process Data Search
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Whenever the device is connected to a USB port subkeys with device's name and serial number will be created in the registry. This query retrieves the list of USBs the system connected to.

osQuery.label: windows_connected_usb_monitoring
osQuery.name: USBSTOR Registry Key

queries.title: Windows USB Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. The "Prefetch" directory is used to speed up the process of application loading. Malware may attempt to place a copy of itself or modify one of the files in this directory to establish persistence on the system. This query monitors the "Prefetch" directory on the host:
* prefetch filename
* prefetch file path
* SHA256 of a prefetch file 
* uid - owning user ID
* gid - owning group ID
* hard_links - number of hard links
* symlink - 1 if the path is a symlink, otherwise 0
* type - file status
* attributes - file attribute string
* volume_serial - volume serial number
* file_id - file ID

osQuery.label: windows_prefetch_directory
osQuery.name: Windows Prefetch Directory
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.filename, f.path, h.sha256, f.uid, f.gid, 
datetime(f.atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
datetime(f.mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,
datetime(f.ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time, 
datetime(f.btime, "unixepoch") as create_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type, f.attributes, f.volume_serial, f.file_id 
FROM file f
JOIN hash h ON f.path = h.path
WHERE 
f.path LIKE (SELECT value FROM orbital_environment WHERE name LIKE "windir") || "\Prefetch\%" 
AND f.size NOT LIKE "0";

queries.title: Windows Prefetch Directory Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows Event Logs indicating direct (keyboard/virtual session) successful logging on from service and computer accounts.Depending on the environment requirements this could be undesirable behavior which could indicate suspicious activity.

osQuery.label: windows_direct_service_computer_logons_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
datetime AS "Time Generated",
data AS Message,
regex_match(data, '"LogonType":"([^"]+)', 1) AS Logon_Type,
regex_match(data, '"TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS Account_Name,
channel AS LogFile,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4624 AND
(Account_Name LIKE "svc%" OR Account_Name LIKE "%$") AND
datetime LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern");

queries.title: Direct Logons for Service/Computer Accounts Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data for a scheduled task specified by the user:
* name - task name
* action - action executed by the task
* path - path to the executable directory
* enabled - 1 if enabled, 0 if disabled
* state - state of the task: ready, disabled, running
* hidden - 1 if the task is not visible in the UI, otherwise 0

osQuery.label: scheduled_task_search
osQuery.name: Scheduled Tasks
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, action, path, enabled, state, hidden, last_run_time, next_run_time, last_run_message, last_run_code
FROM scheduled_tasks
WHERE name LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="task_name")
AND action LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="task_action")
AND state LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="task_state")
AND last_run_time LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="task_last_run_time")
AND next_run_time LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="task_next_run_time")
AND last_run_code LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="task_last_run_code");

queries.title: Scheduled Task Search
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves the names and values of environment variables set on the system. Parameters may be used to refine the search down to specific names or values of interest.

osQuery.label: windows_environment_variables_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Environment Variables Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, value 
FROM 
(SELECT name, value 
FROM orbital_environment
UNION 
SELECT name AS "name", 
data AS "value"
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Environment") AS t 
WHERE 
t.name LIKE regex_match(t.name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="name_pattern"), 0) AND
t.value LIKE regex_match(t.value, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="value_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Environment Variables Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on modules loaded by the "outlook.exe" process. Normally Outlook does not load such modules as vbscript.dll or jscript/jscript9.dll. If these modules are loaded - it could be due to a HTML email with embedded javascript or due to the "ScriptControl" COM object being used. Both cases should be investigated for suspicious activity.

osQuery.label: windows_outlook_loaded_suspicious_dll
osQuery.name: Outlook Loaded Suspicious DLLs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT(pmm.path) 
FROM process_memory_map pmm
WHERE pmm.pid IN (
SELECT pid
FROM processes 
WHERE cmdline LIKE "%outlook%")
AND (
pmm.path LIKE "%vbscript%" OR
pmm.path LIKE "%jscript%");

queries.title: Outlook Loaded Suspicious DLLs

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves registry data on Office Addins installed on the host. Addins are often used by malware for persistence because they are loaded when an office application starts.

osQuery.label: registry_office_addins_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry Office Addins Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Office\%\Addins"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Office\%\Addins"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\WOW6432NODE\Microsoft\Office\%\Addins";

queries.title: Registry Office Addins Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on the registry "Startup" and "Shutdown" keys. The "Script" value for these registry keys contains path to .ini/.ps scripts that will be launched when the system starts or shuts down. Malware could modify these keys to establish persistence on the host.

osQuery.label: registry_startup_shutdown_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry Startup And Shutdown Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Group Policy\Scripts\Startup\%\%"
OR key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Group Policy\Scripts\Shutdown\%\%";

queries.title: Registry Startup And Shutdown Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves the following information about Time Machine Backups for the system:
    * backup_date - date of backup (UTC)
    * backup_destination_id - time Machine destination ID
    * backup_alias - human readable name of drive being used for backup
    * backup_root_volume_uuid - root UUID of backup volume
    * bytes_available - bytes available on volume
    * backup_bytes_used - bytes used on volume
    * backup_encryption - last known encrypted state

osQuery.label: time_machine
osQuery.name: Time Machine Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
datetime(b.backup_date,"unixepoch") as "Backup Date", 
destination_id, alias, root_volume_uuid,
bytes_available, bytes_used, encryption 
FROM time_machine_backups AS b
JOIN time_machine_destinations
USING (destination_id)
WHERE
alias LIKE regex_match(alias, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "alias_pattern"), 0) AND
"Backup Date" LIKE regex_match("Backup Date", (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "backup_date_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Time Machine Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It provides raw data from performance counters that monitor hard or fixed disk drives on the system:
* name - name of the physical disk.
* avg_disk_bytes_per_read - Average number of bytes transferred from the disk during read operations.
* avg_disk_bytes_per_write - Average number of bytes transferred to the disk during write operations.
* avg_disk_read_queue_length - Average number of read requests that were queued for the selected disk during the sample interval.
* avg_disk_write_queue_length - Average number of write requests that were queued for the selected disk during the sample interval.
* avg_disk_sec_per_read - Average time, in seconds, of a read operation of data from the disk.
* avg_disk_sec_per_write - Average time, in seconds, of a write operation of data to the disk.
* current_disk_queue_length - Number of requests outstanding on the disk at the time the performance data is collected.
* percent_disk_read_time - Percentage of elapsed time that the selected disk drive is busy servicing read requests.
* percent_disk_write_time - Percentage of elapsed time that the selected disk drive is busy servicing write requests.
* percent_disk_time - Percentage of elapsed time that the selected disk drive is busy servicing read or write requests.
* percent_idle_time - Percentage of time during the sample interval that the disk was idle.

osQuery.label: windows_physical_disk_performance_monitoring
osQuery.name: Physical Disk Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, avg_disk_bytes_per_read, avg_disk_bytes_per_write, avg_disk_read_queue_length, avg_disk_write_queue_length, 
avg_disk_sec_per_read, avg_disk_sec_per_write, current_disk_queue_length, percent_disk_read_time, percent_disk_write_time, 
percent_disk_time, percent_idle_time
FROM physical_disk_performance
WHERE 
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="disk_name_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Physical Disk Performance Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS and Linux. It retrieves data on Domain Name System (DNS) resolvers from the host:
* id - address type index or order
* type - address type: sortlist, nameserver, search
* ip - resolver IP/IPv6 address
* netmask - address (sortlist) netmask length

osQuery.label: macos_linux_dns_resolvers_monitoring
osQuery.name: DNS Resolvers
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
id, type, address as ip, netmask
FROM dns_resolvers
WHERE
ip LIKE regex_match(ip, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "ip_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: DNS Resolvers Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves device firmware present on the host:
* type - type of device
* device - the device name
* version - firmware version

osQuery.label: macos_device_firmware_monitoring
osQuery.name: Device Firmware Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
type, device, version
FROM
device_firmware
WHERE
device LIKE regex_match(device, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "device_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Device Firmware Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. "CVE-2020-0594" is an out-of-bounds read flaw in IPv6 subsystem in Intel(R) AMT and Intel(R) ISM versions before 11.8.77, 11.12.77, 11.22.77 and 12.0.64 allowing an unauthenticated user to potentially enable escalation of privilege via network access. Query retrieves hosts vulnerable to this CVE based on their Intel version.

osQuery.label: windows_cve_2020_0594_monitoring
osQuery.name: CVE-2020-0594 Vulnerability Status
osQuery.sql:
WITH B1 AS
(SELECT 1 as one, version as "IME_VERSION",
CASE
WHEN version LIKE "11.8%" AND version >= "11.8.77" THEN "Not Vulnerable"
WHEN version LIKE "11.12%"  AND version >= "11.12.77" THEN "Not Vulnerable"
WHEN version LIKE "11.22%" AND version >= "11.22.77" THEN "Not Vulnerable"
WHEN version LIKE "12.0%" AND version >= "12.0.64" THEN "Not Vulnerable"
ELSE "Vulnerable to CVE-2020-0594"
END "CVE_STATUS" FROM intel_me_info),
B2 AS
(SELECT hardware_model, 1 AS one FROM system_info),
B3 AS
(SELECT version, 1 AS one FROM platform_info)
SELECT B1.IME_VERSION, B1.CVE_STATUS, B2.hardware_model, B3.version
FROM B1 JOIN B2 USING(one) JOIN B3 USING(one);

queries.title: CVE-2020-0594 Vulnerability Status Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Account Creation and Removal events from the system. To avoid detection and to hamper forensics attackers often create a user account, perform the malicious actions and then delete the account. This query identifies such events. Cases when accounts were created and deleted in a short period of time should be investigated for suspicious activity.
* time_account_created - system time at which account was created
* time_account_deleted - system time at which account was deleted
* account domain username,
* user security identifier
* data for event "4720" "A User Account Was Created"
* data for event "4726" "A User Account Was Deleted"

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_account_creation_removal_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
WITH b1 AS (
SELECT 
datetime AS time_account_created,
regex_match(data,'TargetDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) || '\' || regex_match(data,'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS account_domain_username,
regex_match(data, 'TargetSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS user_sid,
data AS event_4720 
FROM 
windows_eventlog
WHERE 
channel="Security" AND eventid = 4720), 
b2 AS (
SELECT 
datetime AS time_account_deleted,
regex_match(data, 'TargetSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS user_sid,
data AS event_4726 
FROM 
windows_eventlog 
WHERE 
channel="Security" AND eventid = 4726) 
SELECT 
b1.time_account_created,
b2.time_account_deleted,
b1.account_domain_username,
b1.user_sid,
b1.event_4720,
b2.event_4726 
FROM 
b1 
JOIN b2 USING(user_sid);

queries.title: Windows Event Logs New Accounts Creation/Removal Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Time Providers are Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs) that are loaded by the time provider manager at system startup. These DLLs can be abused by malicious actors attempting to gain persistence on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_time_providers_monitoring
osQuery.name: Time Providers Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT r.key AS reg_key, r.path, r.name, r.data, DATETIME(r.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified, h.sha256
FROM registry r
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=lower(replace(lower(r.data), "%systemroot%", "C:\Windows"))
WHERE r.key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\%%"
AND r.name LIKE "Dllname";

queries.title: Windows Time Providers Monitoring

queries.description: These queries are applicable to Linux. They list the hidden files and directories of the users' home folders. Hidden files and folders in the home directory usually store configuration files, but besides that are a popular place to hide malicious files. A forensic system report should include a listing of hidden files in users' home folders.

osQuery.label: hidden_files_in_home_directories
osQuery.name: Hidden Files In Home Directories
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
h.sha256, f.path, f.filename, 
CASE f.btime    
WHEN "0" THEN " "
ELSE DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") 
END create_time,
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time,
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time
FROM hash h 
JOIN file f ON f.path=h.path
WHERE (
(f.path LIKE "/home/%%/.%") OR (f.path LIKE "/root/.%")) AND 
f.type="regular";

osQuery.label: hidden_directories_in_home_directories
osQuery.name: Hidden Directories In Home Directories
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.path,
CASE f.btime
WHEN "0" THEN " "
ELSE DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") 
END create_time, 
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time, 
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time, 
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modification_time
FROM file f
WHERE (
(f.path LIKE "/home/%%/.%") OR (f.path LIKE "/root/.%")) AND 
f.type!="regular" AND 
NOT f.path LIKE "%/./" AND 
NOT f.path LIKE "%/../";

queries.title: Hidden Files And Directories Monitoring

queries.description:
This queries is applicable to Windows. It is designed to capture volatile and non-volatile data from the endpoint that can help with forensic investigations.
New queries:
* "Windows NT Domains"
* "Shim Cache"
* "Recently Opened Office Documents"
* "Windows ShellBags Content"
Removed queries:
* "Recent Files Data"
Updated queries:
* "Temp Directory Files"
* "SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes"
* "Shortcut Recent Files Data"
* "Listening Ports"
* "Network Profiles Registry Key"
* "USBSTOR Registry Key"
* "UserAssist Registry Key Monitoring"

osQuery.label: windows_nt_domains_search
osQuery.name: Windows NT Domains
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, client_site_name, dc_site_name, dns_forest_name, domain_controller_address, domain_controller_name, domain_name, status
FROM ntdomains;

osQuery.label: windows_bam_entries_monitoring
osQuery.name: Background Activity Monitor Entries
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, 
DATETIME(last_execution_time, "unixepoch") AS last_execution_time,
sid
FROM background_activities_moderator
ORDER BY last_execution_time DESC;

osQuery.label: hosts_file
osQuery.name: Hosts File Data

osQuery.label: sha256_hash_of_running_processes
osQuery.name: SHA256 Hash Of Running Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
p.pid, p.name, p.path, p.cmdline, p.state, p.uid AS owner_uid, u.username AS owner_username, h.sha256 
FROM processes p 
INNER JOIN hash h 
ON p.path=h.path
LEFT JOIN users u ON u.uid=p.uid;

osQuery.label: mapped_drives
osQuery.name: Mapped Drives

osQuery.label: interface_names_and_associated_ips
osQuery.name: Interface Names And Associated IPs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT interface, address, mask, type, friendly_name 
FROM interface_addresses 
WHERE interface NOT LIKE "lo%" 
AND interface!=1;

osQuery.label: system_network_state_monitoring
osQuery.name: System Network State
osQuery.sql:
SELECT disconnected, ipv4_no_traffic, ipv6_no_traffic, ipv4_subnet, ipv4_local_network, ipv4_internet, ipv6_subnet, ipv6_local_network, ipv6_internet
FROM connectivity;

osQuery.label: process_running_without_a_binary_on_disk
osQuery.name: Process Running Without A Binary On Disk

osQuery.label: shared_resources_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shared Resources
osQuery.sql:
SELECT description, install_date, status, allow_maximum, maximum_allowed, name, path, type
FROM shared_resources;

osQuery.label: application_shims
osQuery.name: Application Shims
osQuery.sql:
SELECT apps.executable, h.sha256, apps.path, apps.description, datetime(apps.install_time,"unixepoch") AS install_time, apps.sdb_id 
FROM appcompat_shims apps LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=apps.path;

osQuery.label: logon_sessions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Logon Sessions

osQuery.label: logged_in_users
osQuery.name: Logged In Users

osQuery.label: bitlocker_encryption_monitoring
osQuery.name: Bitlocker Status Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT device_id, drive_letter, persistent_volume_id, conversion_status, protection_status, encryption_method 
FROM bitlocker_info;

osQuery.label: powershell_history
osQuery.name: PowerShell History
osQuery.sql:
SELECT time, datetime, script_block_id, script_block_count, script_text, script_name, script_path
FROM orbital_powershell_events
ORDER BY datetime DESC
LIMIT 500;

osQuery.label: operating_system_attributes
osQuery.name: Operating System Attributes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, version, major, minor, patch, build, platform, codename, install_date
FROM os_version;

osQuery.label: windows_executables_that_automatically_execute
osQuery.name: Windows executables that automatically execute
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT ae.name, ae.path, ae.source, h.sha256 
FROM autoexec ae LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path = ae.path;

osQuery.label: windows_av_products_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows AntiVirus Products Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
displayName, 
pathToSignedProductExe AS "Path To Signed Executable", 
pathToSignedReportingExe AS "Path To Signed Reporting Executable"
FROM AntiVirusProduct;

osQuery.label: windows_hotfixes
osQuery.name: Windows HotFixes

osQuery.label: listening_ports
osQuery.name: Listening Ports
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.path, lp.address, lp.protocol, lp.port 
FROM listening_ports lp LEFT JOIN processes p ON lp.pid = p.pid 
WHERE lp.port != 0 
AND lp.address!="127.0.0.1" 
AND lp.address!="::1" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\system32\%";

osQuery.label: startup_items
osQuery.name: Startup Items
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, path, args, source, type, status, username 
FROM startup_items;

osQuery.label: installed_programs_on_windows_host
osQuery.name: Installed Programs On Windows Host

osQuery.label: user_accounts
osQuery.name: User Accounts
osQuery.sql:
SELECT uid, gid, username, description, directory, shell, uuid, type 
FROM users;

osQuery.label: windows_prefetch_directory
osQuery.name: Windows Prefetch Directory
osQuery.sql:
SELECT f.filename, f.path, f.uid, f.gid, 
datetime(f.atime, "unixepoch") as last_access_time,
datetime(f.mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modification_time,
datetime(f.ctime, "unixepoch") as last_status_change_time, 
datetime(f.btime, "unixepoch") as create_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type, f.attributes, f.volume_serial, f.file_id 
FROM file f
WHERE f.path LIKE (SELECT value FROM orbital_environment WHERE name LIKE "windir") || "\Prefetch\%" 
AND f.size NOT LIKE "0";

osQuery.label: connected_networks_history_monitoring
osQuery.name: Network Profiles Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name,
data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\networklist\profiles\%"
AND name IN ("ProfileName", "DateCreated", "DateLastConnected", "Description", "Category");

osQuery.label: processes_with_network_connections
osQuery.name: Processes With Network Connections

osQuery.label: user_groups_monitoring
osQuery.name: User Groups
osQuery.sql:
SELECT g.groupname, u.username, g.comment
FROM groups g
LEFT JOIN user_groups ug ON g.gid=ug.gid
LEFT JOIN users u ON ug.uid=u.uid;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes_tmp
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes Temp

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_hashes
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Hashes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT module_id, sha256, module_path 
FROM loaded_module_hashes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_processes
osQuery.name: Processes
osQuery.sql:
SELECT pid, name AS process_name, path AS process_path 
FROM processes;

osQuery.label: loaded_modules_vs_processes
osQuery.name: Loaded Module ID To Process ID
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT lh.module_id, pmm.pid AS pid 
FROM process_memory_map pmm JOIN loaded_module_hashes lh ON lh.module_path=pmm.path;

osQuery.label: windows_connected_usb_monitoring
osQuery.name: USBSTOR Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
r2.data AS friendly_name, r1.name AS serial_number,
DATETIME(r2.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry r1
JOIN registry r2 ON r1.path=r2.key
WHERE
r1.key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR\%" AND
r2.name = "FriendlyName";

osQuery.label: scheduled_tasks_monitoring
osQuery.name: Scheduled Tasks
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, action, path, enabled, state, hidden, 
CASE
WHEN last_run_time LIKE "94%" THEN ""
ELSE DATETIME(last_run_time, "unixepoch") END last_run_time,
CASE
WHEN next_run_time < 0 THEN "" 
ELSE DATETIME(next_run_time, "unixepoch") END next_run_time, 
last_run_message, last_run_code
FROM scheduled_tasks;

osQuery.label: temp_directories_monitoring
osQuery.name: Temp Directory Files
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
f.path, f.filename, 
CASE f.filename
WHEN "." THEN ""
ELSE h.sha256
END sha256, 
f.uid, f.gid, f.mode, f.size, 
DATETIME(f.atime, "unixepoch") AS last_access_time, 
DATETIME(f.mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified, 
DATETIME(f.ctime, "unixepoch") AS last_status_change_time, 
DATETIME(f.btime, "unixepoch") AS creation_time,
f.hard_links, f.symlink, f.type
FROM file f 
LEFT JOIN hash h ON f.path=h.path
WHERE 
f.path LIKE "C:\Users\%\AppData\Local\Temp\%%" OR
f.path LIKE (SELECT value FROM orbital_environment WHERE name LIKE "windir") || "\Temp\%%";

osQuery.label: dns_cache_table_monitoring
osQuery.name: DNS Cache Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name AS "domain", type
FROM dns_cache;

osQuery.label: running_services_monitoring
osQuery.name: Running Services Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, service_type, display_name, status, pid, start_type, win32_exit_code, service_exit_code, path, module_path, description, user_account 
FROM services;

osQuery.label: trusted_root_certificates_monitoring
osQuery.name: Trusted Root Certificates Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT common_name, subject, issuer, ca, self_signed, 
DATETIME(not_valid_before, "unixepoch") as "Not Valid Before", DATETIME(not_valid_after, "unixepoch") as "Not Valid After",
sha1, serial, sid, username
FROM certificates
WHERE path LIKE "CurrentUser\Trusted Root Certification Authorities";

osQuery.label: chrome_extensions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Chrome Browser Extensions Monitoring

osQuery.label: windows_environment_variables_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Environment Variables Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, value
FROM
(SELECT name, value
FROM orbital_environment
UNION
SELECT name AS "name", data AS "value"
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Environment") AS t;

osQuery.label: userassist_monitoring
osQuery.name: UserAssist Registry Key Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
path, 
CASE last_execution_time
WHEN "0" THEN "0" 
ELSE DATETIME(last_execution_time, "unixepoch") 
END last_execution_time, 
count, 
sid
FROM userassist
ORDER BY last_execution_time DESC;

osQuery.label: wmi_cli_event_consumer_monitoring
osQuery.name: WMI CommandLineEventConsumer Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, command_line_template, executable_path, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_cli_event_consumers;

osQuery.label: wmi_event_filters_monitoring
osQuery.name: WMI Event Filters Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, query, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_event_filters;

osQuery.label: wmi_script_event_consumers_monitoring
osQuery.name: WMI ActiveScriptEventConsumer Monitoring
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, scripting_engine, script_file_name, script_text, class, relative_path
FROM wmi_script_event_consumers;

osQuery.label: windows_shimcache_monitoring
osQuery.name: Shim Cache
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
entry,
path,
CASE modified_time
WHEN "0" THEN "0"
ELSE DATETIME(modified_time, "unixepoch")
END modified_time,
h.sha256
FROM shimcache 
LEFT JOIN hash h USING(path);

osQuery.label: windows_office_mru
osQuery.name: Recently Opened Office Documents
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
om.application, om.version, om.path,
DATETIME(om.last_opened_time, "unixepoch") AS last_opened_time,
om.sid, u.username, h.sha256
FROM
office_mru om
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=om.path
JOIN users u ON u.uuid=om.sid;

osQuery.label: windows_shellbags_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows ShellBags Content
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
u.username,
sid,
source,
path as directory_name,
DATETIME(modified_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_modified_time,
DATETIME(created_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_created_time,
DATETIME(accessed_time, "unixepoch") AS directory_accessed_time,
mft_entry,
mft_sequence
FROM shellbags
LEFT JOIN users u ON shellbags.sid=u.uuid;

queries.title: System Snapshot Windows 0.0.9 (formerly Forensic Snapshot Windows 0.0.9)
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It tracks events related to computer account changes:
* datetime of the event
* subject_user_name - the name of the account that requested the change
* subject_domain_name - the account domain's name
* target_user_name - the name of the computer account that was changed
* target_domain_name - the domain name of changed computer account
* data - full event data
Events "4742" which were generated by unknown users or where computer account properties (script path or password last set) were updated should be investigated for suspicious activity.
Note that this event is generated only on domain controllers and the group policy "Computer Account Management" configuration is required for this event to be logged. It is not logged by default in a domain environment.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_computer_account_changed
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'TargetDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS target_domain_name,  
data
FROM
windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4742 AND
subject_user_name != target_user_name;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Computer Account Changed

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves information about inactive critical security services on the system that should be running by default: |
	* service_name - service name 
	* service_display_name - service display name 
	* service_pid - service process id 
	* service_path - path to service executable 
	* service_user_account - the name of the account that the service process will be logged on as when it runs
	* service_start_type - service start type
	* service_module_path - path to service dll

osQuery.label: critical_security_service_inactive
osQuery.name: Inactive Critical Security Services
osQuery.sql:
SELECT s.name, s.display_name, s.pid, s.path, s.user_account, s.start_type, s.module_path 
FROM services s 
WHERE s.name IN ("MpsSvc","SepMasterService","SAVAdminService","SavService","wscsvc","wuauserv") 
AND s.status != "RUNNING";

queries.title: Critical Security Service Inactive

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Linux. It retrieves information about Debian packages installed on the system:
* package name
* package version
* package source
* package size in bytes
* package architecture
* package revision

osQuery.label: linux_deb_packages_monitoring
osQuery.name: Installed Debian Packages
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
name, version, source, size, arch, revision, maintainer 
FROM deb_packages
WHERE
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Debian Packages Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: Explorer.exe is a standard system process on the Microsoft Windows OS that is responsible for Windows Explorer allowing users to browse through the file and document libraries on the hard drive. Malware often attempts to disguise itself as a well known, secure system file. One way to detect it is to check if the executable is running from the correct location defined during the Windows installation.

osQuery.label: explorer_default_location
osQuery.name: Path To Explorer.exe Binary
osQuery.sql:
SELECT p.name, p.path, p.pid, p.parent, p.cmdline 
FROM processes p 
WHERE p.name LIKE "explorer.exe" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\explorer.exe" 
AND p.path NOT LIKE "c:\windows\syswow64\explorer.exe" 
AND LOWER(p.path)!="";

queries.title: Explorer.exe Default Location Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. When a user logs into Windows with a Microsoft account, that user's activity on the system is logged in the "ActivitiesCache.db". However, an account can be excluded from synchronization by setting the "ActivityAccountFilter" registry key. This query retrieves such accounts.

osQuery.label: accounts_excluded_from_sync_monitoring
osQuery.name: Accounts Excluded From Sync Registry Data

queries.title: Account Excluded From Sync Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Root certificates are usually pre-installed on the system by the manufacturer. They are used in public key cryptography to identify the Certificate Authority and to establish TLS and SSL connection in web browsers. Attackers can install a fake root certificate to intercept communications or code signing amongst other things. This query returns a list of all certificates installed in Trusted Root Certification Authorities.

osQuery.label: certificates
osQuery.name: Certificates

queries.title: Trusted Root Certificates Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves from the registry saved Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections:
* remote ip 
* username used after each successful connection to remote host

osQuery.label: windows_registry_rdp_saved_connections
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
split(path, '\', 6) AS remote_host,
data AS username_hint
FROM registry
WHERE key IN (
SELECT path
FROM registry
WHERE
key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client\Servers") AND
name LIKE "UsernameHint" AND
remote_host LIKE regex_match(remote_host, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "remote_host_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Windows Registry Remote Desktop Protocol Saved Connections

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. Table "xprotect_reports" lists known malware that attempted to run on the system. This could be used as an early warning for additional attacks. The following data is retrieved: 
* name - description of XProtected malware
* user_action - action taken by the user after prompted
* time - quarantine alert time

osQuery.label: macos_xprotect_reports_monitoring
