osQuery.name: XProtect Reports Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, user_action, DATETIME(time, "unixepoch") AS "Quarantine Alert" 
FROM xprotect_reports
WHERE
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
user_action LIKE regex_match(user_action, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "user_action_pattern"), 0) AND
"Quarantine Alert" LIKE regex_match("Quarantine Alert", (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "quarantine_alert_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: XProtect Reports Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves video card information of the system:
* color_depth - The amount of bits per pixel to represent color.
* driver - The driver of the device.
* driver_date - The date listed on the installed driver.
* driver_version - The version of the installed driver.
* manufacturer - The manufacturer of the gpu.
* model - The model of gpu.
* series - The series of gpu.
* video_mode - The current resolution of the display.

osQuery.label: windows_video_info_monitoring
osQuery.name: Video Card Information
osQuery.sql:
SELECT color_depth, driver, driver_date, driver_version, manufacturer, model, series, video_mode
FROM video_info
WHERE
driver LIKE regex_match(driver, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="driver_pattern"), 0) AND
driver_date LIKE regex_match(driver_date, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="driver_date_pattern"), 0) AND
driver_version LIKE regex_match(driver_version, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="driver_version_pattern"), 0) AND
model LIKE regex_match(model, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="model_pattern"), 0) AND
series LIKE regex_match(series, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="series_pattern"), 0) AND
video_mode LIKE regex_match(video_mode, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="video_mode_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: System Video Card Information

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Scheduled tasks can be created to automatically run a variety of jobs. Tasks can run once, on a schedule, or as the result of specific system events like user login. Legitimate software may use scheduled tasks to run routine maintenance or for other reasons. Malware can use scheduled tasks to cause the system to run the malware automatically. This query retrieves the following data on scheduled tasks:
* task name
* actions executed by the task
* path the executable to be run
* 1 if task is enabled
* task state
* 1 if task is hidden
* last time task ran
* next time task will run
* exit status message of the last task run
* exit status code of the last task run

osQuery.label: scheduled_tasks_monitoring
osQuery.name: Scheduled Tasks
osQuery.sql:
SELECT name, action, path, enabled, state, hidden, last_run_time, next_run_time, last_run_message, last_run_code
FROM scheduled_tasks;

queries.title: Scheduled Tasks Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Server Message Block (SMB) is a protocol for sharing files, printers, serial ports, and communication abstractions such as named pipes and mail slots between computers. A remote code execution vulnerability exists because of the way that SMBv3 handles certain requests (CVE-2020-0796). This query retrieves hosts that are vulnerable to CVE-2020-0796. The configuration of these retrieved hosts should be reviewed and patched to prevent a potential security breach.

osQuery.label: cve_2020_0796_monitoring
osQuery.name: CVE-2020-0796 Vulnerability Status
osQuery.sql:
SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS (
  SELECT build, shares, disablecompression, patched 
  FROM 
  (SELECT build FROM os_version),
  (SELECT count(name) as shares FROM shared_resources WHERE NOT LOWER(name)="admin$" AND NOT LOWER(name)="ipc$" AND NOT LOWER(name)="c$"),
  (SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS
    (SELECT data FROM registry WHERE path="HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters\DisableCompression" AND DATA=1)
   THEN "True" ELSE "False" END AS disablecompression),
  (SELECT CASE WHEN EXISTS
    (SELECT hotfix_id FROM patches WHERE hotfix_id="KB4551762")
   THEN "True" ELSE "False" END AS patched)
  WHERE (build = 18362 OR build = 18363)
  AND shares > 0
  AND disablecompression = "False"
  AND patched = "False"
)
THEN "Vulnerable" ELSE "Not Vulnerable" END AS "SMBv3 CVE-2020-0796";

queries.title: CVE-2020-0796 Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Event Log with ID "5377" is generated every time the user successfully restores the credential manager database. This action is very rarely used by users, and should be investigated for suspicious activity since it could indicate a malware or a compromised account being used to perform malicious actions.
Search suggestions:
* "subject_logon_id" field could used to find other Windows Event Logs related to this event such as corresponding account logon events with ID "4624"

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_cred_manager_restored_from_backup
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserSid":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_sid,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_user_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectDomainName":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_domain_name,
regex_match(data, 'SubjectLogonId":"([^"]+)', 1) AS subject_logon_id, 
data
FROM
windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel="Security" AND
eventid = 5377;

queries.title: Windows Event Logs Credential Manager Credentials Restored from a Backup

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows registry key "CrashDumpEnabled" data from the host indicating the crash dump status. Malware, such as HermeticWiper, will often set this key to "None" in order to prevent data recovery and forensic investigation.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_crashcontrol
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
key, name, 
CASE data
WHEN "0" THEN "None"
WHEN "1" THEN "Complete memory dump"
WHEN "2" THEN "Kernel memory dump"
WHEN "3" THEN "Small memory dump (64KB)"
WHEN "7" THEN "Automatic memory dump"
ELSE "Unknown"
END crash_control,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CrashControl\CrashDumpEnabled" AND
crash_control LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "crash_control_pattern");

queries.title: Windows Registry CrashControl Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) is a vendor agnostic interface which can communicate with the endpoint in order to prevent execution of malware. Adversaries could abuse this service by installing malicious AMSI providers to establish persistence on the host. Registry key "FeatureBits" enables Authenticode signing checks preventing malicious providers from loading. If this key is not present on the host or set to "0x1" then Authenticode signing checks are disabled.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_amsi_feature_bits
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name,
data,
CASE data
WHEN "1" THEN "The signing check is disabled"
ELSE "The check for Authenticode signing is enabled"
END signing_check_status,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
path LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\AMSI\FeatureBits";

queries.title: Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) FeatureBits Configuration

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. A Windows Event Log with ID of "4672" is generated if sensitive privileges are assigned to a new log on session. Monitoring this event could be used for:
* ensuring that non-administrative accounts do not have unexpected privileges
* ensuring that certain privileges are never granted
* monitoring specific sensitive privileges across the environment

osQuery.label: special_privileges_logons_search
osQuery.name: Special Privileges Logon Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
regex_match(data, '"SubjectUserName":"([^"]+)', 1) as Subject_User_Name,
replace(regex_match(data, '"PrivilegeList":"([^"]+)', 1), "\r\n\t\t\t", " ") as Privilege_List,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4672 AND
Subject_User_Name NOT LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "subject_user_name_pattern") AND
Subject_User_Name NOT LIKE "SYSTEM" AND
Subject_User_Name NOT LIKE "DWM-%" AND
Subject_User_Name NOT LIKE "%$" AND
Subject_User_Name NOT LIKE "LOCAL SERVICE" AND
Subject_User_Name NOT LIKE "NETWORK SERVICE";

queries.title: Special Privileges Logons Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on Orbital services running on the host.

osQuery.label: orbital_service_configuration
osQuery.name: Orbital Service Configuration
osQuery.sql:
SELECT s.name, p.name, s.service_type, s.display_name, s.status, s.pid, s.start_type, s.path, s.module_path, s.user_account 
FROM services s
JOIN processes p ON s.pid=p.pid
WHERE s.name LIKE "%orbital";

queries.title: Orbital Service Configuration Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Malware can disable Firewall notifications on the system in order to evade detection by the user. This query monitors the registry for such modifications.

osQuery.label: disabled_registry_firewall_notifications
osQuery.name: Disabled Registry Firewall Notifications

queries.title: Disabled Registry Firewall Notifications Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. "SilentProcessExit" is a debugging feature used for process termination monitoring. This feature launches a debugger executable when the monitored application exits. Malware may abuse this feature to load a malicious executable in order to establish persistence or achieve privilege elevation. This query retrieves data on "SilentProcessExit" registry keys.

osQuery.label: silent_process_exit_monitoring
osQuery.name: Silent Process Exit Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SilentProcessExit\%" OR
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432NODE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SilentProcessExit\%");

queries.title: Silent Process Exit Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Class ID (CLSID) is a globally unique identifier that identifies a Windows Component Object Model (COM) class object and allows operating systems and software to detect and access COM objects without identifying them by name. Adversaries may establish persistence by executing malicious content triggered by hijacked references to COM objects. This query retrieves hosts with hijacked Recycle Bin's COM.

osQuery.label: windows_registry_recycle_bin_persistence
osQuery.name: Registry
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
path, name, data,
DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE
key LIKE (
SELECT path || '%%' 
FROM registry 
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\CLSID\%" AND 
name LIKE "(Default)" AND 
data LIKE "Recycle Bin") AND
path LIKE "%shell\open\command%";

queries.title: Windows Registry Recycle Bin Shell Open Command

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) is a process in Microsoft Windows operating systems that is responsible for enforcing the security policy on the system by verifying users logging on to a Windows computer or server, handling password changes, creating access tokens, and writing to the Windows Security Log. Adversaries may inject code into LSASS in order to evade process-based defenses, establish persistence on the host or to collect sensitive information. This query retrieves data on DLLs currently loaded by LSASS:
* path - loaded DLL path
* original_program_name - the original program name that the publisher has signed
* serial_number - the certificate serial number
* issuer_name - the certificate issuer name
* subject_name - the certificate subject name
* result - the signature check result
* SHA256 of the loaded DLL

osQuery.label: windows_lsass_loaded_modules_monitoring
osQuery.name: Loaded Modules Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT DISTINCT pmm.path, a.original_program_name, a.serial_number, a.issuer_name, a.subject_name, a.result, h.sha256
FROM processes p 
JOIN process_memory_map pmm USING (pid)
LEFT JOIN authenticode a ON a.path=pmm.path
LEFT JOIN hash h ON h.path=pmm.path
WHERE 
p.name = "lsass.exe" AND
pmm.path LIKE regex_match(pmm.path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0) ;

queries.title: Lsass Loaded Modules Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Virtual Network Computing (VNC) is a desktop-sharing system for remote access. Malware, such as Carbanak or Dridex, are known to use VNC for post-compromise activity and as an alternative command and control communication channel. This query returns all hosts where the VNC service is currently running. Collected data should be reviewed for any suspicious entries.

osQuery.label: vnc_installed_or_running_monitoring
osQuery.name: Running VNC Services

queries.title: Virtual Network Computing Services Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. It retrieves data on executables allowed by the Application Layer Firewall (ALF): 
* path - path to the executable that is excepted
* state - firewall exception state

osQuery.label: macos_alf_exceptions_monitoring
osQuery.name: Application Layer Firewall Service Exceptions
osQuery.sql:
SELECT path, state 
FROM alf_exceptions
WHERE
path LIKE regex_match(path, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "path_pattern"), 0) AND
state LIKE regex_match(CAST(state AS TEXT), (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "state_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Application Layer Firewall (ALF) Service Exceptions Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. By default, Windows does not allow network logons with blank passwords. However, this type of logon could be enabled through the registry by setting the "LimitBlankPasswordUse" registry key to 0. If enabled, the security posture of the host becomes considerably weak and vulnerable to remote compromise. This query retrieves systems where this logon is enabled.

osQuery.label: blank_passwords_auth_monitoring
osQuery.name: LimitBlankPasswordUse Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, type, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Control\Lsa"
AND name LIKE "LimitBlankPasswordUse"
AND data!=1;

queries.title: Network Logon With Blank Password Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves data on third party firewalls installed on the host. The data displayed are:
* Display name 
* Path to signed product executable
* Path to signed reporting executable

osQuery.label: third_party_firewall_product_monitoring
osQuery.name: Third Party Firewall Products Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT displayName,
pathToSignedProductExe AS "Path To Signed Executable",
pathToSignedReportingExe AS "Path To Signed Reporting Executable"
FROM FirewallProduct;

queries.title: Third Party Firewall Products Monitoring
warnings: Products must be properly registered with Windows to be present in this table.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Malware could disable access to the Registry Editor to prevent the user from removing suspicious keys, such as the ones used to achieve persistence. This query monitors the registry for such modifications.

osQuery.label: registry_tools_access_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry Tools Access Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") as last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE (key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System" 
OR key LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System") 
AND name LIKE "DisableRegistryTools" 
AND data = "1";

queries.title: Disabled Registry Tools Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. This query will show the registry path if it's data is set to 1, which means computer can startup without prompting for admin password. This defensive evasion technique has been seen in Lockbit ransomware.

osQuery.label: automatic_logon_registry_monitoring
osQuery.name: Turned On Automatic Log On Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT datetime(mtime, "unixepoch", "UTC") AS last_modified, path as reg_path, name, data 
FROM registry 
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\winlogon" 
AND name ="AutoAdminLogon" AND data="1";

queries.title: Automatic Log On Registry Key Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. PuTTY is a popular SSH client application. This query retrieves PuTTY sessions data from a registry key which is forensically important as an indication of an attacker's attempt at lateral movement.
Search suggestions:
* To search for specific hostname value use parameter "data_pattern".
* By default, this query retrieves only "HostName" data from the registry. Use parameter "name_pattern" to retrieves additional data from the registry.

osQuery.name: PuTTY Sessions Registry Key Temp
osQuery.sql:
CREATE TEMP TABLE putty_reg_keys AS 
SELECT path
FROM registry
WHERE path LIKE "HKEY_USERS\%\Software\SimonTatham\PuTTY\Sessions\%";

osQuery.label: putty_sessions_registry_monitoring
osQuery.name: PuTTY Sessions Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT path, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE 
key IN (SELECT path FROM putty_reg_keys) AND
name LIKE regex_match(name, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "name_pattern"), 0) AND
data LIKE regex_match(data, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "data_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: PuTTY Sessions Registry Key Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It retrieves information on user accounts and groups present on the endpoint optionally filtered by the specified username or group-name patterns.

osQuery.label: users_group_search
osQuery.name: Users Group
osQuery.sql:
SELECT u.uid, u.username, g.groupname, g.group_sid, g.comment
FROM user_groups ug 
JOIN users u ON u.uid=ug.uid
JOIN groups g ON g.gid=ug.gid
WHERE g.groupname LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="group_name")
AND username LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="user_name");

queries.title: Users Group Search

queries.description:
EOL 2022-04. This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Event Log with EventID "4625" is generated when a failed logon attempt is detected. The "LogonType" indicates what kind of logon was attempted. A list of well known logon types is given below:
* Logon type 2 - local user logon
* Logon type 3 - logon from the network (access to shared resources, printers, etc.)
* Logon type 4 - batch logon type. This logon type is commonly used by batch servers, where processes may be executing on behalf of a user without their direct intervention
* Logon type 5 - service logon
* Logon type 7 - unlocking password protected screen
* Logon type 8 - logon with clear text credentials passed on the network
* Logon type 9 - logon with new credentials specified for the outbound connections
* Logon type 10 - logon from a remote machine using RDP
* Logon type 11 - cached interactive logon when DC was not contacted to verify supplied credentials

osQuery.label: failed_logons_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs for Failed Logons
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime AS "TimeGenerated",
data AS Message,
regex_match(data, '"LogonType":"([^"]+)', 1) AS LogonType,
eventid AS EventIdentifier,
provider_name AS SourceName,
channel AS Logfile,
"N/A" AS "TimeWritten",
"N/A" AS "Type"
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4625 AND
LogonType LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="logon_type");

queries.title: Failed Logons Monitoring (Deprecated)
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS. Hidden users are users whose accounts are not showing at the log in screen. Such users could be used by adversaries to mask the presence of user account they created. This query retrieves hidden user accounts from the host if present:
* uid - user ID
* username 
* hidden_status - indicates if this user is hidden or not

osQuery.label: macos_hidden_users_monitoring
osQuery.name: Users Data
osQuery.sql:
WITH 
t1 AS (
SELECT 1 AS one, uid, username, is_hidden FROM users),
t2 AS (
SELECT 1 AS one, value FROM plist WHERE
path LIKE "/Library/Preferences/com.apple.loginwindow.plist" AND key LIKE "Hide500Users")
SELECT uid, username,
CASE WHEN NOT EXISTS(SELECT value FROM t2) AND is_hidden="1" THEN "Hidden"
WHEN value="1" AND (uid < 500 OR is_hidden="1") THEN "Hidden" END hidden_status
FROM t1 LEFT JOIN t2 USING(one)
WHERE t1.username LIKE regex_match(t1.username, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "username_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: Hidden Users Monitoring

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves a list of networks that the host has previously connected to from the "NetworkList" registry key. Inspecting these values provides an administrator or incident responder information about when networks were first connected to and with what security posture.  
Category mappings:
  0 - Private Network. Network services fully enabled, least firewall protection.
  1 - Public Network. Network services more secured, more firewall protection.
  2 - Domain Network.

osQuery.label: connected_networks_history_monitoring
osQuery.name: Network Profiles Registry Key
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key AS reg_key, path, name, data, DATETIME(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\microsoft\windows nt\currentversion\networklist\profiles\%"
AND name IN ("ProfileName", "DateCreated", "DateLastConnected", "Description", "Category");

queries.title: Networks Host Connected

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. On Windows, DLLs are kept in virtual memory and shared between applications running on the system to avoid redundant copies of DLLs being loaded in memory. A malicious actor can load a DLL that has the same name as a system DLL, then change the entry in the KnownDLLs list to point to the malicious copy. This way, programs that request the system DLL will instead be directed to the malicious copy. This query retrieves data on the "KnownDLLs" registry key and it's data entries:
* data - DLL file name. By default this DLL is located under "C:\Windows\System32"
* last_modified - date/time this registry entry was last updated

osQuery.label: windows_registry_knowndlls_monitoring
osQuery.name: Registry Data
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
DISTINCT data,
datetime(mtime, "unixepoch") AS last_modified 
FROM registry
WHERE 
key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%ControlSet%\Control\Session Manager\KnownDLLs"
ORDER BY last_modified DESC;

queries.title: Registry Known DLLs Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to MacOS and Linux. It retrieves the current list of APT repositories or software channels:
* name - repository name
* source - source file
* base_uri - repository base URI
* release - release name
* version - repository source version
* maintainer - repository maintainer
* components - repository components
* architectures - repository architectures

osQuery.label: linux_apt_sources_monitoring
osQuery.name: APT Sources
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
name, source, base_uri, release, version, maintainer, components, architectures
FROM
apt_sources
WHERE
base_uri LIKE regex_match(base_uri, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "base_uri_pattern"), 0) AND
version LIKE regex_match(version, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "version_pattern"), 0) AND
maintainer LIKE regex_match(maintainer, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "maintainer_pattern"), 0);

queries.title: APT Sources Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Service paths stored in registry are vulnerable to path interception if the path has one or more spaces and is not surrounded by quotation marks. This query retrieves such paths from the registry.

osQuery.label: registry_service_paths_monitoring
osQuery.name: Service Paths Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT key as reg_key, path, name, data, datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\%"
AND name LIKE "ImagePath"
AND data NOT LIKE '"%'
AND data LIKE "% %"
AND data NOT LIKE "%system32%";

queries.title: Registry Service Paths Monitoring
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. Windows Event with ID "4713" detects a change to a domain's Kerberos policy and Windows Event with ID "4739" is generated when the security policy of a domain was changed. In both cases the detected changes should be checked for suspicious activity on the host.

osQuery.label: windows_domain_kerberos_policy_changes_monitoring
osQuery.name: Windows Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT 
datetime AS "Time Generated",
data AS Message,
eventid,
provider_name AS SourceName
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid IN (4713, 4739) AND
datetime LIKE (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n = "datetime_pattern");

queries.title: Domain or Kerberos Policy Changes Monitoring

queries.description: This query is applicable to Windows. The Windows Portable Devices (WDP) class subkey contains information such as the manufacturer, model/description, and the volume name/label of the connected devices such as phones, external hard drives, cameras, etc. This query retrieves data from this registry key.

osQuery.label: windows_portable_devices_search
osQuery.name: Windows Portable Devices Registry Keys
osQuery.sql:
SELECT regex_split(key, "\", 5)  AS "device_name_and_serial" , data AS "friendly_name", datetime(mtime,"unixepoch") AS last_modified
FROM registry 
WHERE key LIKE "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Portable Devices\Devices\%"
AND data LIKE regex_match(data, (SELECT v FROM __vars WHERE n="device_name"), 0)
ORDER BY last_modified DESC;

queries.title: Windows Portable Devices Search
warnings: This query can result in excessive amounts of data. Additional conditional clauses for reducing data size and increasing relevance are advised.

queries.description:
This query is applicable to Windows. It retrieves Windows event logs related to boot configuration changes:
* time event was generated
* kernel debugging status
* test signing status
* integrity check status
* full event data
To limit the output only logs where kernel debug, test signing or integrity checks were set to "Yes" are retrieve. Adjust according to your environment.

osQuery.label: windows_eventlogs_kernel_debugging_on
osQuery.name: Event Logs
osQuery.sql:
SELECT
datetime,
CASE
WHEN regex_match(data, 'TestSigning":"([^"]+)', 1) LIKE "%1843%" THEN "No"
WHEN regex_match(data, 'TestSigning":"([^"]+)', 1) LIKE "%1842%" THEN "Yes"
ELSE "Unknown"
END test_signing,
CASE 
WHEN regex_match(data, 'KernelDebug":"([^"]+)', 1) LIKE "%1843%" THEN "No"
WHEN regex_match(data, 'KernelDebug":"([^"]+)', 1) LIKE "%1842%" THEN "Yes"
ELSE "Unknown"
END kernel_debug,
CASE
WHEN regex_match(data, 'DisableIntegrityChecks":"([^"]+)', 1) LIKE "%1843%" THEN "No"
WHEN regex_match(data, 'DisableIntegrityChecks":"([^"]+)', 1) LIKE "%1842%" THEN "Yes"
ELSE "Unknown"
END disable_integrity_checks,
data
FROM windows_eventlog
WHERE
channel = "Security" AND
eventid = 4826 AND
(kernel_debug LIKE "yes" OR disable_integrity_checks LIKE "yes" OR test_signing LIKE "yes");

queries.title: Boot Configuration Data Windows Event Logs

scripts.description:
This script combines detection and remediation for CVE-2026-31431 on Linux endpoints. 
It first determines if the system is vulnerable by checking the algif_aead module availability,
load state, modprobe blocking rules, and kernel patch status. If vulnerable, it automatically
applies mitigation by creating a modprobe install override and unloading the module from memory. 
If already mitigated or not vulnerable, no changes are made.
scripts.title: CVE-2026-31431 (copy.fail) Check and Mitigate

parameters.description: The full path of the microcode container file (microcode_amd_fam17h.bin). In Unix like systems it is usually located in /lib/firmware/amd-ucode/microcode_amd_fam17h.bin
parameters.name: amd_ucode_container_file_path

For REG_DWORD, value could be in hex starting with '0x' or an integer value. For REG_SZ string encoded in base64 is accepted.

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. It will attempt to submit the provided filepath to ThreatGrid. It requires your ThreatGrid API Key to work.

parameters.description: Full path of the file or folder you wish to submit to ThreatGrid
parameters.name: filePath

parameters.description: The API Key to use in order to submit the sample to ThreatGrid. This Key must match the region to which you are submitting. The API Key can be found under your account settings in the ThreatGrid Portal UI.
parameters.name: api_key

parameters.description: The region of ThreatGrid servers to submit to. By default samples are sent to our North American servers. Customers in the EU will need to specify the region as EU and ensure that they are using an API Key from the threatgrid.eu account page.
parameters.name: region

scripts.title: Submit ThreatGrid Sample

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. It will attempt to list out all disk partitions on the machine.
scripts.title: List Disk Partitions

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows only. It will attempt to list out all Windows Services or search for services which contain the provided search string. Supplying no argument will list all Windows Services.

parameters.description: A string to search for in the Windows Service name or display name fields. The default will list out all services on the system.
parameters.name: service_name

scripts.title: Enumerate Windows Services

scripts.description: This script is intended to be used for downloading installers for additional products or applications but does not expressly enforce that the files are installers.

parameters.description: Direct URL to the Windows installer you want to download.
parameters.name: url

scripts.title: Download Windows Installer

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows. It will attempt to delete an item from the autorun registry keys. This script has the ability to delete an item from multiple locations all at once.  If you just want to print the existing keys, please set "show_all_run_keys" parameter to True.

parameters.description: Autorun key name to delete (case sensitive). For example SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run has a key called "malware" and value "C:\temp\malicious.exe", then you have to provide "malware" in this parameter.
parameters.name: autorun_key_name

parameters.description: If this flag is set to True, it will show all the items in the run keys given in the description and will not delete the item from run keys.
parameters.name: show_all_run_keys

scripts.title: Delete Autorun Entry from Registry

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows. It can delete a scheduled task using command "schtasks /delete /F /TN task_name".

parameters.description: Scheduled task name. Please note that the scheduled_task_name parameter must be the full path of the task. For example, if the task name is Firefox_updater and the directory is \Mozilla then this parameter should be \Mozilla\Firefox_updater.
parameters.name: scheduled_task_name

scripts.title: Delete Scheduled Tasks

scripts.description:
This script identifies all listening network services on the
endpoint and classifies each by risk level based on port number,
binding interface, and known service signatures. It flags database
ports exposed externally, known backdoor ports, debug interfaces,
admin panels, and services binding to all interfaces when they
should be local-only. Output is a structured JSON report with
findings and actionable recommendations.
scripts.title: List Listening Services Risk

scripts.description: Generates a list of Startup Applications.
scripts.title: Windows List Startup

scripts.description: This script searches for an installed program by matching the display name. If found, executes the uninstallation silently. It is compatible with windows, linux and mac operating systems.

parameters.description: Exact name of the program to uninstall.
parameters.name: program_name

scripts.title: Uninstall a Program by Name

scripts.description:
This script is applicable to Windows. It can add mitigation for CVE-2023-36884 by adding HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Internet Explorer\\Main\\FeatureControl\\FEATURE_BLOCK_CROSS_PROTOCOL_FILE_NAVIGATION key into Windows registry with apps which will be affected by this feature. 
- Advisory link -> https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2023/07/11/storm-0978-attacks-reveal-financial-and-espionage-motives/
scripts.title: CVE-2023-36884 Mitigation

scripts.description:
This script is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It can rename a directory provided in the parameters. If the "force" paramater is set to True, then the script will attempt to rename the directory located in the protected folders like System32 and Program Files in case of Windows and /Applications and other system paths in case of MacOS and Linux. This script has essentially two functions:
- Rename directory
- Move directory

parameters.description: Full path of the directory to be renamed.
parameters.name: directory

parameters.description: Full path of the new directory. If the path is a different directory, script will move the directory to the new location with the new name.
parameters.name: new_directory

parameters.description: If True, will rename protected directories like System directories.
parameters.name: force

scripts.title: Rename/Move a Directory

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows, MacOS and Linux. It can list any existing network connections.
scripts.title: List Network Connections

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It will attempt to delete a directory provided in the parameters. If the "force" paramater is set to True, then the script will attempt to delete the directory located in the protected folders like System32 and Program Files in case of Windows and /Applications and other system paths in case of MacOS and Linux.

parameters.description: Full path of the directory to be deleted.
parameters.name: directory_path

parameters.description: If True, will delete protected directories like System directories.
parameters.name: force

scripts.title: Delete a Directory

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows, MacOS, and Linux. It will attempt to shutdown the host. The Windows Shutdown/Restart History Monitoring Query can be used to verify success.

parameters.description: Number of seconds to wait before shutting down.
parameters.name: wait_seconds

scripts.title: Force Shutdown

scripts.description: This script is applicable to Windows, Linux and MacOS. It will attempt to list a directory's content. If the "recursive" parameter is set to "True", then the script will also list sub-directories.

parameters.description: Full path of the directory to be listed.
parameters.name: directory_path

parameters.description: If True, will recurse through sub-directories.
parameters.name: recursive

scripts.title: List Directory
warnings: This script can produce a huge amount of output if recursive parameter is set to True.

scripts.description:
This script silently installs an executable (.exe) or MSI (.msi) installer on Windows systems.
It supports quiet mode and no reboot. Useful for remote software deployment.

parameters.description: Absolute path to the folder containing the installer.
parameters.name: path

parameters.description: Name of the installer file (e.g., installer.exe or package.msi).
parameters.name: filename

scripts.title: Install Windows Installer

scripts.description: It recursively scans a given directory and returns all files whose last-modified timestamp (mtime) falls within the specified lookback window (in minutes).

parameters.name: directory_path

parameters.description: Lookback window in minutes

warnings: On Linux and macOS, virtual filesystems such as /proc, /sys, /dev, /run, and /snap are automatically skipped to prevent agent timeouts.
On Windows, passing '/' will be automatically resolved to the system drive root (e.g. C:\).

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may abuse time providers to execute DLLs when the system boots. The Windows Time service (W32Time) enables time synchronization across and within domains.(Citation: Microsoft W32Time Feb 2018) W32Time time providers are responsible for retrieving time stamps from hardware/network resources and outputting these values to other network clients.(Citation: Microsoft TimeProvider) Time providers are implemented as dynamic-link libraries (DLLs) that are registered in the subkeys of HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\W32Time\TimeProviders\.(Citation: Microsoft TimeProvider) The time provider manager, directed by the service control manager, loads and starts time providers listed and enabled under this key at system startup and/or whenever parameters are changed.(Citation: Microsoft TimeProvider) Adversaries may abuse this architecture to establish persistence, specifically by registering and enabling a malicious DLL as a time provider. Administrator privileges are required for time provider registration, though execution will run in context of the Local Service account.(Citation: Github W32Time Oct 2017) *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Time Providers

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may send spearphishing messages with a malicious link to elicit sensitive information that can be used during targeting. Spearphishing for information is an attempt to trick targets into divulging information, frequently credentials or other actionable information. Spearphishing for information frequently involves social engineering techniques, such as posing as a source with a reason to collect information (ex: [Establish Accounts](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1585) or [Compromise Accounts](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1586)) and/or sending multiple, seemingly urgent messages.

All forms of spearphishing are electronically delivered social engineering targeted at a specific individual, company, or industry. In this scenario, the malicious emails contain links generally accompanied by social engineering text to coax the user to actively click or copy and paste a URL into a browser.(Citation: TrendMictro Phishing)(Citation: PCMag FakeLogin) The given website may be a clone of a legitimate site (such as an online or corporate login portal) or may closely resemble a legitimate site in appearance and have a URL containing elements from the real site. URLs may also be obfuscated by taking advantage of quirks in the URL schema, such as the acceptance of integer- or hexadecimal-based hostname formats and the automatic discarding of text before an “@” symbol: for example, `hxxp://google.com@1157586937`.(Citation: Mandiant URL Obfuscation 2023)

Adversaries may also embed “tracking pixels”, "web bugs", or "web beacons" within phishing messages to verify the receipt of an email, while also potentially profiling and tracking victim information such as IP address.(Citation: NIST Web Bug) (Citation: Ryte Wiki) These mechanisms often appear as small images (typically one pixel in size) or otherwise obfuscated objects and are typically delivered as HTML code containing a link to a remote server. (Citation: Ryte Wiki)(Citation: IAPP)

Adversaries may also be able to spoof a complete website using what is known as a "browser-in-the-browser" (BitB) attack. By generating a fake browser popup window with an HTML-based address bar that appears to contain a legitimate URL (such as an authentication portal), they may be able to prompt users to enter their credentials while bypassing typical URL verification methods.(Citation: ZScaler BitB 2020)(Citation: Mr. D0x BitB 2022)

Adversaries can use phishing kits such as `EvilProxy` and `Evilginx2` to perform adversary-in-the-middle phishing by proxying the connection between the victim and the legitimate website. On a successful login, the victim is redirected to the legitimate website, while the adversary captures their session cookie (i.e., [Steal Web Session Cookie](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1539)) in addition to their username and password. This may enable the adversary to then bypass MFA via [Web Session Cookie](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1550/004).(Citation: Proofpoint Human Factor)

Adversaries may also send a malicious link in the form of Quick Response (QR) Codes (also known as “quishing”). These links may direct a victim to a credential phishing page.(Citation: QR-campaign-energy-firm) By using a QR code, the URL may not be exposed in the email and may thus go undetected by most automated email security scans.(Citation: qr-phish-agriculture) These QR codes may be scanned by or delivered directly  to a user’s mobile device (i.e., [Phishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1660)), which may be less secure in several relevant ways.(Citation: qr-phish-agriculture) For example, mobile users may not be able to notice minor differences between genuine and credential harvesting websites due to mobile’s smaller form factor.

From the fake website, information is gathered in web forms and sent to the adversary. Adversaries may also use information from previous reconnaissance efforts (ex: [Search Open Websites/Domains](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1593) or [Search Victim-Owned Websites](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1594)) to craft persuasive and believable lures.

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Spearphishing Link

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may patch the authentication process on a domain controller to bypass the typical authentication mechanisms and enable access to accounts. 

Malware may be used to inject false credentials into the authentication process on a domain controller with the intent of creating a backdoor used to access any user’s account and/or credentials (ex: [Skeleton Key](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0007)). Skeleton key works through a patch on an enterprise domain controller authentication process (LSASS) with credentials that adversaries may use to bypass the standard authentication system. Once patched, an adversary can use the injected password to successfully authenticate as any domain user account (until the the skeleton key is erased from memory by a reboot of the domain controller). Authenticated access may enable unfettered access to hosts and/or resources within single-factor authentication environments.(Citation: Dell Skeleton)

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Domain Controller Authentication

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may remove indicators from tools if they believe their malicious tool was detected, quarantined, or otherwise curtailed. They can modify the tool by removing the indicator and using the updated version that is no longer detected by the target's defensive systems or subsequent targets that may use similar systems.

A good example of this is when malware is detected with a file signature and quarantined by anti-virus software. An adversary who can determine that the malware was quarantined because of its file signature may modify the file to explicitly avoid that signature, and then re-use the malware.

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Indicator Removal from Tools

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may acquire user credentials from third-party password managers.(Citation: ise Password Manager February 2019) Password managers are applications designed to store user credentials, normally in an encrypted database. Credentials are typically accessible after a user provides a master password that unlocks the database. After the database is unlocked, these credentials may be copied to memory. These databases can be stored as files on disk.(Citation: ise Password Manager February 2019)

Adversaries may acquire user credentials from password managers by extracting the master password and/or plain-text credentials from memory.(Citation: FoxIT Wocao December 2019)(Citation: Github KeeThief) Adversaries may extract credentials from memory via [Exploitation for Credential Access](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1212).(Citation: NVD CVE-2019-3610)
 Adversaries may also try brute forcing via [Password Guessing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1110/001) to obtain the master password of a password manager.(Citation: Cyberreason Anchor December 2019)

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Password Managers

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may grant additional permission levels to maintain persistent access to an adversary-controlled email account. For example, the Add-MailboxPermission [PowerShell](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/001) cmdlet, available in on-premises Exchange and in the cloud-based service Office 365, adds permissions to a mailbox.(Citation: Microsoft - Add-MailboxPermission)(Citation: FireEye APT35 2018)(Citation: Crowdstrike Hiding in Plain Sight 2018) In Google Workspace, delegation can be enabled via the Google Admin console and users can delegate accounts via their Gmail settings.(Citation: Gmail Delegation)(Citation: Google Ensuring Your Information is Safe) Adversaries may also assign mailbox folder permissions through individual folder permissions or roles. In Office 365 environments, adversaries may assign the Default or Anonymous user permissions or roles to the Top of Information Store (root), Inbox, or other mailbox folders. By assigning one or both user permissions to a folder, the adversary can utilize any other account in the tenant to maintain persistence to the target user’s mail folders.(Citation: Mandiant Defend UNC2452 White Paper) This may be used in persistent threat incidents as well as BEC (Business Email Compromise) incidents where an adversary can add [Additional Cloud Roles](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1098/003) to the accounts they wish to compromise. This may further enable use of additional techniques for gaining access to systems. For example, compromised business accounts are often used to send messages to other accounts in the network of the target business while creating inbox rules (ex: [Internal Spearphishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1534)), so the messages evade spam/phishing detection mechanisms.(Citation: Bienstock, D. - Defending O365 - 2019) *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Additional Email Delegate Permissions

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may abuse an integrated development environment (IDE) extension to establish persistent access to victim systems.(Citation: Mnemonic misuse visual studio) IDEs such as Visual Studio Code, IntelliJ IDEA, and Eclipse support extensions - software components that add features like code linting, auto-completion, task automation, or integration with tools like Git and Docker. A malicious extension can be installed through an extension marketplace (i.e., [Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1195/001)) or side-loaded directly into the IDE.(Citation: Abramovsky VSCode Security)(Citation: Lakshmanan Visual Studio Marketplace)   

In addition to installing malicious extensions, adversaries may also leverage benign ones. For example, adversaries may establish persistent SSH tunnels via the use of the VSCode Remote SSH extension (i.e., [IDE Tunneling](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1219/001)).  

Trust is typically established through the installation process; once installed, the malicious extension is run every time that the IDE is launched. The extension can then be used to execute arbitrary code, establish a backdoor, mine cryptocurrency, or exfiltrate data.(Citation: ExtensionTotal VSCode Extensions  2025)

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: IDE Extensions

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may abuse rundll32.exe to proxy execution of malicious code. Using rundll32.exe, vice executing directly (i.e. [Shared Modules](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1129)), may avoid triggering security tools that may not monitor execution of the rundll32.exe process because of allowlists or false positives from normal operations. Rundll32.exe is commonly associated with executing DLL payloads (ex: rundll32.exe {DLLname, DLLfunction}). Rundll32.exe can also be used to execute [Control Panel](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1218/002) Item files (.cpl) through the undocumented shell32.dll functions Control_RunDLL and Control_RunDLLAsUser. Double-clicking a .cpl file also causes rundll32.exe to execute. (Citation: Trend Micro CPL) Rundll32 can also be used to execute scripts such as JavaScript. This can be done using a syntax similar to this: rundll32.exe javascript:"\..\mshtml,RunHTMLApplication ";document.write();GetObject("script:https[:]//www[.]example[.]com/malicious.sct")" This behavior has been seen used by malware such as Poweliks. (Citation: This is Security Command Line Confusion) Adversaries may also attempt to obscure malicious code from analysis by abusing the manner in which rundll32.exe loads DLL function names. As part of Windows compatibility support for various character sets, rundll32.exe will first check for wide/Unicode then ANSI character-supported functions before loading the specified function (e.g., given the command rundll32.exe ExampleDLL.dll, ExampleFunction, rundll32.exe would first attempt to execute ExampleFunctionW, or failing that ExampleFunctionA, before loading ExampleFunction). Adversaries may therefore obscure malicious code by creating multiple identical exported function names and appending W and/or A to harmless ones.(Citation: Attackify Rundll32.exe Obscurity)(Citation: Github NoRunDll) DLL functions can also be exported and executed by an ordinal number (ex: rundll32.exe file.dll,#1). Additionally, adversaries may use [Masquerading](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1036) techniques (such as changing DLL file names, file extensions, or function names) to further conceal execution of a malicious payload.(Citation: rundll32.exe defense evasion) *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Rundll32

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may establish persistence by executing malicious content triggered by user inactivity. Screensavers are programs that execute after a configurable time of user inactivity and consist of Portable Executable (PE) files with a .scr file extension.(Citation: Wikipedia Screensaver) The Windows screensaver application scrnsave.scr is located in C:\Windows\System32\, and C:\Windows\sysWOW64\ on 64-bit Windows systems, along with screensavers included with base Windows installations. The following screensaver settings are stored in the Registry (HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\) and could be manipulated to achieve persistence: * SCRNSAVE.exe - set to malicious PE path * ScreenSaveActive - set to '1' to enable the screensaver * ScreenSaverIsSecure - set to '0' to not require a password to unlock * ScreenSaveTimeout - sets user inactivity timeout before screensaver is executed Adversaries can use screensaver settings to maintain persistence by setting the screensaver to run malware after a certain timeframe of user inactivity.(Citation: ESET Gazer Aug 2017) *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Screensaver

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may establish persistence through executing malicious commands triggered by a user’s shell. User [Unix Shell](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/004)s execute several configuration scripts at different points throughout the session based on events. For example, when a user opens a command-line interface or remotely logs in (such as via SSH) a login shell is initiated. The login shell executes scripts from the system (/etc) and the user’s home directory (~/) to configure the environment. All login shells on a system use /etc/profile when initiated. These configuration scripts run at the permission level of their directory and are often used to set environment variables, create aliases, and customize the user’s environment. When the shell exits or terminates, additional shell scripts are executed to ensure the shell exits appropriately. Adversaries may attempt to establish persistence by inserting commands into scripts automatically executed by shells. Using bash as an example, the default shell for most GNU/Linux systems, adversaries may add commands that launch malicious binaries into the /etc/profile and /etc/profile.d files.(Citation: intezer-kaiji-malware)(Citation: bencane blog bashrc) These files typically require root permissions to modify and are executed each time any shell on a system launches. For user level permissions, adversaries can insert malicious commands into ~/.bash_profile, ~/.bash_login, or ~/.profile which are sourced when a user opens a command-line interface or connects remotely.(Citation: anomali-rocke-tactics)(Citation: Linux manual bash invocation) Since the system only executes the first existing file in the listed order, adversaries have used ~/.bash_profile to ensure execution. Adversaries have also leveraged the ~/.bashrc file which is additionally executed if the connection is established remotely or an additional interactive shell is opened, such as a new tab in the command-line interface.(Citation: Tsunami)(Citation: anomali-rocke-tactics)(Citation: anomali-linux-rabbit)(Citation: Magento) Some malware targets the termination of a program to trigger execution, adversaries can use the ~/.bash_logout file to execute malicious commands at the end of a session. For macOS, the functionality of this technique is similar but may leverage zsh, the default shell for macOS 10.15+. When the Terminal.app is opened, the application launches a zsh login shell and a zsh interactive shell. The login shell configures the system environment using /etc/profile, /etc/zshenv, /etc/zprofile, and /etc/zlogin.(Citation: ScriptingOSX zsh)(Citation: PersistentJXA_leopitt)(Citation: code_persistence_zsh)(Citation: macOS MS office sandbox escape) The login shell then configures the user environment with ~/.zprofile and ~/.zlogin. The interactive shell uses the ~/.zshrc to configure the user environment. Upon exiting, /etc/zlogout and ~/.zlogout are executed. For legacy programs, macOS executes /etc/bashrc on startup. *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Unix Shell Configuration Modification

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may modify component firmware to persist on systems. Some adversaries may employ sophisticated means to compromise computer components and install malicious firmware that will execute adversary code outside of the operating system and main system firmware or BIOS. This technique may be similar to [System Firmware](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1542/001) but conducted upon other system components/devices that may not have the same capability or level of integrity checking.

Malicious component firmware could provide both a persistent level of access to systems despite potential typical failures to maintain access and hard disk re-images, as well as a way to evade host software-based defenses and integrity checks.

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Component Firmware

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may modify Group Policy Objects (GPOs) to subvert the intended discretionary access controls for a domain, usually with the intention of escalating privileges on the domain. Group policy allows for centralized management of user and computer settings in Active Directory (AD). GPOs are containers for group policy settings made up of files stored within a predictable network path `\\SYSVOL\\Policies\`.(Citation: TechNet Group Policy Basics)(Citation: ADSecurity GPO Persistence 2016) Like other objects in AD, GPOs have access controls associated with them. By default all user accounts in the domain have permission to read GPOs. It is possible to delegate GPO access control permissions, e.g. write access, to specific users or groups in the domain. Malicious GPO modifications can be used to implement many other malicious behaviors such as [Scheduled Task/Job](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1053), [Disable or Modify Tools](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1562/001), [Ingress Tool Transfer](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1105), [Create Account](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1136), [Service Execution](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1569/002), and more.(Citation: ADSecurity GPO Persistence 2016)(Citation: Wald0 Guide to GPOs)(Citation: Harmj0y Abusing GPO Permissions)(Citation: Mandiant M Trends 2016)(Citation: Microsoft Hacking Team Breach) Since GPOs can control so many user and machine settings in the AD environment, there are a great number of potential attacks that can stem from this GPO abuse.(Citation: Wald0 Guide to GPOs) For example, publicly available scripts such as New-GPOImmediateTask can be leveraged to automate the creation of a malicious [Scheduled Task/Job](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1053) by modifying GPO settings, in this case modifying <GPO_PATH>\Machine\Preferences\ScheduledTasks\ScheduledTasks.xml.(Citation: Wald0 Guide to GPOs)(Citation: Harmj0y Abusing GPO Permissions) In some cases an adversary might modify specific user rights like SeEnableDelegationPrivilege, set in <GPO_PATH>\MACHINE\Microsoft\Windows NT\SecEdit\GptTmpl.inf, to achieve a subtle AD backdoor with complete control of the domain because the user account under the adversary's control would then be able to modify GPOs.(Citation: Harmj0y SeEnableDelegationPrivilege Right) *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Group Policy Modification

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may modify file time attributes to hide new or changes to existing files. Timestomping is a technique that modifies the timestamps of a file (the modify, access, create, and change times), often to mimic files that are in the same folder. This is done, for example, on files that have been modified or created by the adversary so that they do not appear conspicuous to forensic investigators or file analysis tools.

Timestomping may be used along with file name [Masquerading](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1036) to hide malware and tools.(Citation: WindowsIR Anti-Forensic Techniques)

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Timestomp

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may buy, steal, or download software tools that can be used during targeting. Tools can be open or closed source, free or commercial. A tool can be used for malicious purposes by an adversary, but (unlike malware) were not intended to be used for those purposes (ex: [PsExec](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0029)). Tool acquisition can involve the procurement of commercial software licenses, including for red teaming tools such as [Cobalt Strike](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0154). Commercial software may be obtained through purchase, stealing licenses (or licensed copies of the software), or cracking trial versions.(Citation: Recorded Future Beacon 2019)

Adversaries may obtain tools to support their operations, including to support execution of post-compromise behaviors. In addition to freely downloading or purchasing software, adversaries may steal software and/or software licenses from third-party entities (including other adversaries).

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Tool

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of email addresses and accounts. Adversaries may try to dump Exchange address lists such as global address lists (GALs).(Citation: Microsoft Exchange Address Lists) In on-premises Exchange and Exchange Online, the Get-GlobalAddressList PowerShell cmdlet can be used to obtain email addresses and accounts from a domain using an authenticated session.(Citation: Microsoft getglobaladdresslist)(Citation: Black Hills Attacking Exchange MailSniper, 2016) In Google Workspace, the GAL is shared with Microsoft Outlook users through the Google Workspace Sync for Microsoft Outlook (GWSMO) service. Additionally, the Google Workspace Directory allows for users to get a listing of other users within the organization.(Citation: Google Workspace Global Access List) *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Email Account

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may inject malicious code into suspended and hollowed processes in order to evade process-based defenses. Process hollowing is a method of executing arbitrary code in the address space of a separate live process. Process hollowing is commonly performed by creating a process in a suspended state then unmapping/hollowing its memory, which can then be replaced with malicious code. A victim process can be created with native Windows API calls such as CreateProcess, which includes a flag to suspend the processes primary thread. At this point the process can be unmapped using APIs calls such as ZwUnmapViewOfSection or NtUnmapViewOfSection before being written to, realigned to the injected code, and resumed via VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, SetThreadContext, then ResumeThread respectively.(Citation: Leitch Hollowing)(Citation: Elastic Process Injection July 2017) This is very similar to [Thread Local Storage](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1055/005) but creates a new process rather than targeting an existing process. This behavior will likely not result in elevated privileges since the injected process was spawned from (and thus inherits the security context) of the injecting process. However, execution via process hollowing may also evade detection from security products since the execution is masked under a legitimate process. *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Process Hollowing

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may setup email forwarding rules to collect sensitive information. Adversaries may abuse email forwarding rules to monitor the activities of a victim, steal information, and further gain intelligence on the victim or the victim’s organization to use as part of further exploits or operations.(Citation: US-CERT TA18-068A 2018) Furthermore, email forwarding rules can allow adversaries to maintain persistent access to victim's emails even after compromised credentials are reset by administrators.(Citation: Pfammatter - Hidden Inbox Rules) Most email clients allow users to create inbox rules for various email functions, including forwarding to a different recipient. These rules may be created through a local email application, a web interface, or by command-line interface. Messages can be forwarded to internal or external recipients, and there are no restrictions limiting the extent of this rule. Administrators may also create forwarding rules for user accounts with the same considerations and outcomes.(Citation: Microsoft Tim McMichael Exchange Mail Forwarding 2)(Citation: Mac Forwarding Rules)

Any user or administrator within the organization (or adversary with valid credentials) can create rules to automatically forward all received messages to another recipient, forward emails to different locations based on the sender, and more. Adversaries may also hide the rule by making use of the Microsoft Messaging API (MAPI) to modify the rule properties, making it hidden and not visible from Outlook, OWA or most Exchange Administration tools.(Citation: Pfammatter - Hidden Inbox Rules)

In some environments, administrators may be able to enable email forwarding rules that operate organization-wide rather than on individual inboxes. For example, Microsoft Exchange supports transport rules that evaluate all mail an organization receives against user-specified conditions, then performs a user-specified action on mail that adheres to those conditions.(Citation: Microsoft Mail Flow Rules 2023) Adversaries that abuse such features may be able to enable forwarding on all or specific mail an organization receives. 

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Email Forwarding Rule

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may compromise third-party network devices that can be used during targeting. Network devices, such as small office/home office (SOHO) routers, may be compromised where the adversary's ultimate goal is not [Initial Access](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0001) to that environment -- instead leveraging these devices to support additional targeting.

Once an adversary has control, compromised network devices can be used to launch additional operations, such as hosting payloads for [Phishing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566) campaigns (i.e., [Link Target](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1608/005)) or enabling the required access to execute [Content Injection](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1659) operations. Adversaries may also be able to harvest reusable credentials (i.e., [Valid Accounts](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1078)) from compromised network devices.

Adversaries often target Internet-facing edge devices and related network appliances that specifically do not support robust host-based defenses.(Citation: Mandiant Fortinet Zero Day)(Citation: Wired Russia Cyberwar)

Compromised network devices may be used to support subsequent [Command and Control](https://attack.mitre.org/tactics/TA0011) activity, such as [Hide Infrastructure](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1665) through an established [Proxy](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1090) and/or [Botnet](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1584/005) network.(Citation: Justice GRU 2024)

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Network Devices

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may employ a known symmetric encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol. Symmetric encryption algorithms use the same key for plaintext encryption and ciphertext decryption. Common symmetric encryption algorithms include AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish, and RC4.

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Symmetric Cryptography

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may execute their own malicious payloads by hijacking the search order used to load DLLs. Windows systems use a common method to look for required DLLs to load into a program. (Citation: Microsoft Dynamic Link Library Search Order)(Citation: FireEye Hijacking July 2010) Hijacking DLL loads may be for the purpose of establishing persistence as well as elevating privileges and/or evading restrictions on file execution.

There are many ways an adversary can hijack DLL loads. Adversaries may plant trojan dynamic-link library files (DLLs) in a directory that will be searched before the location of a legitimate library that will be requested by a program, causing Windows to load their malicious library when it is called for by the victim program. Adversaries may also perform DLL preloading, also called binary planting attacks, (Citation: OWASP Binary Planting) by placing a malicious DLL with the same name as an ambiguously specified DLL in a location that Windows searches before the legitimate DLL. Often this location is the current working directory of the program.(Citation: FireEye fxsst June 2011) Remote DLL preloading attacks occur when a program sets its current directory to a remote location such as a Web share before loading a DLL. (Citation: Microsoft Security Advisory 2269637)

Phantom DLL hijacking is a specific type of DLL search order hijacking where adversaries target references to non-existent DLL files.(Citation: Hexacorn DLL Hijacking)(Citation: Adversaries Hijack DLLs) They may be able to load their own malicious DLL by planting it with the correct name in the location of the missing module.

Adversaries may also directly modify the search order via DLL redirection, which after being enabled (in the Registry and creation of a redirection file) may cause a program to load a different DLL.(Citation: Microsoft Dynamic-Link Library Redirection)(Citation: Microsoft Manifests)(Citation: FireEye DLL Search Order Hijacking)

If a search order-vulnerable program is configured to run at a higher privilege level, then the adversary-controlled DLL that is loaded will also be executed at the higher level. In this case, the technique could be used for privilege escalation from user to administrator or SYSTEM or from administrator to SYSTEM, depending on the program. Programs that fall victim to path hijacking may appear to behave normally because malicious DLLs may be configured to also load the legitimate DLLs they were meant to replace.

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: DLL Search Order Hijacking

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may host seemingly genuine Wi-Fi access points to deceive users into connecting to malicious networks as a way of supporting follow-on behaviors such as [Network Sniffing](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1040), [Transmitted Data Manipulation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1565/002), or [Input Capture](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1056).(Citation: Australia ‘Evil Twin’)

By using a Service Set Identifier (SSID) of a legitimate Wi-Fi network, fraudulent Wi-Fi access points may trick devices or users into connecting to malicious Wi-Fi networks.(Citation: Kaspersky evil twin)(Citation: medium evil twin)  Adversaries may provide a stronger signal strength or block access to Wi-Fi access points to coerce or entice victim devices into connecting to malicious networks.(Citation: specter ops evil twin)  A Wi-Fi Pineapple – a network security auditing and penetration testing tool – may be deployed in Evil Twin attacks for ease of use and broader range. Custom certificates may be used in an attempt to intercept HTTPS traffic. 

Similarly, adversaries may also listen for client devices sending probe requests for known or previously connected networks (Preferred Network Lists or PNLs). When a malicious access point receives a probe request, adversaries can respond with the same SSID to imitate the trusted, known network.(Citation: specter ops evil twin)  Victim devices are led to believe the responding access point is from their PNL and initiate a connection to the fraudulent network.

Upon logging into the malicious Wi-Fi access point, a user may be directed to a fake login page or captive portal webpage to capture the victim’s credentials. Once a user is logged into the fraudulent Wi-Fi network, the adversary may able to monitor network activity, manipulate data, or steal additional credentials. Locations with high concentrations of public Wi-Fi access, such as airports, coffee shops, or libraries, may be targets for adversaries to set up illegitimate Wi-Fi access points. 

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Evil Twin

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may clear artifacts associated with previously established persistence on a host system to remove evidence of their activity. This may involve various actions, such as removing services, deleting executables, [Modify Registry](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1112), [Plist File Modification](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1647), or other methods of cleanup to prevent defenders from collecting evidence of their persistent presence.(Citation: Cylance Dust Storm)

In some instances, artifacts of persistence may also be removed once an adversary’s persistence is executed in order to prevent errors with the new instance of the malware.(Citation: NCC Group Team9 June 2020)

*(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Clear Persistence

subtechniques.description: Adversaries may achieve persistence by adding a program to a startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key. Adding an entry to the "run keys" in the Registry or startup folder will cause the program referenced to be executed when a user logs in.(Citation: Microsoft Run Key) These programs will be executed under the context of the user and will have the account's associated permissions level. The following run keys are created by default on Windows systems: * HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run * HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce * HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run * HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce Run keys may exist under multiple hives.(Citation: Microsoft Wow6432Node 2018)(Citation: Malwarebytes Wow6432Node 2016) The HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnceEx is also available but is not created by default on Windows Vista and newer. Registry run key entries can reference programs directly or list them as a dependency.(Citation: Microsoft Run Key) For example, it is possible to load a DLL at logon using a "Depend" key with RunOnceEx: reg add HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnceEx\0001\Depend /v 1 /d "C:\temp\evil[.]dll" (Citation: Oddvar Moe RunOnceEx Mar 2018) Placing a program within a startup folder will also cause that program to execute when a user logs in. There is a startup folder location for individual user accounts as well as a system-wide startup folder that will be checked regardless of which user account logs in. The startup folder path for the current user is C:\Users\\[Username]\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup. The startup folder path for all users is C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\StartUp. The following Registry keys can be used to set startup folder items for persistence: * HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\User Shell Folders * HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders * HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Shell Folders * HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\User Shell Folders The following Registry keys can control automatic startup of services during boot: * HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunServicesOnce * HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunServicesOnce * HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunServices * HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunServices Using policy settings to specify startup programs creates corresponding values in either of two Registry keys: * HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\Run * HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\Run Programs listed in the load value of the registry key HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows run automatically for the currently logged-on user. By default, the multistring BootExecute value of the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager is set to autocheck autochk *. This value causes Windows, at startup, to check the file-system integrity of the hard disks if the system has been shut down abnormally. Adversaries can add other programs or processes to this registry value which will automatically launch at boot. Adversaries can use these configuration locations to execute malware, such as remote access tools, to maintain persistence through system reboots. Adversaries may also use [Masquerading](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1036) to make the Registry entries look as if they are associated with legitimate programs. *(C) 2021 The MITRE Corporation. This work is reproduced and distributed with the permission of The MITRE Corporation.*
subtechniques.title: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder

subtechniques.description:
Adversaries may carry out malicious operations using a virtual instance to avoid detection. A wide variety of virtualization technologies exist that allow for the emulation of a computer or computing environment. By running malicious code inside of a virtual instance, adversaries can hide artifacts associated with their behavior from security tools that are unable to monitor activity inside the virtual instance.
